Notes: 52353988789 This looks like a small gall, but it's actually an odd insect -- a small Gall-like Scale Insect in the family Kermesidae of the superfamily Coccoidea in the Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera. It feels hard like it has a shell. I found this one on a stem of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. It seems to match the species Allokermes essigii in these photos that I found on the web. Unlike some other scale insects, I usually find these alone or in small groups. They don't appear to do any damage to the trees. (San Marcos Pass, 12 September 2022)

File.Name: m919b_m919-010.jpg
Notes:
52370622993 Here are four interesting -- and beautiful! -- "summer galls" on Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. All are the work of different Cynipid Wasps (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera). I found them all in the woods yesterday and today. Here they are clockwise from the upper-left:

* A gall of the Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella) on a twig;
* An unpinched gall of (I guess) the Pink Bow Tie Gall Wasp (undescribed Cynipidae), on the underside of a leaf;
* A gall of the Fuzzy Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis washingtonensis) on a twig;
* A colony of galls of the Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus crystallinus) on the underside of a leaf.

The summer galls are just beginning, and I hope there will be many more. The nicely fill the gap as insects become scarce in late summer. (San Marcos Pass, 19 September 2022)
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Beaked Twig Gall Wasp; Pink Bow Tie Gall Wasp; Fuzzy Gall Wasp; Crystalline Gall Wasp
associatedTaxa: Disholcaspis plumbella; Disholcaspis washingtonensis; Andricus crystallinus

Today is the Autumnal Equinox -- happy equinox! We're starting to lose morning sunlight in the garden at home on the north-facing side of the canyon, but it's interesting that we're actually getting more sunlight under the trees on the other side of the canyon -- the sun is lower, so it lets sunlight in under the leaves. Window eaves work the same way.
CommonNames: Leafhopper Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak

Notes:
52398183519 This is a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a flower of native Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I believe this spider is genus Mecaphesa with that hairy abdomen. The spider is really tiny, less than 1/8 inch. Arachtober 1. (San Marcos Pass, 1 October 2022)

The month of Arachtober has finally arrived! The group challenge is to post spider photos every day during October, and I like to treat it like a scavenger hunt to find new spiders every day. It's autumn, and this is the time for spiders to pick off the pollinators before they're gone for good. It's their time.

File.Name: ma01b_ma01-020.jpg
Notes: 52398157029 Here are different Assassin Bugs in the Reduviidae family in the woods today. I believe the one on the left is the species Pselliopus spinicollis in the subfamily Harpactorinae on an old seed whorl of Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). The spines and striped legs are remarkable. I don't find them very often. The one on the right is the Spiny Assassin Bug (genus Sinea) on Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). My photo gives a good view of this predator's spike-like proboscis. They are gnarly-looking predators, though they are actually shy and retiring in my experience. (San Marcos Pass, 1 October 2022)
CommonNames: Black Sage; Spiny Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: ma08a_ma08-008.jpg
Notes: 52414277645 Here is a fairly large Crab Spider (Thomisidae) with a wild bee for prey in a flowering shrub of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) in the woods today. The spider is mostly hairless, which separates it from the more common Mecaphesa crab spiders in our woods. This one might a White-banded Crab Spider (Misumenoides formosipes) with a ridge between its chelicerae and anterior eyes, but the Goldenrod Crab Spider (Misumena vatia) is similar, and I may have them mixed up. I believe this unfortunate bee is family Megachilidae. These bees collect pollen under their abdomens rather than on their hind legs like most other bees. Arachtober 8. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2022)
CommonNames: wild bee; Coyote Bush; White-banded Crab Spider; Goldenrod Crab Spider

File.Name: ma09c_ma09-002.jpg
Notes: 52416722235 This is an odd cap of a Dead Man's Foot (Pisolithus arhizus aka Pisolithus tinctorius) "mushroom" growing with the native California Fuchsia (Epilobium canum aka Zauschneria californica, Onagraceae) under a large Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree in the woods today. I've also heard it called the "Dog Turd Fungus", "Dyemaker's Puffball", and "Bohemian Truffle". The genus name "Pisolithus" literally means "pea stone" -- the fungus gets its scientific name from the pea-shaped peridioles or spore capsules in this photo of a another specimen. They very slowly break down to release the spores. The species name "tinctorus" refers to its use as a fabric dye for colors ranging from gold to dark blue depending on the mordant. It's not really fleshy, but it is a fungus at an odd time of year. (San Marcos Pass, 9 October 2022)
CommonNames: Dead Man's Foot; California Fuchsia; Coast Live Oak
associatedTaxa: Epilobium canum; Zauschneria californica; Quercus agrifolia

Notes: 52423192272 Here is the another web of a Bowl and Doily Spider (Frontinella communis aka Frontinella pyramitela, Linyphiidae) in a shrub of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). File.Name: ma15a_ma15-014.jpg
Notes: 52430259366 I believe this is a Cellar Spider in the family Pholcidae in the corner of a window inside our house today. I usually find these little long-legged spiders on our windows, or walking across the ceiling, or in the sink or shower, or in my empty coffee cup in the morning just before I pour . . . pretty much everywhere inside, there must be 100s of them all told! Their legs look like they're made of capillary glass tubing. Apparently they are introduced from Central Europe - but they sure have made themselves at home here. It was drizzly all day, and so I figured I better look for my daily spider indoors. Arachtober 15a. (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2022)

The Arachtober group challenge is to post spider photos every day during October, and I like to treat it as a scavenger hunt to find new spiders every day of the month. This year I've added the condition to find all my daily spiders in the canyon at home. I've been successful this year! I've also learned a lot from other group photos, mostly that there are more spiders in the world than I find at home in October. Now to get back to photographing all the other creatures in the woods.

File.Name: mb02b_mb02-005.jpg
Notes: 52473778448 This is an odd cap of a Dead Man's Foot (Pisolithus arhizus aka Pisolithus tinctorius) "mushroom" growing with the succulents in the garden this morning. I've also heard it called the "Dog Turd Fungus", "Dyemaker's Puffball", and "Bohemian Truffle". The genus name "Pisolithus" literally means "pea stone" -- the fungus gets its scientific name from the pea-shaped peridioles or spore capsules in this photo of a another specimen. They very slowly break down to release the spores. The species name "tinctorus" refers to its use as a fabric dye for colors ranging from gold to dark blue depending on the mordant. It's not really fleshy, but it is a fungus at an odd time of year. (San Marcos Pass, 2 November 2022)

They are the principal vector of Lyme Disease on the West Coast, but apparently our lizards protect us -- see this photo for the fantastic story. This tick is small, only about 1/8 inch. It's the first one I've photographed since last January. (San Marcos Pass, 26 November 2022)

File.Name: mc01a_mc01-001.jpg
Notes: 52537180368 This is an odd cap of a Dead Man's Foot (Pisolithus arhizus aka Pisolithus tinctorius) "mushroom" growing with the succulents in the garden this morning. I've also heard it called the "Dog Turd Fungus", "Dyemaker's Puffball", and "Bohemian Truffle". The genus name "Pisolithus" literally means "pea stone" -- the fungus gets its scientific name from the pea-shaped peridioles or spore capsules. They very slowly break down to release the spores. The species name "tinctorus" refers to its use as a fabric dye for colors ranging from gold to dark blue depending on the mordant. It's not really fleshy, but it is a fungus at an odd time of year. (San Marcos Pass, 1 December 2022)

File.Name: mc01b_mc01-002.jpg
Notes: 52537098310 This are galls of the Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella) in the family Cynipidae, Hymenoptera growing on Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae) in the rain today. I don't think of these galls as plant pests -- they are more like decorations! (San Marcos Pass, 1 December 2022)
CommonNames: Beaked Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak

File.Name: mc02a_mc02-010.jpg
Notes: 52538664631 Here is of Bolete mushroom with pores instead of gills growing in leaf mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees in the dribble today. I believe this is the Oak-Loving Bolete (Boletus dryophilus aka Xerocomellus dryophilus), but there are a number of similar species. That patch of white mold on the right is the "Bolete Eater" (Hypomyces chrysospermus) fungus that quickly engulf the the fungus. Note how the stem has blue stains where I touched it. There are other mushrooms that stain blue, but this isn't one of those. These mushrooms are said to be edible but not choice. I haven't tried them, mostly because they are nearly always parasitized by the Bolete Eater which is not appetizing. Look close, there is a tiny Mycena mushroom under the large one! (San Marcos Pass, 2 December 2022)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Oak-Loving Bolete; Bolete Eater

File.Name: mc02b_mc02-005.jpg
Notes: 52538186022 This is a Horsehair Fungus (Marasmius androsaceus aka Gymnopus androsaceus aka Setulipes androsaceus) mushroom growing in the mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees in the woods today. Or maybe this one is Marasmius quercophilus aka Gymnopus quercophilus that favors oaks? (Those darn mycologists!) Whatever it is, the fungus has spread to the nearby fallen leaves which is sprouting horsehair-like rhizomorphs that will never produce mushrooms. I got this photo above our retaining wall which makes a perfect window into the floor of the woods at eye level. This is the first proper mushrooms I've found there this season, and I've been waiting for them! (San Marcos Pass, 2 December 2022)
ScientificName: Marasmius androsaceus; Gymnopus androsaceus; Setulipes androsaceus; Marasmius quercophilus; Gymnopus quercophilus

File.Name: mc03c_mc03-017.jpg
Notes: 52541213728 Here are a pair of very similar "oak apple" galls growing on very different oaks in the woods. I got both these photos today. On the left is a gall of the Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis) growing on a branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). On the right is a gall of the California Gall Wasp (Andricus quercuscalifornicus) growing on a branch of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). It's interesting that the large gall is accompanied by a smaller different gall on the same branch. Scrub Oak is a "white oak" and Coast Live Oak is a "black oak". They have different galls, which is sometimes useful for telling our many different oaks apart. Both gall wasps are in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. (San Marcos Pass, 3 December 2022)
CommonNames: Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak; California Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak
associatedTaxa: Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Quercus berberidifolia

This is probably the Southern Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax stramineus), see this notice by a local biologist. They are something that the rain brings, and they seem to disappear between storms. I don't know where they hide, and it's a mystery to me how they survive our hot dry summers. I regard these slugs as "environmental indicators" of the kind of place I want to live, and I love finding them near home. (San Marcos Pass, 5 December 2022)

File.Name: mc12c_mc09-011.jpg
Notes: 52560522008 I found these two different Ticks (Ixodidae, Arachnida) a few inches apart on dead stems of the same perennial grass plant a couple of days ago -- probably Small-flowered Melic (Melica imperfecta, Poaceae) though it hardly matters. They are female and male Western Black-legged Ticks (Ixodes pacificus). Apparently the adult male ticks do not suck blood to become engorged. The females need a blood meal to produce their eggs, but the males don't. These ticks are said to the principal vector of Lyme Disease on the West Coast, but apparently lizards protect us - see this photo! Sounds fantastic, but apparently it's true. (San Marcos Pass, 9 December 2022)

File.Name: mc14c_mc12-009.jpg
Notes: 52564072396 This is an oak leaf with galls along the edges. They are one of several different kinds of "rolled leaf gall" that we seem to have on our trees. I'm sure they are proper galls and not the work of leaf-roller insects because the cellular structure of the leaf has been changed and not just mechanically folded and tied down. I know nothing else about these galls as they are not listed in Ron Russo's "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006). I suppose I should open a few up, though I hate to disturb whatever is in there. (San Marcos Pass, 12 December 2022)

File.Name: mc17b_mc17-007.jpg
Notes: 52569377022 Here are lichens growing on dead twigs of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae). I'm not sure why this shrub is particularly attractive to lichens -- it's evergreen, but maybe the short leaves let in a bit more winter light than other native shrubs? Two of the larger lichens are distinctive. The orange lichen on the right is the Powdered Orange Bush Lichen (Teloschistes flavicans) which is uncommon in our mountains. And I believe that is Lace Lichen (Ramalina menziesii) reaching across to it. This lichen is common in the Santa Ynez Valley where it decorates the deciduous Valley Oak trees, see this photo, but I don't think I've ever seen it up here in the mountains. It's interesting to find these two unusual lichens on the same shrub. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2022)
CommonNames: Chamise; Powdered Orange Bush Lichen; Lace Lichen

There are hoards of the mushrooms starting to appear under the trees right now, and I reckon they'll last as long as the woods stay wet. (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2022)

File.Name: mc20a_mc20-026.jpg
Notes: 52576773060 This is a tiny fly (order Diptera) with a large head in the woods in the woods today. Maybe this is a Big-headed Fly in the Pipunculidae family, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2022)

File.Name: mc20b_mc20-006.jpg
Notes: 52576841688 This might be the last flower of native California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia, Asteraceae) in the woods today. It's easy to spot the first flower, but it's hard to find the last one! (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2022)

File.Name: mc20c_mc16-042.jpg
Notes: 52576304286 This is a brand new earthstar "mushroom" just coming up in the woods a few days ago. I added scare quotes because these fungi are not at all fleshy. I guess it is the Hygroscopic Earthstar (Astraeus hygrometricus). These fungi are built with an inner puffball or spore sack surrounded by an outer rind. The rind splits to make a star, and the inner sack opens to release spores. The rays of the outer rind open and close depending on the humidity. The inner puffball is ephemeral, but the outer rinds are tough and last for years. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2022)

File.Name: mc21a_mc21-008.jpg
Notes:
52577970452 This is a small Orb Web Weaver (family Araneidae) spider tending its web on native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae) that's gone to seed in the woods, see my previous photo. The spider is quite small, about 1/8 inch. I believe this is a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) with than "folium pattern" on its abdomen. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2022)

The winter solstice happens today. The days will start getting just a bit longer tomorrow, and the sun will rise just a bit higher in the sky. It's a time of new hope for the future. Happy solstice everyone!

File.Name: mc21b_mc21-011.jpg
Notes: 52578682564 This is a small Orb Web Weaver (family Araneidae) spider on its web in native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae) that's gone to seed in the woods today. The spider is quite small, about 1/8 inch, and its web is a couple of inches across. I believe this is a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) with than "folium pattern" on its abdomen, see my my photo. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2022)

File.Name: mc21c_mc16-035.jpg
Notes: 52578388831 Spiders aren't the only ones! This is webbing on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) and the caterpillar that is responsible. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2022)

File.Name: mc22a_mc22-022.jpg
Notes: 52580431051 Here is a Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) butterfly among fallen oak leaves in the woods today. This is one of a group of large butterflies that overwinter as adults and come out on warm days -- including the Mourning Cloak and California Tortoiseshell. This is the first one I've managed to get close to this year. (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2022)

File.Name: mc22b_mc22-034.jpg
Notes: 52579947822 Here is a tiny orb-weaving spider, but it's a Long-jawed Orb Weaver spider in the Tetragnathidae family instead of Araneidae. I believe it is species Metellina mimetoides by the shape of its abdomen. Please excuse the white-balance, as I got the different photos on different days, but it's the same spider. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2022)

File.Name: mc22c_mc18-014.jpg
Notes: 52580842685 Here is a tiny wasp (Hymenoptera) that I believe is an actual Gall Wasp in the family Cynipidae -- their shape is pretty distinctive. I found it on a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods a couple of days ago, see this photo. The wasp is tiny, about 1/8 inch. I have hundreds of photos of different wasp galls but few of the wasps themselves, see this photo. It's a pretty good "butt shot" too, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2022)

File.Name: mc23a_mc23-023.jpg
Notes: 52581729372 Like the swallows return to Capistrano, the Bee Flies have returned to San Marcos Pass! I found this cute little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) looking at its shadow on a fallen oak leaf today. I believe it is genus Bombylius, probably the Greater Bee Fly (Bombylius major) which is said to appear early. They appear in our woods around New Year's, but they are early this year. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2022)

File.Name: mc23b_mc22-028.jpg
Notes: 52582440494 This is a "just a fly" (Diptera) on a leaf of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the woods yesterday. I don't know what kind of fly it is. I already have my two photos for the Fly Day Friday group, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2022)

File.Name: mc23c_mc21-020.jpg
Notes: 52582430889 This is a golden Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) on a sandstone outcrop in the woods a couple of days ago. This fly might be species Syrphus opinator which is said to be common in central California, though it might be something else like Eupeodes fumipennis. Whatever it is, it's a handsome fly. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2022)

File.Name: mc24a_mc23-015.jpg
Notes: 52584363769 I was staring right at this dead-looking plant of on native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae) that's gone to seed in the woods, when I suddenly saw this dragonfly (Odonata) hanging from it like some strange fruit! It's our usual winter dragonfly, a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) in the family Libellulidae. These dragonflies stay active in the canyon all winter long, though they sit out cold and windy days like today by finding an out-of-the-way perch like this. They keep moving around, so it's always a surprise when you suddenly notice one right in front of you. It's interesting how something so colorful can be so well camouflaged. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2022)

File.Name: mc24b_mc24-030.jpg
Notes: 52584323519 This is a Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) on a fallen oak leaf today. This fly is the Eristalini tribe, possibly the genus Eristalis or Asemosyrphus polygrammus aka Lejops polygrammus -- compare this photo taken a few years ago. (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2022)

File.Name: mc24c_mc24-004.jpg
Notes: 52584314794 This is another shot of small Orb Web Weaver (family Araneidae) spider on its web in native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. I believe this is a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) with that "folium pattern" on its abdomen, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2022)
CommonNames: Orb Web Weaver; Black Sage; Labyrinth Spider

File.Name: mc25a_mc25-014.jpg
Notes:
52586234714 This is a pretty little diurnal (daytime) moth perched for a moment on a sandstone outcrop in the woods today. I'm pretty sure this is Pyrausta dapalis -- a Crambid Snout Moth family (Crambidae) of the order Lepidoptera, the first I've managed to capture this year. It has brilliant red "underwings" that make them easy to spot in the air. They are quite small, less than 1/2 inch long. I seen a few flying past this winter, but this is the first one I've managed to photograph. I regard these moths as almost butterflies. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2022)

I wish you all the best for the holidays,
Peace on earth, goodwill to all.

File.Name: mc25b_mc25-003.jpg
Notes:
52585483567 Here are blushing pink flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. Usually the new flowers are pure white, but occasional shrubs are more interesting like this. I guess its just individual variation. There's a bonus creature in this photo -- a tiny Thrips (Thysanoptera) in the the lower-left. I believe they are Predatory Thrips in the genus Aeolothrips of the family Aeolothripidae, see this photo for a whole lot of them a few years ago. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2022)

I wish you all the best for the holidays,
Peace on earth, goodwill to all.

File.Name: mc25c_mc25-055.jpg
Notes:
52586468808 Here are ripe Christmas berries on native Toyon aka California Holly (as in Hollywood), Heteromeles arbutifolia in the Rosaceae plant family. The ripe berries are edible, but they are quite astringent (unless you're a bird!) and need cooking. They are beautiful and appropriate for the holidays. They seem to be having a good year, despite the ongoing drought conditions. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2022)

I wish you all the best for the holidays,
Peace on earth, goodwill to all.

File.Name: mc26a_mc26-030.jpg
Notes: 52588162891 I found this little slug crawling along a near-vertical sandstone outcrop today. It's about an inch long or a little less. After consulting "Land Snails and Slugs of Santa Barbara County" at iNaturalist, I believe it is a Meadow Slug aka Marsh Slug (Deroceras laeve). Apparently these slugs are native but widespread around the world. I've only found it a few time times, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2022)

File.Name: mc26b_mc26-005.jpg
Notes: 52588415654 Here is another hand-sized Banana Slug in the woods today. These slugs are 6-8 inches long. This is probably the Southern Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax stramineus), see this notice by a local biologist. They are something that the rain brings, and they seem to disappear between storms. I regard these slugs as "environmental indicators" of the kind of place I want to live, and I love finding them near home. (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2022)

File.Name: mc26c_mc15-031.jpg
Notes: 52588629178 I believe this is snail poop that's caught up in a branch of Snowberry, but its anus isn't where you think it should be. The snail's anus and genitals share the same opening, hence this its making poop through its breathing hole. (Hah, it reminds me of a certain notorious South Park episode!) One web site I found puts it like this: "The odd position of these openings so far forward on the body is a result of the slugs evolutionary descent from snails. In a snail these openings must be outside the shell (nobody wants to poop in their own shell after all)." See this this photo (snail) and this photo (slug). (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2022)

File.Name: mc27a_mc26-017.jpg
Notes: 52590738925 This is a wild bee on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods yesterday. Maybe it is Halictidae? I was surprised to finds any bee this close to the solstice, maybe the attracted to the Manzanita flowers in the this photo? (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2022)

File.Name: mc27b_mc26-015.jpg
Notes: 52590304131 This is another shot of small Orb Web Weaver (family Araneidae) spider on its web in native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. I believe this is a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) with that "folium pattern" on its abdomen, see this photo and this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2022)
CommonNames: Orb Web Weaver; Black Sage; Labyrinth Spider

File.Name: mc27c_mc25-027.jpg
Notes: 52589809122 This is a Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) on along a near-vertical sandstone outcrop a couple of days ago. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2022)

File.Name: mc28a_mc28-033.jpg
Notes: 52592937980 Here are succulent plants of native Southern California Dudleya (Dudleya lanceolata, Crassulaceae) growing on a near-vertical sandstone outcrop today. (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2022)

File.Name: mc28b_mc28-021.jpg
Notes: 52592928320 Raindrops on a spider web ... what else can I say? The web was close against a sandstone outcrop. The color difference is just the reflected sunlight when I looked at the web just so. Happy Web Wednesday! (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2022)

File.Name: mc28c_mc24-001.jpg
Notes: 52592485571 This is web of a small Orb Web Weaver (family Araneidae) spider in native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae) that's gone to seed in the woods a few days ago. The spider is quite small, about 1/8 inch, and its web is a couple of inches across. I believe this is another Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae). The spider's orb web is on the right and its cobweb-like retreat on the left, and there is a "sensing line" which connects the two and allows the spider to sense the vibrations of its prey. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2022)

File.Name: mc29a_mc29-021.jpg
Notes: 52595129865 Here are new plants of native Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata) in the Portulacaceae or Montiaceae plant family - with raindrops. They are hardly recognizable at this stage because they lack the umbrella-like "perfoliate leaves" that surround the flower stems, but those will come. These plants have three differently shaped leaves - strap-like at first, then spade-like, finally umbrella-like. They grow almost like a weed in our canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2022)

File.Name: mc29b_mc25-034.jpg
Notes: 52595208508 Here is a Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) on a sandstone outcrop in the woods a couple of days ago. This is the wasp that has its abdomen on a stalk or petiole. They mostly stay at their nests in mid-winter, but it was warm enough that they were out and about. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2022)

File.Name: mc29c_mc25-024.jpg
Notes: 52594197217 This is another shot of a little Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) on along a near-vertical sandstone outcrop a couple of days ago. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2022)

File.Name: mc30a_mc30-020.jpg
Notes: 52596783416 Here is another hand-sized Banana Slug in the woods today. These slugs are 6-8 inches long. This is probably the Southern Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax stramineus), see this notice by a local biologist. They are something that the rain brings, and they seem to disappear between storms. I regard these slugs as "environmental indicators" of the kind of place I want to live, and I love finding them near home. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2022)

File.Name: mc30b_mc28-024.jpg
Notes: 52597279873 This is a "just a fly" (Diptera) on a leaf of a fallen oak a couple of days ago. I'm not sure what kind of fly this is, but it's probably a Blow Fly (Calliphoridae) with the hint of blue on it's abdomen. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2022)

File.Name: mc30c_mc25-044.jpg
Notes: 52597007929 This is a another Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) on a fallen oak leaf today. This fly is the Eristalini tribe, possibly the genus Eristalis or Asemosyrphus polygrammus aka Lejops polygrammus -- compare this photo taken a few years ago. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2022)

File.Name: mc31a_mc30-035.jpg
Notes: 52598829681 Here's lots of mushrooms in the woods from yesterday and the day before. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2022)

File.Name: mc31b_mc29-005.jpg
Notes: 52599253735 Here's lots of mushrooms in the woods from yesterday and the day before. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2022)

File.Name: mc31c_mc29-034.jpg
Notes: 52599068139 Here's lots of mushrooms in the woods from yesterday and the day before. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2022)

File.Name: l101a_l101-025.jpg
Notes:
50789473656 It's the start of the new year, but it's not quite the end of last year's flowers. I found this pretty composite flower of California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia, Asteraceae) in the woods today, and there are a few more as well that are still in bloom. These plants have been flowering at least since late August, see this photo. I really didn't think they would last until the new year because it's been so dry, but here one is. (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2021)

The new year started nice today, sunny but chilly, and not much wind. I suspect there are new flowers out in the mountains as well as the old - probably Bigberry Manzanita and Chaparral Currant, though I can't be sure after our very dry autumn - but they are not flowering in our home canyon. Since the stay-at-home order for the Covid-19 pandemic was announced last March 19, I've really stayed in the home canyon except for the occasional run for beer and other necessities. Now I'm in it for the record, and I plan to stay put until March to complete my year in the canyon. I hope the rains keep coming to make my time here more interesting!

File.Name: l101b_l101-012.jpg
Notes: 50789383956 Here is a tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) making a new web to replace the one that was knocked down by the wind and rain earlier this week. The new web looks like it has segments of fluorescent light tubes, but that's just some sort of refraction of the sunlight coming from behind. This new web is replacing the one in this photo which is only an inch or two across. The tiny spider is only a couple of millimeters across. I was thinking It was one of the tiny Labyrinth Spiders (Metepeira) that I've been finding lately, like in this photo, but it doesn't look hairy enough. Maybe it's genus Hypsosinga for its size as much as anything else? I'll have more photos for the Web Wednesdays group next week. (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2021)

File.Name: l101c_l101-023.jpg
Notes: 50789338426 This winged (alate) Termite (order Isoptera) was stuck to the seat of the folding chair I carry in the woods. The chair isn't wet or sticky, but I guess that the termite is being held down by "static cling" just like clothes stick to each other when they comes out of the drier. Maybe this is a Subterranean Termite in the family Rhinotermitidae? We had swarms of these termites a couple of days ago, see this photo, but this one seems to be late for the party. I tried to rescue it and failed. (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2021)

File.Name: l101d_l101-036.jpg
Notes: 50789303231 This is "just a fly" on an oak leaf in the woods today, maybe another Cluster Fly in the genus Pollenia of the family Polleniidae that has lost the hairy coat that is visible in this photo. It's just a fly, but it's interesting that it's the only fly I've seen all week except for the Robber Fly that's still parked in the same place, see this photo. The rain and wind and cold this week have really cut down on the insects. I was happy to spot this fly today . . . for HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2021)

File.Name: l102a_l102-043.jpg
Notes:
50793879992 This is a golden Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) perched on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. This fly might be the species Syrphus opinator which is said to be common in central California during the winter months. There's something leonine abut its pose - think Lion King. (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2021)

Today started sunny but chilly without much wind, but clouds started moving in after noon, it cooled down, and the breeze picked up. My understanding is that this is a weakening cold front moving down the coast bringing us wind but no rain. We have a better chance of some light rain on Monday.

File.Name: l102b_l102-017.jpg
Notes: 50792935428 I'm sure there's a story here, but I don't know what it is. The little dark fly on the left appears to be a male Gall Midge in the family Cecidomyiidae with those long antennae with whorls of hairs on each segment like a bottle brush. And I believe that orange thing is a female of the same kind extruding *something* from her abdomen. My first thought was that the male was feeling amorous and the female was pushing its way out of a pupal husk like the ones in this photo and this photo - but no, they clearly exit head first, and there was no gall to be seen. I'm guessing now that the female is damaged and is actually releasing her eggs early, and the male is just curious. I found them on a dead branch of native Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae) in the woods today, and I couldn't get behind the shrub for a better view. Like I said, I'm sure there's more of a story here, but I don't know what it is. (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2021)

File.Name: l102c_l102-035.jpg
Notes: 50793773342 This is a little Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider on its web in the woods today. These spiders always have that odd bump on their abdomen shaped like a bottle gourd. It's small but not tiny like the other ones I've been finding lately, like the one in this photo. In time it will decorate its web with the bodies of its prey, but so far this one only has a clean white stabilimentum to mark where its future trash line will be - a sign that it's a new web rebuilt after the wind and rain last week took out the old one. (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2021)

File.Name: l102d_l102-029.jpg
Notes: 50793722982 Here are lichens growing on dead twigs of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae). I'm not sure why this shrub is particularly attractive to lichens - it's evergreen, but maybe the short leaves let in a bit more light than other native shrubs? Two of the larger lichens are distinctive. The orange lichen on the right is the Powdered Orange Bush Lichen (Teloschistes flavicans) which is uncommon in our mountains. And I believe that is Lace Lichen (Ramalina menziesii) reaching across to it. This lichen is common in the Santa Ynez Valley where it decorates the deciduous Valley Oak trees, see this photo, but I don't think I've ever seen it up here in the mountains. It's interesting to find these two unusual lichens on the same shrub. I've shown them before in this photo from 2019, and no doubt I'll show them again. (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2021)
CommonNames: Chamise; Powdered Orange Bush Lichen; Lace Lichen

File.Name: l103a_l103-006.jpg
Notes:
50798089911 This is a Triangle Web Spider (Hyptiotes gertschi, Uloboridae) hanging from an old flower/seed whorl of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. It's anchored by one end of its line and holding the other end, see this photo and this photo. The spider is only about 900 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.6 mm - a bit more than 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) See this photo for its unusual web. They remind me of furry octopuses or cuttlefish, I can imagine them as stuffed animals! (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2021)

There were clouds for much of the day and even some drizzle this morning. There was a 30% chance of rain for Monday in the forecast yesterday, but that's gone away now. There seem to be a series of weak storms this week that all wash out before they get here. We might get wind instead, but today was not bad at least.

File.Name: l103b_l103-033.jpg
Notes: 50797288563 This is looking eye to eyes with a cute little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on an oak leaf in the woods today. It might be a female Pelegrina aeneola spider, compare this photo. I haven't seen these spiders for a couple of months - and today I photographed two of them. This one is larger than the spider in my [Previous] photo, about 4.8 mm or 3/16 inch - more than twice the size. (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2021)

File.Name: l103c_l103-023.jpg
Notes: 50797995131 Here are two shots of probably the tiniest Jumping Spider (Salticidae) that I've ever noticed on an oak leaf in the woods today. The spider is only about 465 pixels across in these 1:1 macro shots, which comes to 2.0 mm - less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) Hah, I usually figure that the spines of an oak leaf are about one millimeter long, and that checks out in the shot on the left. I only noticed this spider because it was climbing a line of webbing - rather than jumping - to get to this leaf. (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2021)

File.Name: l103d_l102-016.jpg
Notes: 50797953301 Here are two more shots of the tiny Gall Midge (Cecidomyiidae) flies that I showed yesterday in this photo. Today's photos show the male; the female is that orange blur in the background in rather reduced circumstances, see my other photo for a better shot and the story. This is a small mosquito-like fly. Two things stand out, its long antennae with whorls of hairs on each segment like a bottle brush or a pipe cleaner, and its hairy wings with much-reduced venation. I don't see these flies very often, but I know they're out there. (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2021)

File.Name: l104a_l104-022.jpg
Notes:
50801330148 Here are three more shots of a tiny Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) in the woods today. This is the same spider I've been showing lately, most recently in a different triptych in this photo. The spider seemed to be rebuilding its web today, but it reconsidered as the wind picked up and retreated to this plant of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae). The spider walked around in circles for a while before setting down as in the pose on the right. ("Why does a dog circle around three times before lying down? Because one good turn deserves another." For some reason, I still remember that joke from an old Dell comic book back in the 50s!) This spider is tiny, about 1/8 inch across. I only noticed it because I've found it in the same place before. (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2021)

Our small storm came through as expected today, with clouds but no rain and lots of wind. The wind picked up suddenly as I was getting photos of this spider and stayed the rest of the day with 30-40 mph gusts - more than was expected in the NWS forecast. I hope the rest of the forecast is right, that it will calm down in the morning tomorrow.

File.Name: l104b_l104-002.jpg
Notes: 50802032661 Here is the shadow of a fly on the other side of a leaf of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae) in the woods today. Insects are scarce right now - even flies - so I was happy to catch a glimpse of this one. (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2021)

File.Name: l104c_l103-017.jpg
Notes: 50802114797 This is the same tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) starting to make a new web to replace the one that it was working on in this photo taken a few days ago. Hah, its new web is probably knocked down again with the big winds today! When I started playing around with HTML and web pages back in the 1990s, it seemed like every other web page had a little icon of somebody digging up a road with the message "under construction". This web really is under construction. (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2021)

File.Name: l104d_l103-013.jpg
Notes: 50801969731 This is a small shrub of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae) that is finally getting some green leaves after our good rain a week ago - compare this photo taken a week ago. In time, the new leaves will become the next prickers. Our rains have been late this year, and our flora is suffering for it. I'm not seeing new leaves on all these plants, and I fear some are dead - time will tell. (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2021)

File.Name: l105a_l105-031.jpg
Notes:
50805545361 Here is a tiny Tick (Ixodoidea, Arachnida) waiting at the end of a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) in the woods today. This one is a female Western Black-legged Tick (Ixodes pacificus). They are the principal vector of Lyme Disease on the West Coast, but apparently our lizards protect us - see this photo for the fantastic story. This tick is small, only about 800 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.4 mm, about 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) It's the first one I've photographed since last April. (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2021)

There was some residual wind today, but not too bad. Some clouds appeared in the afternoon which might be another small weather trough with no rain, we'll see if the wind kicks up again tomorrow. The NWS forecast discussion calls this "about as quiet a pattern as is possible in January with very little change next several days and no rain in sight.

File.Name: l105b_l105-008.jpg
Notes: 50804754848 This is the same tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) that I showed in this photo yesterday. Here it is today making a new web after it's already-new web was destroyed by the wind last night. Hah, the same spider was also working on a new web on New Year's Day, see this photo. We've had some windy days lately to keep the spiders busy! I'll have more photos tomorrow for Web Wednesday. (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2021)

File.Name: l105c_l105-015.jpg
Notes: 50805546857 I believe this is a colony of wool-producing Woolly Oak Aphids in the genus Stegophylla of the Aphididae on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). If you look closer than I did when I got the photo, you can see one of the tiny aphids near the top-middle of my photo and what appear to be eggs in the fluff. It's curious that the "eggs" are actually larger than the aphids, and they might be something else like pupae. There is also a third form which is not visible in this photo of larger critters that look like mealybugs, see this photo for both kinds. I suspect these are the same aphids that cause the "rolled leaf galls" in this photo, but I sure don't know why they sometimes fold leaves and sometimes don't. I'll have another photo to document this soon. (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2021)
CommonNames: Woolly Oak Aphids; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: l105d_l105-002.jpg
Notes: 50805523572 This looks like the work of a terrestrial caddisfly, but I believe it is the stone nest of a Resin Bee in the family Megachilidae, probably genus Dianthidium. The whole nest is about 1/2 inch across, and the stones are tiny. It was located on a dead stem of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) and made entirely out of small stones that are glued together. The stones probably came from our nearby chipseal road. The glue that's holding it all together is visible in the cracks, and I guess its some sort of wax or resin. I don't find these interesting nests very often, and I don't know that I've seen the bee that's responsible. See this photo for another similar nest with an exit hole taken last summer. (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2021)

File.Name: l106a_l106-012.jpg
Notes:
50809067392 Beer and other necessities were getting low again, so it was time to make our usual shopping run to the Santa Ynez Valley that we do every two weeks - but I had time for a few photos before we left. I found this spider under a loose piece of oak bark in our woodpile close to home. It's a male as shown by its impressive palps. The spider must have originally been upside down under the bark, but I flipped it over when I removed it. (And sure, I replaced the bark after I got my photo.) I'm not sure what kind of spider it is. I would first guess that it's a Wolf Spider in the family Lycosidae, but the eye arrangement seems wrong. Maybe it's a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) in a winter retreat? Any help with ID will be appreciated. (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2021)

There was an ill wind blowing today . . .
I hope some good comes of it.

File.Name: l106b_l101-014.jpg
Notes: 50809031492 Here is a tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) making a new web to replace the one that was knocked down by the wind and rain last week. The new web looks like it has segments of fluorescent light tubes, but that's just some sort of refraction of the sunlight coming from behind. The finished web is only a couple of inched across, see this photo and below. The tiny spider is only a couple of millimeters across. I was thinking it was one of the tiny Labyrinth Spiders (Metepeira) that I've been finding lately, like in this photo, but it doesn't look hairy enough. Maybe it's genus Hypsosinga for its size as much as anything else? Yeah, this photo is similar to this photo and this photo, but there are subtle differences. https://www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/50728505372/

File.Name: l106c_l101-018.jpg
Notes: 50808885676 Here is a spider web under construction after the spider's old web was knocked down by our good Christmas rain storm. The tiny spider is going 'round and 'round to make the finished web that I show in my [Next] photo. It's interesting is to compare the "inner circle" of the web with the outer wrap - the inner web goes counter-clockwise while the outer web goes clockwise in the direction the spider is going. I think that all orb webs are like this, though it's sometimes hard to see - this photo shows it well. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2021)

File.Name: l106d_l101-026.jpg
Notes: 50808866806 This is the finished web that I show under construction in my [Next] two photos, taken just 20 minutes after the next one. It's the work of a tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) between dead stems in a shrub of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). The spider may even be visible on the old flower whorl on the left by the end of a sensing line, I'll make a note. (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2021)

File.Name: l107a_l107-017.jpg
Notes:
50811717978 Here is another dragonfly (Odonata) on a dead branch in the woods today. It's our usual winter dragonfly, a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) in the family Libellulidae, like the one in this photo taken a month ago. It wasn't particularly chilly this morning, but it was cloudy. These dragonflies will stay active in the canyon all winter long, though they sit out cold and/or stormy days by finding an out-of-the-way perch like this. They keep moving around, so it's a real challenge to try to find them - and always a surprise when you suddenly notice one right in front of you! (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2021)

Nice day - mostly cloudy but relatively warm in the 60s and quite calm in the morning at least. I like these cloudy days which lighten the shadows and let me get photos in places I can't usually access without flash - which I prefer not to use. There's no rain in the forecast for the next two weeks at least.

File.Name: l107b_l107-006.jpg
Notes: 50812517297 I believe this really is a Wolf Spider in the spider family Lycosidae - the spider I showed in this photo yesterday from our woodpile is definitely something else. I found this one today on a crumbly near-vertical sandstone outcrop. If you look close, you can see that its posterior lateral eyes well behind the posterior median eyes and seem to be looking straight up. The anterior eyes in front are not visible in my photo, but the result is that there are three distinct rows of eyes. That's how I recognized that yesterday's spider was something else. These spiders usually live on the ground and hunt at night, so I don't usually find them. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2021)

File.Name: l107c_l103-028.jpg
Notes: 50811644498 This is the furry rear end of the cute little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) that I showed the other end of in this photo a few days ago. The spider is perched on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I'm thinking it might be a female Pelegrina aeneola spider, compare this photo. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2021)

File.Name: l107d_l102-040.jpg
Notes: 50811624843 This is the lumpy rear end of the golden Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) that I showed the rest of in this photo a few days ago. The fly is perched on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It might be the species Syrphus opinator which is said to be common in central California during the winter months. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2021)

File.Name: l108a_l108-027.jpg
Notes:
50815991922 Here is another tiny Tick (Ixodoidea, Arachnida) waiting at the end of a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) in the woods today. It's questing - waiting with arms out for a potential host to grab on to. Hah, that could be you or me! I noticed the first one of these little devils three days ago on January 5, see this photo, and I probably found a dozen more yesterday and today. Be warned! This one is a female Western Black-legged Tick (Ixodes pacificus). They are the principal vector of Lyme Disease on the West Coast, but apparently our lizards protect us - see this photo for the fantastic story. (San Marcos Pass, 8 January 2021)

The morning was calm, but a wind picked up in the afternoon, and we're under another NWS Wind Advisory tonight. It continues to be relatively warm, about 60∞ on our upstairs deck on the shady side of the canyon and warmer in the sun. Warm and dry - it doesn't feel like winter.

File.Name: l108b_l108-024.jpg
Notes: 50815845341 This is "just a fly" on a sandstone embankment with green liverworts that are already starting to dry out and fade to black. I believe these are the Orobus-seed Liverwort (Targionia hypophylla) which has a remarkable ability to dry out and revive in a matter of hours, see this photo from last spring. I guess the fly is a Flesh Fly (Sarcophagidae), the first one I've seen for a while. It seems to be getting something from the liverworts, but I don't know what. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 8 January 2021)

File.Name: l108c_l107-043.jpg
Notes: 50815800891 Here are two shots taken less than a minute apart of a small Robber Fly (family Asilidae, Diptera) in its usual place on a faded flower of native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) in the woods yesterday. On the left, the predator has its prey that appears to be a tiny beetle. Then it dropped the prey and made like it's washing its hands - so much for that beetle! I've been finding this predator - or one just like it - in the same place for a full month now, compare this photo taken on December 6. It is active - it moves around between the flowers and sometimes fly off to hunt and then return like this. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2021)

File.Name: l108d_l107-014.jpg
Notes: 50815757176 This is looking into the head of the dragonfly (Odonata) in my [Previous] photo. It's our usual winter dragonfly, a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) in the family Libellulidae. It was sitting out a cloudy morning let me get close. Two things stand out for me. First, its head is hollow, and can freely rotate all around its neck - I wonder how that joint works? Second, look how all those eyes are a single sheet and not separate components like a circuit board - it's more like the image sensor of my digital camera, made in one take. Very interesting! (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2021)

File.Name: l109a_l109-054.jpg
Notes:
50819582267 This is looking face to face with a Sheet Web Weaver spider in the family Linyphiidae hanging from its web, probably another Hammock Spider in the genus Pityohyphantes. I show a female spider of the same kind with narrow palps in my [Previous] photo, but this one is very male with enlarged palps. It's interesting that both males and females make substantial permanent webs. This one's web is only a few feet away from the lady spider's web in the same small oak tree. I wonder if they will get together? (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2021)

The wind finally died down last night, and it was quite nice this morning, sunny and bright. *Sigh* It still doesn't feel like winter as our very dry start to the rainy season continues - despite our Christmas rain. One thing I miss are the gray winter days - they let light into the shadows that contrasty sunlight creates. Three of my photos today are from two days ago on January 7 which was cloudy. Of course I miss all the mushrooms and banana slugs too.

File.Name: l109b_l107-054.jpg
Notes: 50818713308 This is a Sheet Web Weaver spider in the family Linyphiidae, probably another Hammock Spider in the genus Pityohyphantes. That tuning fork pattern on its carapace and serrated stripe down its back are distinctive markings. This one in on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) with its web nearby. If you look close, you can see that it has narrow lady-like palps - it's female, different from the male in my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2021)

File.Name: l109c_l107-022.jpg
Notes: 50819427171 Here are two shots of a tiny orb-weaving spider in the woods, but it's a Long-jawed Orb Weaver spider in the Tetragnathidae family instead of Araneidae. Judging by the shape of its abdomen, I believe it is species Metellina mimetoides. It's tiny, just 600 pixels long in these 1:1 macro shots, which comes to 2.5 mm - about 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I've been finding a number of these spiders lately. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2021)

File.Name: l109d_l107-033.jpg
Notes: 50819361081 Here are two shots of what I'm pretty sure is a male Western Black-legged Tick (Ixodes pacificus) on a dead stem of a perennial grass plant - see my [Previous] photo for the more familiar female. It's tiny, just 575 pixels across in these 1:1 macro shots, which comes to 2.4 mm, less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figured.) The female ticks are larger and look different with a shield-like structure on their back, which has something to do with the fact that the males do not suck blood to become engorged. The females need a blood meal to produce their eggs, but the males don't - mosquitoes and deer flies are similar. This raises the question what this one is doing "questing" on the left with its arms out ready to grab onto something (or someone). It's my impression that the adult males don't feed but climb on to a host animal to find a female. I did find a mating pair of ticks near this lone male, so they don't have to do this - I'll save that photo for the Insect Hump Day group on Wednesday. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2021)

File.Name: l110a_l110-004.jpg
Notes:
50823424786 I found this tiny cricket on an old seed stalk of of non-native English Plantain (Plantago lanceolata, Plantaginaceae) in the woods today. The cricket kept moving to the shady side of the stalk, so I finally picked it and kept turning it as I got my photo - it came out better than I thought. I believe this is a juvenile Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae), the first one I've seen this year. It's tinier than the ones I usually find later in the spring - only about 1030 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 4.3 mm - about 3/16 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I'm glad these crickets are out there - with this very dry start to our rain year, it's hard to know. (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2021)

Today was sunny and dry again, with a bit of a breeze from the south in the morning. Insects are still scarce, so I was happy to find this cricket today. I've actually been seeing a few of our winter butterflies fly by. One of these days, I'll get a photo to prove it.

File.Name: l110b_l110-010.jpg
Notes: 50823346131 Here are two different "rolled leaves" in the same small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) that each have something leaking from inside. They look similar but differ in more than color. I believe the one on top is the work of Woolly Oak Aphids (Stegophylla, Aphididae). It's a proper gall in that the leaf isn't just folded over, but the cell structure is actually changed by the aphids. You can actually see one of the aphids walking across the leaf, but see my [Previous] photo for a better view. These aphids produce a white wool that is pushing out from the end on the right. The folded leaf in the bottom photo is leaking something different - insect frass or poop. Hah, the frass pellets are about the same size as the aphid above, so I reckon something else is responsible - probably a small caterpillar. The two folded leaves look remarkably similar, and I wonder if both count as galls - the cell structure of the bottom leaf looks altered as well. (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2021)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Woolly Oak Aphids

File.Name: l110c_l109-030.jpg
Notes: 50823294391 Here are two shots of tiny Woolly Oak Aphids (Stegophylla, Aphididae) on a folded leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). These aphids are responsible for the "rolled leaf galls" that I find on these oak leaves, see my [Next] photo and this photo for examples. They might also be responsible for the woolly colonies I find on the leaves, see this photo. It seems unusual to find them out in the open, but here they are. Both of these shots today are to the same scale, on the same section of the folded oak leaf. Even the big aphid is tiny, just 320 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 1.4 mm - call it 1/16 inch. (See here for how I figure.) The small one is less than half that size. See my [Next] photo for a shot of the whole folded leaf. (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2021)
CommonNames: Woolly Oak Aphids; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: l110d_l109-003.jpg
Notes: 50823230921 These photos show Erineum Galls on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). The galls appear as bumps on the top surface of the leaves and as hair-lined pits on the underside, and my photos show the top and bottom of one leaf. These are the work of Eriophyid mites in the family Eriophyidae. Presumably the mites live amongst the hairs, but this is all I know about them. (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2021)
CommonNames: Erineum Galls; Coast Live Oak; Eriophyid mites

File.Name: l111a_l111-018.jpg
Notes:
50827001482 Here is a Pacific Azure aka Echo Azure (Celastrina echo) butterfly perched for just a moment on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I've been seeing these butterflies out of the corner of my eye for some time as they fly by, but this is the first one I've managed to get close to. I believe this one is female with that wide dark area on its upper forewings. These butterflies are distinctive in having no distinctive markings below even when fresh - they are handsome though, male and female both, at least with wings open. This is the first new butterfly I've seen this year that is not overwintering like those in my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2021)

Today was sunny and dry again, with a bit of a breeze from the south in the morning. It felt a bit warmer than it's been, up to the mid-60s on our shady porch. Bah, humbug - we need rain!

File.Name: l111b_l111-022.jpg
Notes: 50826106868 Here are two shots of different butterflies in the woods today that are cropped too tight because I couldn't get any closer to them. Both are rummaging around in fallen oak leaves, probably looking for moisture. On the left is a California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica), the first one I've managed to photograph this season. On the right is a Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta), and I have found these before this year, see this photo from mid-December. Both butterflies are past of a trio of large butterflies that overwinter as adults and come out on warm days. The third member of the trio is the Mourning Cloak (Nymphalis antiopa), see this photo. I've been seeing all three butterflies in the air lately, and I'll get better photos in time. (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2021)
CommonNames: California Tortoiseshell; Red Admiral; Mourning Cloak

File.Name: l111c_l111-005.jpg
Notes: 50826799571 I found this tiny nymph of a Planthopper (Fulgoroidea) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. At least it shows the critter's rear end - true to its name, it hopped away before I could get a better shot, but compare this photo from a couple of years ago. This might be the nymph which becomes the adult in this photo which I also find in the spring - genus Danepteryx in the family Tropiduchidae. On the other hand, I think they usually have longer jet-like hairs coming out of their rear ends like this. I suspect there are more different kind of these planthoppers than I know about. This is the first one I've managed to photograph this year. (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2021)

File.Name: l111d_l106-003.jpg
Notes: 50826867117 I found this lobster-like wingless Bristletail insect in the order Microcoryphia - formerly Thysanura - under a piece of oak bark in our woodpile, and what an odd beast it is! I believe it is a Jumping Bristletail in the family Machilidae of that order. Like the Chimera of Greek mythology, it seems to be an amalgam of different parts - scales like a moth, antennae like a katydid, and mouthparts like a crane fly. See this photo for another shot taken in less contrasty light a couple of years ago. By the way, I don't like to tear rotting logs apart. It's a way to find uncommon salamanders, but it's too destructive. I figure it's important that we don't destroy the habitats we're trying to photograph. That said, I figure it's OK to dig in a woodpile - there's not much future to that habitat anyway. (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2021)

File.Name: l112a_l112-004.jpg
Notes:
50830280307 Tick affection?? This photo shows a female Western Black-legged Tick (Ixodes pacificus) above a male tick with their legs entwined on an old grass stem in the woods today. I've always thought my job as a naturalist is to make up convincing stories to explain what I find in the woods - stories that may or may not be true but sound good, though I hope they're true. My story in this case is that the ticks were doing what the ticks in this photo posted earlier today are doing, and this is a tender moment afterwards. Is that a convincing story? (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2021)

Today started out sunny and bright like too many of our days lately in what should be our rain season, but we ended up with something different as high clouds moved in - enough to lighten up the shadows anyway. I've been missing cloudy days this year. *Damn* I just read that an "exceptional atmospheric river" is streaming over Oregon right now, and we're missing out.

File.Name: l112b_l112-016.jpg
Notes: 50830230527 I found this tiny wasp (Hymenoptera) caught up in a spider web on my walk today. I first thought it was a tiny fly, but I can see four wings and wasp antennae - lookat the base of the wings on the left. The web belongs to a Triangle Web Spider (Hyptiotes gertschi, Uloboridae) - see this photo of another web of the same spider. The wasp was still struggling, and the spider was just watching from its nearby retreat. I don't know how it ended. (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2021)

File.Name: l112c_l110-025.jpg
Notes: 50829362678 This is another shot of a Sheet Web Weaver spider in the family Linyphiidae hanging from its web, probably another Hammock Spider in the genus Pityohyphantes. This spider is very male with those enlarged palps, see this photo for another photo of the same spider and this photo of different male and female spiders of the same kind together. (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2021)

File.Name: l112d_l107-032.jpg
Notes: 50830039461 Here are a mating pair of Western Black-legged Ticks (Ixodes pacificus) on an old grass stem in the woods a few days ago - something I've never seen before. That's the female on top and the smaller male underneath facing her. It sounds like they "do it" sort of like spiders - visit TickCheck for a serious anatomy lesson involving the male hypostome (like a male spider's palps) and the female genital aperture (like a lady spider's epigynum). It's almost Wednesday, so happy Insect Hump Day - even though ticks are arachnids and not insects. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2021)

File.Name: l113a_l113-004.jpg
Notes:
50832842533 Here is (most of) another dragonfly (Odonata) on a dead stem of Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae) in the woods today. It's our usual winter dragonfly, a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) in the family Libellulidae, like the one in this photo taken last week on a different plant. I'm not sure if I keep finding the same one or whether they are all different. These dragonflies stay active in the canyon all winter long, though they sit out cold and/or stormy days by finding an out-of-the-way perch like this - though it wasn't even cold today. They keep moving around, so it's always a surprise when you suddenly notice one right in front of you! (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2021)

Today was warm for a mid-winter day, in the 60s on our upstairs deck on the shady side of the canyon, and in the 70s in sunnier places. We might even be in the 80s by Friday. Yipes, we're actually under a NWS Fire Weather Watch on Friday night and Saturday when a dry wind is expected to pick up -- in January!

File.Name: l113b_l113-020.jpg
Notes: 50833628777 Here is some critter's nest in a shrub of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) in the woods today. I'm not sure who's in there, but it has a neat house of leaves held together with silk webbing. I pulled back the one leaf on the right to show the silk curtain inside. There are deformed flower buds below the nest that are probably a plant disease, see this photo for more info. I already have my two photos for the Web Wednesday group, but HWW anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2021)

File.Name: l113c_l108-013.jpg
Notes: 50833514526 This is a tiny Long-jawed Orb Weaver spider in the Tetragnathidae family rather than an Orb Web Weaver in the Araneidae family, but it spins an orb web as well. I believe the spider is species Metellina mimetoides, see this photo for another look. If you compare this photo of a different web-spinner, you can see that the inner circle of its web has a different character - more like individual cells, almost honeycomb-like. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 8 January 2021)

File.Name: l113d_l108-005.jpg
Notes: 50833487826 This spider web has an unfinished look, but maybe it's just a trick of the light reflecting from the wheel but not the spokes. The web is between dead stems in a shrub of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae), probably the work of a tiny Labyrinth Spider (Metepeira). You can see its sensing line going from the center of the web to its shelter on the right. I may even have a shot of the tiny spider in this photo. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 8 January 2021)

File.Name: l114a_l114-005.jpg
Notes:
50836896407 Here is a handsome California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly on a sandstone outcrop in the woods today. If you look close, you can see that it has its proboscis out - no doubt trying to get water from the rock. Sandstone holds a lot of water after a rain, but there's probably not much left after our Christmas storm - the liverworts on the same outcrop are starting to dry out. This butterfly is part of a group that overwinter as adults and come out on warm days like today. I rarely see them in summer. (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2021)

It was downright warm today, in the mid-70s on our shady deck and up to 80∞ in sunnier places in the mountains according to remote weather stations on the web. Tomorrow might be both warmer and windier. We're under a NWS Fire Weather Watch on Friday night, and I'll be watching.

File.Name: l114b_l114-016.jpg
Notes: 50836056973 This is another Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. These wasps have a distinctly petiolate (stalked) abdomen with distinguishes them from other paper wasps in the genus Polistes. They mostly stay at their nests on winter days, but it was warm enough today that they were out and about. I already have my two photos for the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday group - but HBBBT anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2021)
CommonNames: Western Paper Wasp; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: l114c_l113-017.jpg
Notes: 50836842027 Here is "just a fly" on an oak leaf in the woods that has just taken a dump! I was looking through my viewfinder when it happened, but my reaction time was too slow to catch it in the act - this is a few seconds later. I don't want to think too much about what that yellow goo is, but I reckon it's a fresh flyspeck. I figure this is what rear ends are for - HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2021)

File.Name: l114d_l108-029.jpg
Notes: 50835973453 This is the rear end of a female Western Black-legged Tick (Ixodes pacificus) as it was trying to get away from me under a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) in the woods last week. You can see some interesting anatomy here, especially that little round thing behind her screen-left hind leg that looks like a pop rivet or an EKG electrode. According to the TickCheck website, that's a spiracular plate that "has glands that may be involved in regulation of water loss and air diffusion." Does that mean it's some sort of in/out valve? I've posted some interesting tick photos lately, including ticks mating and being affectionate, find them here, here, here, and here. I'll wager this is a first for the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday group, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 8 January 2021)

File.Name: l115a_l115-017.jpg
Notes:
50839979576 Here are two more shots of a tiny Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) with a freshly wrapped prey in the woods today. This is the same spider I've been showing lately, most recently in this triptych. I could tell by the prey's antennae that it's some sort of true bug, but that's all I could see. This spider has a retreat off to the side of its proper orb web in this plant of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae), see this photo of the same spider by its web a month ago. It's a handsome spider. (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2021)

It was unseasonably warm today - almost 80∞ on our shady upstairs porch and surely into the 80s in sunnier places. Wind has not been an issue today. There is more good news that our weather in California might "experience a major pattern change during the last week in January, and that it will strongly favor colder and wetter conditions." There's more info at Daniel Swain's California Weather Blog.

File.Name: l115b_l114-019.jpg
Notes: 50840020097 Hah, sometimes timing is everything. I was getting photos of this golden Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) when it suddenly took a dump! If you compare this photo of a similar fly, you can see that its abdomen actually telescopes out to deliver its drop of goo. Maybe this is the species Syrphus opinator which is said to be common in central California. I just happened to post another similar photo yesterday of a different kind of fly that just did the same thing. I already have my two photos for the Fly Day Friday group, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2021)

File.Name: l115c_l113-012.jpg
Notes: 50839158203 This fly looks like it's walking in a bowl of potato chips, but it's actually in a windrow of fallen oak leaves along our road. It's very bristly, and it has an attractive color combination of bars (on its thorax) and bands (on its abdomen). I've seen similar flies before, compare this photo and this photo. On possibility is the species Vanderwulpia sequens in the family Tachinidae. There are no records for that one in California at BugGuide, though there are records for Arizona which is our neighbor. Any suggestions for ID will be appreciated. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2021)

File.Name: l115d_l112-019.jpg
Notes: 50839948667 This is just a fly kicking up its heels on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods a few days ago. I don't know or much care what kind of fly it is, but HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2021)

File.Name: l116a_l116-002.jpg
Notes:
50843477192 I found this tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) on a leaf cluster of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae) in the woods today. I never did see its whole body, but its abdomen is only about 500 pixels wide in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.1 mm, a bit less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figured.) It looks like one of the large bottlecap-size Halloween spiders like in this photo, though it's much smaller. I believe this one is Araneus bispinosus or Araneus montereyensis, probably the former because there is no particular dip between the humps on its abdomen. See Alice Abelia's comment on this photo - which probably shows that latter of the two species. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2021)

Today was maybe a degree or two cooler than yesterday but it was still unseasonably warm - about 78∞ on our shady upstairs porch and surely into the 80s in sunnier places. It was breezy this morning but calm in the afternoon. It might be a bad week coming up with Santa Ana winds, but there is still a rumor of rain next weekend. There's info at Daniel Swain's California Weather Blog.

File.Name: l116b_l116-004.jpg
Notes: 50843335386 Here are two shots of a tiny spider and its web on an old seed whorl of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. I believe this a Mesh Web Weaver in the family Dictynidae, maybe genus Dictyna itself - at least it matches the illustration for that genus in my book. The spider is tiny, about 500 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.1 mm - less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) It's also quite shy, hurrying away to hide in one of the empty seed cavities. The webbing is interesting too, often joining the seed whorls like rigging on a ship model. In the photo on the right, it looks more like a dusty maypole. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2021)

File.Name: l116c_l116-025.jpg
Notes: 50843371542 Here is another pair of mating Western Black-legged Ticks (Ixodes pacificus) on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) in the woods today. Yes, their position is interesting and has to do with their anatomy. They "do it" sort of like spiders - visit the TickCheck website for a serious anatomy lesson involving the male hypostome (like a male spider's palps) and the female genital aperture (like a lady spider's epigynum). I have another photo of mating ticks from last week. That time they stayed in one place, but today they were in constant motion, running back and forth. Now what are the odds of finding mating ticks twice in ten days?? (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2021)

File.Name: l116d_l115-032.jpg
Notes: 50842521708 Fade to black . . . Here are tiny Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) plants going dormant on a sandstone rock in the canyon. They will completely revive as soon as we get more rain, whether in six days or six months. I believe these are the Orobus-seed Liverwort (Targionia hypophylla), and they are real "resurrection plants" that will stay green as long as there is moisture. I have two more photos of this same rock taken before and after our recent Christmas rain. The liverworts green up overnight and then enter dormancy again when the sandstone finally dries out. They look like hieroglyphics. (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2021)

File.Name: l117a_l117-040.jpg
Notes:
50846578593 I found this early wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. Maybe it's a Mining Bee in the genus Andrena which are said to be active early in the season? I don't think any plants are flowering yet in our canyon. Even the early-flowering Bigberry Manzanita, which should be flowering by now, has been afflicted by a plant disease that has ruined all the flower buds at least in our canyon this year, see this photo for more info. Wild bees and caterpillars - despite the lack of rain this year, life is trying to go on. (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2021)

Our warm weather probably has something to do with this early bee being out, it was still in the mid-70s today even on our shady side of the canyon. We might have one more day of this pleasant but unseasonably dry weather, then it will cool down and the wind will pick up to make it even drier. Santa Ana winds are expected down south next week. We don't get those in the Santa Barbara mountains, but we're under both a NWS High Wind Watch and a Fire Weather Watch on Monday night through Tuesday morning. And then . . . a chance of rain next weekend!

File.Name: l117b_l117-032.jpg
Notes: 50846516918 This is the first new caterpillar I've seen this year of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on a still winter-bare stem of Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae) in the woods today. Not to worry about the lack of leaves to eat, Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Diplacus longiflorus) is right next door, and they like that one too. These are the same web-spinning caterpillars that I find in summer, for example see this photo from last summer. Apparently they form their creepy webs on the host plants in the summer, then form tough skins and drop to the forest floor for late summer and winter. They re-emerge in the following spring, get fat on their host plants, and then pupate into butterflies. It seems early to find this caterpillar, but two years ago I found the first one even earlier in December, see this photo. Adult butterflies and caterpillars were scarce last summer, and I hope they do better this year. (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2021)
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Climbing Penstemon; Heart-leaved Keckiella

File.Name: l117c_l117-008.jpg
Notes: 50846483808 Here are two shots of a little Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider on its web in the woods today. I got the close shot on the left and then backed off to show more of it's "trash line" - and wouldn't you know that the spider picked that moment to rush down its web to retrieve a tiny prey. They're small but not really tiny - this one is about 1000 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 4.2 mm - about 3/16 inch. (See here for how I figure.) These spiders decorate their webs with a "trash line" mostly made of the bodies of their prey, pirate fashion. They always have that odd bump on their abdomen shaped like a bottle gourd.
