(San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2021)

File.Name: l117d_l117-027.jpg
Notes: 50847179531 This is another of those tiny Labyrinth Spiders (Metepeira, Araneidae) that I've been showing lately, most recently in this photo. This one is in its minimalist retreat off to the side of its orb web. It's tiny, about 680 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.9 mm - a bit over 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) It's on a stem of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) that looks quite dead. (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2021)

File.Name: l118a_l118-024.jpg
Notes:
50851140166 Here is another Sheet Web Weaver spider in the family Linyphiidae hanging from its web in the woods today, probably another Hammock Spider in the genus Pityohyphantes. This spider is female with slender palps, see my last photo today for a male spider with more gnarly enlarged palps. The odd thing is that I found this female on the same web where I found the different male spider in this photo. I suspect hanky panky has been going on, see this photo from last February to see what they're up to. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2021)

It was cooler today but still pleasant, about 70∞ on our upstairs deck. There was a breeze this morning, and we're expecting big winds later tonight and tomorrow. We're under a NWS Wind Advisory tonight, followed by a High Wind Warning and Red Flag Warning tomorrow, so it might not be a good day for new photos. Now we're under consideration for a Public Safety Power Shutoff. There's still talk of rain this weekend after the wind blows by.

File.Name: l118b_l118-005.jpg
Notes: 50850363363 Here is another shot taken today of the same tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) that I found a couple of days ago, see this photo. The spider is in the same leaf cluster of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae), still tending a sensing line to its nearby orb web. I can see the whole spider in this photo - it's about 825 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.4 mm - about 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) It looks like one of the large bottlecap-size Halloween spiders like in this photo, though it's much smaller. I believe this one is Araneus bispinosus with no particular dip between the humps on its abdomen, see Alice Abelia's comment on this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2021)

File.Name: l118c_l118-021.jpg
Notes: 50850341813 This is a tiny Long-jawed Orb Weaver spider in the Tetragnathidae family rather than an Orb Web Weaver in the Araneidae family, but it spins an orb web as well. I believe it is species Metellina mimetoides from the angular shape of its abdomen, see this photo for another look. These spiders often do spin their webs at an angle, just as shown. This one has a prey that I can't identify - but it's really tiny. It was windy this morning, and this photo came out better than it should have as spider and web rocked back and forth. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2021)

File.Name: l118d_l117-026.jpg
Notes: 50851047151 This is another Sheet Web Weaver spider in the family Linyphiidae hanging from its web, probably another Hammock Spider in the genus Pityohyphantes. This spider is male with those enlarged palps, see my first photo today for a female spider with more lady-like slender palps. This one has a prey, though I can't make out what it is. (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2021)

File.Name: l119a_l119-007.jpg
Notes:
50854852827 This is the problem with being early. Here is a new caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on its preferred food plant - a still winter-bare stem of Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae) - that has old seed capsules but no new foliage yet. You can see a hint of new green leaves, but they have a ways to go. There is Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Diplacus longiflorus) right next door, and I found another caterpillar on that plant, but it still has old leaves which don't look very appetizing. See this photo from a couple of days ago for a closer shot of the caterpillar. I found three of them today, so they are definitely coming out, food or no food. (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2021)

Today was kind of a screwball weather day. I was expecting wind, but we woke up to clouds and even a few sprinkles, with calm wind. It half-cleared as the day went on, and the wind did blow with gusts to 40 mph so far - but there were always calm moments to get a few photos. It was chilly, not quite 60∞. I'm looking forward to a new day tomorrow!
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Climbing Penstemon; Heart-leaved Keckiella

File.Name: l119b_l119-019.jpg
Notes: 50853989283 Here is a tiny "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) inside a boat-like seed capsule of native Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens aka Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). I'm not sure what kind of bug it is, but it seems to have unusually long antennae - longer than its body. I suspect it's eating the seeds - a couple are visible to the screen-right of the bug's head. I've seen this one before, see this photo from last year. I've found several different kinds of insects and spiders in these old seed capsules, try a search of my photos. (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2021)
associatedTaxa: Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens; Diplacus longiflorus

File.Name: l119c_l119-016.jpg
Notes: 50854678141 I found a pair of these small Robber Flies (family Asilidae, Diptera) on old faded flowers of native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I've been finding them on the old flowers since early December, compare this photo taken on December 6. The flies are active, but it was chilly and windy today, and it must have seemed prudent to sit it out. I was lucky to get this photo with the wind today. (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2021)

File.Name: l119d_l116-022.jpg
Notes: 50853924283 Here are two more shots of mating Western Black-legged Ticks (Ixodes pacificus) on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) in the woods a few days ago, see this photo for more. The shot on the left shows a typical face-to-face position with the female walking around, but look under the leaf - there's another male waiting under there! After a while, the first male dropped off, the female climbed under the leaf, and the second male climbed aboard - not quite in the right position yet. I don't know if this is typical behavior, but it's what I saw. It's almost Wednesday, so happy Insect Hump Day - even though ticks are arachnids and not insects. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2021)

File.Name: l120a_l120-001.jpg
Notes:
50857429703 I found this small Millipede (Diplopoda, Myriapoda) curled up under under a loose piece of oak bark in our woodpile this morning. It seems to be on a nest of webbing, but that's probably just an accident. After a minute, it uncurled and hurried away on its many tiny legs. By the way, I don't approve of tearing rotting logs apart. It's a way to find uncommon salamanders, but it's too destructive. I figure it's important that we don't destroy the habitats we're trying to photograph. That said, I figure it's OK to dig around in the woodpile - there's not much future to that habitat anyway. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2021)

Beer and other necessities were getting low again, so it was time to make our usual shopping run to the Santa Ynez Valley that we do every two weeks - but I had time for a few photos before we left. When we did our shopping trip two weeks ago on January 6, insurrection was in the air - I prefer inauguration. The ill wind blew all night but finally died down this morning, and then it warmed up. It is indeed a new day!

File.Name: l120b_l119-021.jpg
Notes: 50858114731 Here is another shot of a Sheet Web Weaver spider in the family Linyphiidae hanging from its web in the woods, probably a Hammock Spider in the genus Pityohyphantes. It's no doubt the same spider I showed in this photo two days ago, a lady with slender palps. It has a little orange grub for its prey, and if you look close you can see one of its fangs. (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2021)

File.Name: l120c_l118-016.jpg
Notes: 50857363703 This is spider webbing strung between old seed whorls of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). I believe this is the work of a Mesh Web Weaver spider in the family Dictynidae, see this photo from last week that actually shows the kind of tiny spider responsible. The webbing joins the seed whorls like rigging on a dusty ship model. I have no doubt that the spider is in one of those empty seed pockets maybe looking right at me. Happy Web Wednesday! (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2021)

File.Name: l120d_l116-009.jpg
Notes: 50858135597 Here are two shots taken a few minutes apart showing a tiny Labyrinth Spider (Metepeira, Araneidae) in its retreat and most of its web in a shrub of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). The spider is tucked into that top-left seed whorl, though it's too small to see in the photo on the right. It's tiny, about 600 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.5 mm - about 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) Looking at its web, I can see its sensing line that it holds to sense vibrations - and I notice that the web is missing a section right where it passes. I've taken several photos of this same small web on different days, and it always seems to make a break there, so it seems to be something deliberate. Happy Web Wednesday! (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2021)

File.Name: l121a_l121-013.jpg
Notes:
50860795283 Here is another tiny Labyrinth Spider (Metepeira, Araneidae) in its retreat among old seed pods of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) in the woods today. It's tiny, only about 530 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.2 mm - less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) It's got an even tinier critter for prey that I can't identify. I've been finding quite a few of these spiders lately, partly because I'm learning how to follow a web back to the spider in hiding - see this photo. They always seem to arrange their retreat in the shade, so this is shot towards the sun and overexposed. (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2021)

Today was sunny with some morning breeze but no big wind. This will change tomorrow with a 50% chance of rain starting in mid-morning. The rain next week is more troubling, with a potential for snow on Monday down to 2000 feet and heavy rain on Wednesday/Thursday. Hah, be careful what you wish for! I welcome the rain in this drought year, but snow can damage our oak trees. We'll see what happens - starting tomorrow!

File.Name: l121b_l121-023.jpg
Notes: 50861457036 This is another shot of the same Sheet Web Weaver spider in the family Linyphiidae that I've been showing lately, e.g. in this photo yesterday. It's probably a Hammock Spider in the genus Pityohyphantes, that "serrated dorsal abdominal stripe" seems distinctive. This photo shows the spider's large rear end for HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2021)

File.Name: l121c_l120-013.jpg
Notes: 50861520972 This is a big black beetle trying to get away from me after I exposed its hiding place under a loose piece of oak bark in our woodpile yesterday. It might be a Darkling Beetle in the family Tenebrionidae, but it's smaller and faster than the larger Pinacate Beetles (genus Coelocnemis or Eleodes) that I sometimes find. This one's bumpy behind is just right for the Beautiful Beetle Butt Thursday group, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2021)

File.Name: l121d_l120-004.jpg
Notes: 50861373506 Here is one more photo of the same small Millipede (Diplopoda, Myriapoda) that I showed in my [Previous] photo that I found curled up under a loose piece of oak bark in our woodpile yesterday. This is after it uncurled and started to walk away from me towards the left. Millipedes have two pairs of legs per body segment, while centipedes only have a single pair. They move their legs in a wave-like motion that travels down the length of their body in a way that seems almost snake-like to me. Maybe it's a limit thing - I can imagine a series of millipedes with more and shorter legs until the legs finally disappear altogether and all that's left are the scales. Hah, is that like the Cheshire Cat that gradually disappears until nothing is left but its grin?? (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2021)

File.Name: l122a_l122-012.jpg
Notes:
50865067357 This caterpillar looks like it's going for a ride in a boat-like seed capsule of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). It's the caterpillar that will become a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly in May or June if it makes it - but not all do, see this photo of a casualty. I've found several different kinds of insects and spiders in these old seed capsules, try a search of my photos. (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2021)

It was cloudy and chilly today without much rain except for one downpour around 2:40 pm that dumped 1/10 inch of rain and hail in a few minutes. I don't think it ever got out of the 40s on our upstairs deck, and it's dropping into the 30s as I write this. We still have a 50% chance of rain tonight and tomorrow and then rain is likely on Monday and again on Wednesday/Thursday. These storms will be cold, and snow is possible. It seems that meteorologist Daniel Swain nailed it in this post at the California Weather Blog - a "a major pattern change during the last week in January" indeed!

File.Name: l122b_l122-025.jpg
Notes: 50865037607 Here is another small Robber Fly (family Asilidae, Diptera) on old faded flower of native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) in the woods today. l been finding these flies on the old flowers since early December, compare this photo taken on December 6. The flies are active, but it was chilly and windy with a few sprinkles today, and it must have seemed prudent to sit it out. I was lucky to get this photo with the wind today, and it was literally the only fly I saw. I've shown these flies several times before in almost identical poses, and why not?? HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2021)

File.Name: l122c_l121-017.jpg
Notes: 50865019217 This is "just a fly" on an oak leaf in the woods yesterday, maybe another Cluster Fly in the genus Pollenia of the family Polleniidae that has lost the hairy coat that is visible in this photo. It's just a fly, but it's remarkable how many different kinds of flies you can spot in just one place if you look close, compare the fly in my [Previous] photo which is about the same size and in the same small tree - and is clearly different. I already have my two photos for the Fly Day Friday group, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2021)

File.Name: l122d_l118-034.jpg
Notes: 50864890016 Here is another fly on an oak leaf with different colors and long bristles on its abdomen. Maybe it's a Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae, maybe not - but it certainly works for HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2021)

File.Name: l123a_l123-001.jpg
Notes:
50868596061 Here is another shot of the little caterpillar I showed yesterday in this photo - now with raindrops from our storm last night. Hah, that large drop on its head is kind of like a reverse diving helmet - a bubble of water surrounded by air! It's the caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly. With luck, it will become a handsome butterfly like this one from last June - that is, if it doesn't drown first! (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2021)

We've had less than 2/10 inch of rain from our storm so far, and we're still only at 3.9 inches for the rain-year, only 25% of normal. But even a small rain is nice to have, it smells so good outside, and we have more storms coming next that will change things. We have rain likely on Sunday night, maybe turning to snow on Monday when the snow level might drop to 1500 feet. (We're at about 2300 feet.) Hah, be careful what you wish for! I welcome any rain in this drought year, but too much snow can damage our oak trees. A bigger storm is expected later in the week.

File.Name: l123b_l123-019.jpg
Notes: 50867844738 This is a colony of tiny Woolly Oak Aphids (Stegophylla, Aphididae) on a folded leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. These aphids are coming out of one of the "rolled leaf galls" that I find on these oak leaves, see this photo and this photo for examples. The aphids seem to come in big and small versions that look different, and maybe only the larger ones make their white wool? Several of these are blowing bubbles out of their rear ends - it that's honeydew, I've never seen ants in attendance. I don't think I've ever seen so many of these aphids out in the open like this. They are an interesting part of the life of our native oaks. (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2021)
CommonNames: Woolly Oak Aphids; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: l123c_l123-017.jpg
Notes: 50868521466 Here is a little spider trying to hide from me under a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) that still has water from our rain today. I can see enough of the spider to tell that it's another Hammock Spider (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae) - a male with enlarged palps like the one in this photo, though this one's palps are a darker color. I don't see any sign of a web, so maybe he's cruising for a mate? (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2021)

File.Name: l123d_l123-008.jpg
Notes: 50868576092 Yesterday, I showed a little caterpillar in a boat-like seed capsule of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) - this photo taken today shows the seed capsules better. They are surrounded at first by the persistent dried-out flowers which are finally starting to fall away. I think of the seed capsules as little boats. They don't hold water, but they do hold lots of different insects and spiders at times, try a search of my photos to see a few of them. (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2021)

File.Name: l124a_l124-021.jpg
Notes:
50871839753 This fly (Diptera) looks like it's surfing a boat-like seed capsule of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the woods today, compare my [Previous] photo of a caterpillar doing the same thing. I'm not sure what kind of fly it is, and it hardly matters, though its interesting that its abdomen looks like it's been dipped in white paint. I've found quite a few different kinds of insects and spiders in these old seed capsules, try a search of my photos. (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2021)

There's no rain yet, but I'm sure it's coming soon. Today started sunny, but it's been windy with 30+ mph gusts all day and clouds started moving in by noon. It's been chilly, only in the 40s, and probably freezing later tonight. We're under a NWS Winter Storm Warning tonight and tomorrow for possible snow. I don't expect much rain/snow from this first storm, but our Wednesday/Thursday storm sounds more serious. Good, we really need it!

File.Name: l124b_l124-010.jpg
Notes: 50872512226 This is another caterpillar that looks like it's going for a ride in a boat-like seed capsule of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the woods today, compare this photo and this photo of probably the same caterpillar on the same seed capsule. It's the caterpillar that will become a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly in May or June if it makes it. I've found quite a few different kinds of insects and spiders in these old seed capsules, try a search of my photos. (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2021)

File.Name: l124c_l124-039.jpg
Notes: 50871771463 Here are a pair of male Western Black-legged Ticks (Ixodes pacificus) doing *something* on a dead grass stem in the woods today. The one on the left has its "arms" out like it's doing questing behavior, but that might just be a show to get me to back off. They really seemed to be communicating something, but who know what? These male ticks are smaller than the females, see this photo for a comparison. They are tiny, just 550 pixels across in these 1:1 macro shots, which comes to 2.3 mm, less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figured.) This isn't the best photo as the wind was blowing today, but it's good enough to raise the question of what are they doing. (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2021)

File.Name: l124d_l124-006.jpg
Notes: 50871731108 Here are two shots of tiny Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) plants fading to black in the woods today. I got the photo on the left at 11:00 am and the photo on the right at 12:46 pm - both taken today. They've faded away in less than two hours, but they'll revive just as fast after the next rain later tonight. In fact, they've just greened up after our small rain yesterday, but that 2/10 inch wasn't enough to soak the sandstone rock to keep them fresh. I believe these are the Orobus-seed Liverwort (Targionia hypophylla). They have a remarkable ability to revive in a matter of hours after being dried out for months - compare my photos here and here both taken on the same rock. (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2021)

File.Name: l125a_l125-019.jpg
Notes:
50875501578 I ventured out in the blustery weather today mainly to check in with the little caterpillar I've been showing lately in this seed capsule of of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) over the last few days, see my photos here, here, and here. It's the caterpillar that will become a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly in May or June. I couldn't see it at first, then I realized it's getting out of the weather by hiding under the seed capsule. That caterpillar's not so dumb! (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2021)

I did my duty and got outside today, but it was a pretty miserable - cold and windy with moments of rain and hail. (No snow, but maybe graupel?) We've had 30-50 mph wind gusts today, and it never got out of the 30s. We've still only had about 1/10 inch of rain, but our Wednesday/Thursday storm sounds more serious.

File.Name: l125b_l125-012.jpg
Notes: 50875435798 This is a lichenscape on dead twigs of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae) in the woods today. I believe the large black and gray one in the center of my photo is a Tube Lichen in the genus Hypogymnia. The brown disks are apothecia (spore-bearing structures) of the fungal part of the lichen partnership between the fungus and algae. It's nice to see the lichens revive a bit, even with the small rain we've had today. I set out to learn more about lichens every year, and every year I get distracted. (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2021)

File.Name: l125c_l125-001.jpg
Notes: 50876206797 Here are two plants of native Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea, Lamiaceae) that are looking less distressed than they were a month ago, compare this photo and this photo. This would have been the driest start of the rain year in the records for San Marcos Pass but for a good rain at the very end of December. We've had a bit of rain since then but not much, and hopefully there is a good storm coming later this week to keep the plants going. (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2021)

File.Name: l125d_l124-012.jpg
Notes: 50876073271 Here is new foliage of native Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae) coming up in oak mulch in the canyon. It's a perennial, and these leaves are coming from a root rather than a seed. With our very dry start to the rain year, annuals and perennials both have had a late start. I got this photo yesterday, but I like it better than the one I got in the wind and rain today. (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2021)

File.Name: l126a_l126-018.jpg
Notes:
50885534313 This golden Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) is perched on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. This fly might be the species Syrphus opinator which is said to be common in central California during the winter months. It was cold today, and this was the only fly that I saw. These handsome flies are usually quite camera shy, but this one landed right in front of me and started grooming. I suspect it just woke up on a cold morning and was still feeling groggy - I know how it feels. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2021)

Today was sunny and not very windy, but it was cold - still icy when I got outside in the morning and only up to the 40s later on. We're still on track for heavy rain over the next three days - maybe 3-6 inches or more. Sounds good, but all the hype makes me nervous. This is an Atmospheric River type of storm, which means that it has a narrow moisture plume - too far north or south and you miss it completely. It's cloudy now, and I hope the storm is coming.

File.Name: l126b_l126-006.jpg
Notes: 50879827022 Here are two shots of a tiny nymph of a Planthopper (Fulgoroidea) on and under a leaf of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae) in the woods today. This looks like the same kind of nymph I showed in this photo a couple of weeks ago. It's tiny, maybe 1/8 inch long or less. This might be the nymph which becomes the adult in this photo which I find in the spring - genus Danepteryx in the family Tropiduchidae. On the other hand, I think they usually have longer jet-like hairs coming out of their rear ends like this. I suspect there are more different kind of these planthoppers than I know about. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2021)

File.Name: l126c_l126-028.jpg
Notes: 50879647846 Here are of two shots of "flower clusters" of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) in the woods today. The flowers on the left have been engulfed by a plant disease that might be caused by the fungus Neofusicoccum australe, see this report on the web. I always meant to contact the author to see if it's the same thing, and I never did - maybe I will now. I have another photo of the flower buds on the right back in November, and it is thoroughly infected too - but it's flowering now anyway. Nature is resilient, though it's probably too late for the flowers on the left. These are the first flowers of this shrub - or any shrub - in our canyon this year, though I've seen the flowers elsewhere in the mountains. Hah, I didn't spot it at the time, but there's also a tiny Crab Spider on the flowers to the right - I'll make a note. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2021)

File.Name: l126d_l126-024.jpg
Notes: 50878924003 This is one more shot of the little caterpillar I've been showing lately in its seed capsule of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae), see my [Previous] photo and follow the links. Today was sunny, and the caterpillar was curled up like a kitten in a canoe. It's the caterpillar that will become a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly in May or June. Very cute! (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2021)

File.Name: l127a_l126-031.jpg
Notes:
50883143751 Here is another shot of new flowers on native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) that have managed to overcome the plant disease that is taking over many of the flower buds on this shrub, see this photo from yesterday. The blushing pink flowers are a striking contrast to the ugly deformed buds below them that look gooey but feel dry. The plant disease might be caused by the fungus Neofusicoccum australe, see this report on the web. I always meant to contact the author to see if this is the same thing, and I never did - maybe now I will. The shrub in my photo is flowering anyway, proving that nature is (sometimes) resilient. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2021)

It never stopped raining today, and I never did get outside - but It was a good day for some computer backups. We had a steady soaking rain, about 2.1 inches so far, that's doing lots of good and has caused no problems that I know of so far. The storm is still expected to drop south tomorrow bringing us some heavier rain. The NWS Forecsat Discussion says: "Southern Santa Barbara County will be hit hard with strong orthogonal flow helping to enhance rainfall rates as the front moves through that area Thursday afternoon and evening." That's us - just what we need!

File.Name: l127b_l124-027.jpg
Notes: 50883072611 I took this to be a "looper caterpillar" of a Geometrid Moth (Geometridae, Lepidoptera) when I found it, but I realize now that it has too many prolegs for that, five pairs instead of the three pairs that those caterpillars have. (True legs are towards the head, prolegs towards the tail.) It does seem to have a little hump in its middle like a real looper or inchworm, but I don't know what it is. It's heading towards a new green leaf on a stem of Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae) in the woods. This might be the first caterpillar I've seen this year other than the Checkerspot I've been showing. (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2021)
CommonNames: Geometrid Moth; Climbing Penstemon; Heart-leaved Keckiella

File.Name: l127c_l122-016.jpg
Notes: 50883159677 This is the web of a Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider in the woods a few days ago. The web is strung between dead stems of (I think) Tall Brome (Bromus grandis) grass in the Poaceae plant family. (See this photo for the grass.) I got this photo after a windy night, and I'm surprised the web held up as well as it did. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2021)

File.Name: l127d_l120-006.jpg
Notes: 50883133217 I found this nest of webbing under a loose piece of oak bark in our woodpile last week. It's hollow, like a silk purse that's been flattened. If you look in the lower-right corner, you can see the exuviae (shed skin) of a spider, but I don't know that it's the nest of this - or any other - spider. It is somebody's nest of webbing, so Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2021)

File.Name: l128a_l126-008.jpg
Notes:
50885534313 Here is another shot of a golden Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods a couple of days ago, see this photo for another shot of the same fly. If you compare both photos, you can see that this one has its tail extended - like those collapsible cups they used to sell for backpacking with nested cups that open up. See this photo to see what that's for! I believe this fly is species Syrphus opinator which is said to be common in central California. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2021)

My photos today should look familiar because they are all critters I've shown before. It rained all day again, and I never got outside for the second day in a row, so I'm using photos from earlier this week. We stand now at six inches of rain for the storm, and it's still coming down. That's actually on the low side compared with locations more to the west - 11.6 inches in the hills by Lompoc and 14.6 inches at Rocky Butte in the NW corner of San Luis Obispo county! This has been an Atmospheric River type of storm with a narrow moisture plume - too far north or south and you miss it completely. I hope it will clear out tomorrow so I can get out with my camera - I reckon the woods have changed!

File.Name: l128b_l126-004.jpg
Notes: 50885503928 This is another shot of the tiny nymph of a Planthopper (Fulgoroidea) that I found under a leaf of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae) in the woods a few days ago, see this photo for its other end. This looks like the same kind of nymph I showed in this photo a couple of weeks ago. It's tiny, just 880 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.7 mm, a bit over 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) This might be the nymph which becomes the adult in this photo which I find in the spring - genus Danepteryx in the family Tropiduchidae. I already have my two photos for the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday group - but HBBBT anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2021)

File.Name: l128c_l122-028.jpg
Notes: 50886177156 This is another Sheet Web Weaver spider in the family Linyphiidae hanging from its web in the woods, probably another Hammock Spider in the genus Pityohyphantes. This spider is male with gnarly enlarged palps, compare this photo for a female spider with more slender palps. The odd thing is that I found this male on the same web where I found that different female - and it's also the same web where I first found the male spider in this photo! Musical webs?? Maybe hanky panky has been going on, but It seems odd that I've never seen male and female together. (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2021)

File.Name: l128d_l121-005.jpg
Notes: 50886263232 Here is another Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider on its web. This spider has a handle on its abdomen - just right for HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2021)

File.Name: l129a_l129-006.jpg
Notes:
50888564338 I was looking for mushrooms in the soggy oak mulch today when I noticed this tiny cricket on a fallen leaf. Hah, I always figure that the spines on the oak leaves are about one millimeter long, which gives a sense of scale. I can also measure the cricket by counting pixels in these 1:1 macro shots - it's just 670 pixels across, which comes to 2.8 mm - less than 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) It might well be a juvenile Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae), compare this photo of the cerci (appendages) on its rear end. These crickets will become common - and larger - later in the spring. I've never seen one this small. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2021)

I think our rain is finally done, but it was still pretty soggy today with light rain and heavier showers for most of the day. We ended up with 7-3/4 inches of rain from the three-day storm, just what we needed. That's actually on the low side compared with locations more to the west - 12 inches in the hills by Lompoc and 15 inches in the NW corner of San Luis Obispo county! This storm brings our total rainfall to 11.72 inches for the season. The hell of it is that this is still just 68% of normal, that's how dry we were before this welcome storm. I tried to take a walk today but was turned back by the rain. I never did find mushrooms. Maybe tomorrow!

File.Name: l129b_l129-017.jpg
Notes: 50889365607 Here is the first new flower of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum, Grossulariaceae) that I've seen this year. I got the photo in light rain today that was just right to leave a few raindrops. Hah, the flowers always remind me of ballet dancers "en pointe" with tight ballet shoes and petticoats and skirts in the air. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2021)

File.Name: l129c_l129-002.jpg
Notes: 50889336242 This is a small fly (Diptera) on the stem of a fallen oak leaf in the soggy woods today. I believe this one is a Hybotid Dance Fly in the family Hybotidae - see Thijs de Graaf's comment to this photo. It's got a round head like a Dance Fly (Empididae) but a shorter proboscis which points backwards rather than straight down. It's tiny, just 700 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.0 mm, not quite 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) These flies are predators, and this one might be genus Platypalpus which are said to have a swollen middle femur to hold their prey while feeding. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2021)

File.Name: l129d_l129-014.jpg
Notes: 50889316862 "Oh that feels so good!" This is another shot of the golden Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) on an oak leaf that I show in my [Previous] photo (here) - scratching its back, grooming anyway. I believe this fly is species Syrphus opinator which is said to be common in central California. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2021)

File.Name: l130a_l130-007.jpg
Notes:
50893065597 This is an attractive Sheet Web Weaver spider in the family Linyphiidae hanging from its web in the woods, probably another Hammock Spider in the genus Pityohyphantes. This spider is male with enlarged palps. It was almost ten feet high in a small oak tree, and I only got the photo by extending my tripod all the way and standing on my tiptoes. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2021)

Our rain cleared out today, but there were still some clouds and a steady breeze. It was chilly this morning, though it finally made it into the 50s in the afternoon. *Sigh* I had a big smear on my lens when I got home, and I don't know when it started. I was looking for mushrooms and banana slugs, but I couldn't find any yet - nothing but arachnids today. I hope our slugs are alright - we had such a dry start to the rain year, and we have a small population of slugs to start with . . . I'll keep looking in all the usual places.

File.Name: l130b_l130-017.jpg
Notes: 50892979962 Here are two shots of a rather attractive spider in the curl of a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. It's tiny, just 500 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.1 mm, less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) In the photo on the right, you can see that it has the enlarged palps of a male. My first thought is that it's a Cobweb Weaver aka Comb-Footed Spider in the family Theridiidae - you can see rows of comb-like bristles on its long legs in the photo on the left. The small size and pretty colors may suggest genus Theridion, but I really don't know. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2021)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Cobweb Weaver; Comb-Footed Spider

File.Name: l130c_l130-014.jpg
Notes: 50892049128 Here is a tiny Tick (Ixodidae, Arachnida) waiting on the end of last year's stem of Tall Brome (Bromus grandis) grass in the Poaceae plant family. Hah - it's waiting for you or me to walk by so it can fall away and attach itself! Our most common tick is the Western Black-legged Tick (Ixodes pacificus) like in this photo, but this one looks different - for one thing, it doesn't have black legs. I believe it's the Pacific Coast Tick (Dermacentor occidentalis) - a male that will not become engorged. I don't find these ticks very often, but they are small and pretty well camouflaged. It's about 1020 pixels across, which comes to 4.3 mm - about 3/16 inch. (See here for how I figured.) An odd thing about this tick is that little "bridge" near its face like tiny eyeglasses - I don't see anything like that in this photo at BugGuide. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2021)

File.Name: l130d_l128_Goes17-28jan21-0245pm.jpg
Notes: 50891984518 This is a satellite image of our storm last week showing its incredibly long and narrow "tail" pulling tropical moisture all the way from Hawaii into California. This is the kind of storm known as an Atmospheric River, also known as a Pineapple Express. It sat over central California last week, bringing as much as 15 inches of rain to the NW corner of San Luis Obispo. We were on the edge of it at home with 7.8 inches; Ventura to the southeast of us only got an inch. That's how it is with these Atmospheric River storms that have such a narrow moisture plume - too far north or south and you miss it completely. This is a GOES 17 (GOES West) satellite image from about 2:45 pm on Thursday, 28 January 2021. The satellites produce these amazing images about every five minutes around the clock - money well spent. (GOES 17, 28 January 2021)

File.Name: l131a_l131-026.jpg
Notes:
50896810792 I found this tiny Beetle (Coleoptera) on an oak leaf in the woods today. It's small, about 780 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.3 mm, just 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) Insects are still scarce with the rain and chilly weather we've been having lately, so it was nice to find this beetle today - even if I know nothing else about it. (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2021)

Today was mostly cloudy without much breeze and nice soft light that gets into the shadows. I still haven't found any mushrooms or banana slugs, but I have to think they're coming. Maybe tomorrow.

File.Name: l131b_l131-039.jpg
Notes: 50896654946 I found this parasitic wasp (Hymenoptera) on an oak leaf in the woods today - looking right at me! I reckon it's either a Braconid or Ichneumon wasp which are both in the same superfamily Ichneumonoidea, and I figure that's good enough for ID. These are the wasps that lay their eggs on the larvae of other insects, see this photo for an example. Insects are still scarce with the rain and chilly weather we've been having lately, so it was nice to find this wasp today - even if I know nothing else about it. I like the shadows. (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2021)

File.Name: l131c_l131-037.jpg
Notes: 50896610561 This is a small Triangle Web Spider (Hyptiotes gertschi, Uloboridae) on its web in the woods today. It's got a wrapped prey in its grasp, though it's hard to see in this photo. This spider is small, about 1000 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 4.2 mm, about 3/16 inch. (See here for how I figured.) This is the first spider I've seen with a repaired web after our good rains a few days ago wiped them all out. (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2021)

File.Name: l131d_l131-045.jpg
Notes: 50896561906 Here are two shots of cute little Jumping Spiders (Salticidae) on an oak leaf in the woods today. The two shots look different, but they might be the same spider, even though it disappeared for a while between the shots. Lighting, exposure, and white balance all can change in just a few minutes - I really don't trust photos as evidence. This might be another female Pelegrina aeneola spider, compare this photo and this photo - mine had similar markings on its abdomen. (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2021)

File.Name: l201a_l201-011.jpg
Notes:
50900138456 I found this handsome Cicada (family Cicadidae in the order Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. It's the first one I've seen this year, maybe genus Platypedia. I don't think these early cicadas sing at all, they just make the clicking noise that I sometimes hear up in the oaks. I don't run into these handsome insects very often, but I do seem to find one or more in most years. (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2021)

There was a layer of high clouds this morning that cleared out in the afternoon. It was warmer today, up to 60∞ on our upstairs porch. There was a good chance of rain tomorrow in the NWS forecast a couple of days ago, but it vanished from the forecast this morning. Now it's back, with a 20% chance of a shower on Tuesday - meaning that there's an 80% chance it won't happen.

File.Name: l201b_l201-023.jpg
Notes: 50899374723 Here is a nice cluster of Cortinarius mushrooms pushing up through deep mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees on the sunny south-facing side of our mostly east-west canyon. This one might be Cortinarius regalis, see this photo of a similar mushroom. They have an almost white cap and stem, a bulbous base, and signs of rusty-colored hairs on its stem that match its rusty-colored gills. These are the first mushrooms of any kind that I've found in the woods this year, may there be many more! (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2021)

File.Name: l201c_l201-017.jpg
Notes: 50899341413 Here are two shots of a tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) in an old stem of native Yellow-flowered Hawkweed aka Southern Hawkweed (Hieracium argutum, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I found it on its small orb web, but it didn't stay there. I'm guessing that it's genus Eustala, and maybe species Eustala rosae. It has a remarkable pattern of furry hairs on its back - almost like feathers. These aren't the best photos in a breeze this morning, but it's an interesting spider that I've only found once before, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2021)
CommonNames: Orb-Weaver Spider; Yellow-flowered Hawkweed; Southern Hawkweed

File.Name: l201d_l131-015.jpg
Notes: 50900147852 This is another Sheet Web Weaver spider in the family Linyphiidae hanging from its web in a small oak tree, probably another Hammock Spider in the genus Pityohyphantes. This spider is male with enlarged palps. I seem to be finding mostly males lately, not wandering looking for mates but tending webs in one place. February is when I've most often found these spider mating, try a search of my photos to find them. (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2021)

File.Name: l202a_l202-033.jpg
Notes:
50902790883 I found this small Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) tending its web from a nearby flower cluster of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) that has been taken over by a plant disease, see this photo. I never would have noticed the brown spider on the brown "flowers" but for that white patch between the humps. It appears to be another male with enlarged palps. It looks like one of the large bottlecap-size Halloween spiders like in this photo, though it's much smaller - about 1040 pixels across in another 1:1 photo, which comes to 4.4 mm, about 3/16 inch. (See here for how I figured.) I believe this one is Araneus bispinosus or Araneus montereyensis depending on whether or not there is a strong dip between the humps on its abdomen, see Alice Abelia's comment on this photo. I still can't tell, maybe the former? (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2021)

Today is Groundhog Day, midway between solstice and equinox, and we are getting more light in our winter-shady canyon. If any ground-dwelling gopher stuck its head out today, it definitely saw its shadow - it was sunny and clear. By tradition, that means six more weeks of winter - but do you really trust your weather to rodents?? The NWS long-range outlook doesn't look so good.
associatedTaxa: Arctostaphylos glauca; Araneus bispinosus; Araneus montereyensis

File.Name: l202b_l202-028.jpg
Notes: 50903469861 The tendrils of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) don't seem to wrap around anything else quite as well as other sweetpea tendrils - making a sort of Gordian knot of tendrils. (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2021)

File.Name: l202c_l202-002.jpg
Notes: 50902733288 Here is another photo of the little caterpillar I've been showing lately curled up in a boat-like seed capsule of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the woods today. The caterpillar seems attached to this one particular seed capsule and keeps coming back to it. These caterpillars are webspinners, and this one has made some webbing connecting its "boat" to a docking leaf. It's the caterpillar that will become a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly in May or June. (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2021)

File.Name: l202d_l202-017.jpg
Notes: 50903523417 This is another Sheet Web Weaver spider in the family Linyphiidae hanging from its web in a manzanita shrub, but it's a much smaller spider than the one in my [Previous] photo. This is a Bowl and Doily Spider (Frontinella communis aka Frontinella pyramitela). It also appears to be a male with those enlarged palps, compare the female in this photo. These spiders get their common name from their distinctive webs, see this photo for an example. (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2021)

File.Name: l203a_l203-019.jpg
Notes:
50906837671 I found this transparent "worm" under a loose piece of oak bark in our woodpile this morning. Hah, you can see the whole process of digestion from head (on the right) to tail! It seemed to be following a slime trail under the bark, and what looks like webbing is actually a maze of old slime tracks made by the same critter. It was moving at a pretty good clip, even thrashing about, hard to photograph - not bad for a critter with no legs! I believe this is a transparent maggot - the larva of a fly (Diptera). One possibility for ID is the maggot of a Stiletto Fly in the family Therevidae - see the photos at BugGuide. I've actually seen something similar before, see this photo from two years ago. (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2021)

Beer and other necessities were getting low again, so it was time to make our usual shopping run to the Santa Ynez Valley that we do every two weeks - but I had time for a few photos before we left. It became too windy for photos anyway with gusts to 35 mph.

File.Name: l203b_l202-008.jpg
Notes: 50906781426 Here is another shot of the same small spider web that I show in my [Previous] photo - but from the front rather than the side. It's the web of a tiny Labyrinth Spider (Metepeira, Araneidae) in a shrub of Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae). This photo is somewhat confusing because the hills so easily become valleys, but I can see both the spider and its sensing line which allows it to sense the vibrations of its prey. The sensing line is actually pulling the web away towards the spider, the web has a shape like a face mask. This is a good web shot, but I already have my two photos for the Web Wednesday group - but Happy Web Webnesday anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2021)

File.Name: l203c_l202-006.jpg
Notes: 50906727441 Here is the web of a tiny Labyrinth Spider (Metepeira, Araneidae) in a shrub of Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae). I can actually see the spider on the first branch to the right, but it's easier to find by following the "sensing line" which allows the spider to sense the vibrations of its prey. What I find most interesting is that the sensing line is under tension, actually pulling the center of the web towards the spider. I guess it carries vibrations better that way, like an old-fashioned cup and string telephone which only works if the string is tight. I've seen this spider before, and I have a close photo of it here. I also have another shot of this web from the front in my [Next] photo. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2021)

File.Name: l203d_l131-019.jpg
Notes: 50906691816 This is another Hammock Spider (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae) hanging from its sheet web in a small oak tree. I have a closer shot of this sheet web weaver in this photo, but this one shows its web - both the sheet-like lower part and the more random upper part that supports the sheet and maybe also works to knock down flying insects. I believe this spider is male with enlarged palps, and its interesting that he has such a substantial web. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2021)

File.Name: l204a_l204-017.jpg
Notes:
50910300752 Here is a tough-looking Spiny Assassin Bug (genus Sinea, Reduviidae) on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. It's the first one of these that I've seen since last October. I believe they are active in fall and spring, with a little winter gap when they just hang out and chill - literally! They are gnarly-looking predators with that rough exterior and spike-like proboscis, though they are actually shy and retiring in my experience. (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2021)

Today was quite nice with highs in the low 60s and mostly calm wind. We're actually starting to get some sun on our upstairs deck which is nice when I get home for the day. Insects are still rather scarce, so I was happy to find this assassin today. I'm still finding more spiders than insects - which seems backwards.
CommonNames: Spiny Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: l204b_l204-007.jpg
Notes: 50910276497 Here is another shot taken today of the same tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) that I found a couple of weeks ago, see this photo. The spider is usually hunched up in a little bundle, but I guess I pestered it enough trying to get a clear shot that it loosened up - maybe trying to look bigger? The spider is on the same leaves of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae). It's about 8oo pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.4 mm - about 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) It looks like one of the large bottlecap-size Halloween spiders like in this photo, though it's much smaller. I believe this one is Araneus bispinosus with no particular dip between the humps on its abdomen, see Alice Abelia's comment on this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2021)

File.Name: l204c_l201-013.jpg
Notes: 50909426228 This is looking at the rear end of the same handsome Cicada (family Cicadidae in the order Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) that I found a few days ago on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods, see this photo. I believe this one is genus Platypedia. You can see its rear end through the window of its wings, and I'm guessing this one is male - I can't see an obvious ovipositor, and the rear end looks like this one at BugGuide which is identified as male. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2021)

File.Name: l204d_l131-024.jpg
Notes: 50909407653 This is the furry behind of the tiny Beetle (Coleoptera) that I showed in this photo a few days ago. It's on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). This one's cute behind is just right for the Beautiful Beetle Butt Thursday group, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2021)

File.Name: l205a_l205-006.jpg
Notes:
50913389806 I found this cute Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) looking at its shadow on a fallen oak leaf in the woods today. I believe it is in the genus Bombylius, probably the Greater Bee Fly (Bombylius major) which is said to appear early. I've been looking for these flies that usually first appear in our woods around New Year's, but they're a month late this year. Well, its been an odd weather year, with almost no rain at all until the end of December, and these flies have been affected. I've seen a few over the week, but this is the first one I've managed to photograph. I'm glad they're back. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2021)

Today was another nice day with highs in the mid-60s and mostly calm wind, a bit warmer than yesterday. We're actually starting to get some sun on our upstairs deck which is a pleasure when I get home for the day.

File.Name: l205b_l205-028.jpg
Notes: 50913352201 Here is a little wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) climbing up under the petals of a new flower of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum, Grossulariaceae) that have just started to appear in the canyon. I don't know what kind of bee it is, and I'll be trying to get better photos of it in the days ahead. Flowers are still scarce enough that maybe the bees don't have much choice yet about where to go! (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2021)

File.Name: l205c_l205-015.jpg
Notes: 50913319891 I found this extensive colony of tiny Woolly Oak Aphids (Stegophylla, Aphididae) under a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. This leaf was originally lying flat on the folded leaf below it, and I propped it up on another folded leaf above for my photo. The "folded leaves" also contain the aphids, and I think they are proper galls - see this photo and this photo for examples. This is probably the largest colony of these aphids I've ever found. I'll have more photos tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2021)
CommonNames: Woolly Oak Aphids; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: l205d_l202-022.jpg
Notes: 50913303941 This is an attractive fly (Diptera) looking at me from its perch on a bunch of new leaves of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the woods a few days ago. I don't know what kind of fly it is, but it looks bristly enough to be another Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae - but I know I say that too often. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2021)

File.Name: l206a_l206-018.jpg
Notes:
50916908391 This is another tough-looking Spiny Assassin Bug (genus Sinea, Reduviidae) on new flower buds of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae). It seems to be doing something with its spike-like proboscis, but I can't see what it is. Maybe it's just getting nectar? I've seen these predators visit flowers before, see this photo, so it's possible. This is the second one I've found recently after this one a couple of days ago. I guess the warm weather is bringing them out of hiding. They are gnarly-looking predators with that rough exterior and spike-like proboscis, though they are actually shy and retiring in my experience. (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2021)

Today was sunny and warm for February, up to 70∞ on our upstairs porch on the shady side of the canyon. Very nice, though it seems all wrong for February - which should be our rainiest month of the year.

File.Name: l206b_l206-016.jpg
Notes: 50917005362 Here is another tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) tending its web in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. It's about 8oo pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.4 mm - about 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) It looks like one of the large bottlecap-size Halloween spiders like in this photo, though it's much smaller. I believe this one is another Araneus bispinosus with no particular dip between the humps on its abdomen, see Alice Abelia's comment on this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2021)

File.Name: l206c_l206-004.jpg
Notes: 50916138603 The caterpillar I've been showing in the boat-like seed capsule of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) has a companion! Here are two separate caterpillars of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly in adjoining boats in the woods today. I've shown the upper one a number of times, most recently in this photo - always in the same seed capsule which is now decorated with some webbing. There's also a third one in the same shrub. Now if I could just get all three in the same boat! (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2021)

File.Name: l206d_l205-011.jpg
Notes: 50916114033 Here is a closer shot of the tiny Woolly Oak Aphids (Stegophylla, Aphididae) that I showed in this photo yesterday. They seem to be blowing bubbles, and I wonder if that has something to do with the white wool that they also make. I've never seen ants tend these aphids, so I doubt that the bubbles are honeydew. Maybe they related to the "eggs" in this photo? I did see a syrphid fly oviposit near a different colony today, so they are ordinary aphids in that regard anyway. (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2021)

File.Name: l207a_l207-047.jpg
Notes:
50920893767 I found this handsome "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. It's no doubt some kind of Stink Bug in the family Pentatomidae, but it has more interesting colors than most - dark, like it's made of bronze, with blue and red highlights. I searched all my photos, and I don't think I've ever seen one like it before. (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2021)

It was a few degrees cooler today than yesterday, but still unseasonably warm and dry. Wind was not an issue. There is a chance of rain on Friday and again on Sunday.

File.Name: l207b_l207-031.jpg
Notes: 50920836632 Here are a pair of Sheetweb Spiders (Linyphiidae) that have been mating under a cupped leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I'm sure they are the same kind of Hammock Spiders in the genus Pityohyphantes that I've been showing lately. I was disappointed by the fuzzy quality of my photos at first, but I realized I'm shooting through a veil of webbing which obscures the details - and that's entirely appropriate. That's the female on the left with slender palps, and the male on the right with swollen palps. They've been going at it and are now having a moment of closeness before going at it some more. I'll save a more graphic shot for the Insect Hump Day group on Wednesday. (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2021)

File.Name: l207c_l207-013.jpg
Notes: 50919984298 This is a Long-jawed Orb Weaver spider in the Tetragnathidae family rather than an Orb Web Weaver in the Araneidae family, though it spins an orb web as well. I believe it is species Metellina mimetoides from the angular shape of its abdomen, see this photo for another look. It's got some kind of tiny midge fly for prey that's looking right at me. (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2021)

File.Name: l207d_l207-011.jpg
Notes: 50919941813 In this photo yesterday, I showed two caterpillars in separate but adjoining boat-like seed capsules of Bush Monkeyflower at the same time. There's a third caterpillar in the same shrub, and I thought how cool it would be to show all three in the same boat. Well, here's two in the same boat! These are caterpillars of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly, and they are quite social when they are young, see this photo from last July. They are web-spinners, and you can see some webbing around the seed capsule. (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2021)

File.Name: l208a_l208-009.jpg
Notes:
50923650003 Here is a tiny nymph of a Planthopper (Fulgoroidea) on an old leaf of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. This one has a long tail of white bristles that you can't really see, but you can see its interesting face - like the grille of classic car! It's tiny, about 700 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.0 mm - less than 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) Maybe this is the nymph that becomes the adult in this photo which I find later in the spring - genus Danepteryx in the family Tropiduchidae. And maybe it's the same as the one in this photo from last month, but that one has a short tail and a different face. I suspect there are more different kind of these planthoppers than I know about. (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2021)

It was cooler today, only about 60∞, and there was a breeze from the south that felt suspiciously like the "fog winds" that comes with the marine layer. I never saw fog/clouds spill over the mountains today, but I won't be surprised if they show up overnight.

File.Name: l208b_l208-023.jpg
Notes: 50923588368 This is a small Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I reckon it's genus Mecaphesa with that hairy abdomen, and it's interesting that I haven't seen these common spiders since December - I reckon the warm weather recently has brought this one out. If you look close at the narrow part of its abdomen, you can see something that I missed when I got this photo - the spider has a passenger! I'm pretty sure that little orange cylinder that looks corrugated is the larva of a Mantidfly insect in the family Mantispidae of the order Neuroptera. I've seen these passengers on spiders before, see this photo and this photo. I'm not sure if it is actually parasitic on the spider or merely hitching a ride to the spider's nest. (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2021)
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Coast Live Oak; Mantidfly

File.Name: l208c_l206-034.jpg
Notes: 50924255756 Here is a cute little moth (Lepidoptera) hiding on a twig of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods a couple of days ago. Sorry, I have no idea what kind of moth it is, and it probably looks quite different with its wings open rather than rolled up like a tube. Any help with ID will be appreciated. Happy Moth Monday! (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2021)

File.Name: l208d_l207-019.jpg
Notes: 50924337402 The first leaves of seedling plants are the seed leaves or cotyledons - they come in pairs in my photo of these dicot plants, while other plants are monocots with just a single seed leaf. Seed leaves usually aren't very distinctive, but the first true leaves often are. I'm pretty sure my photo shows the paddle-like seed leaves and dissected true leaves of Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae). Sure, these plants will have flowers later in spring, see this photo and this photo, but it's a fun challenge to try to recognize different native plants by their first leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2021)

File.Name: l209a_l209-014.jpg
Notes:
50927914737 Here are new flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) in the woods today - with drizzle drops from the fog this morning. These particular flowers have largely avoided the plant disease that is damaging other flowers on this shrub, compare this photo. I've missed these drizzly days this winter when we've had either drought or downpour with very little in between. (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2021)

We were in a cloud this morning as marine-layer fog spread over the mountains. This was our first real fog of the year, it's more typical as May gray and June gloom later in spring. The fog finally cleared out in the afternoon and was replaced by real mid-level clouds. It was cooler than it's been, in the 50s.

File.Name: l209b_l209-001.jpg
Notes: 50927879697 These are new flowers of native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) in the Lauraceae plant family in the woods on this foggy day. The tree is related to sassafras and avocado and has similar surprisingly intricate flowers. They have a pistil in the center, surrounded by an inner-circle of three fat stamens with four anthers each, with two orange glands at the base of each, finally surrounded by an outer circle of six more fat stamens with four anthers each. The whole thing is contained within a circle of (usually) six sepals/petals. They are strange and wonderful flowers for such a common tree in our California woods. If you haven't looked close at the flowers of this tree, I recommend you do so. (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2021)

File.Name: l209c_l209-007.jpg
Notes: 50927013938 This photo shows the enlarged palps of a male Hammock Spider (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae) on a faded oak leaf in the woods today. You can see that the palps are complicated - they have a structure and are not just "feelers". Male spiders use their palps to transfer their genetic material to the receptive part of a female, see my [Previous] photo of another pair of spiders "doing it". (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2021)

File.Name: l209d_l207-030.jpg
Notes: 50927671511 Here are mating Hammock Spiders (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae) in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods a couple of days ago. That's the male on the right with large palps and the female on the left with slender palps. As I understand it, the male deposits drops of his sperm on a special "sperm web" and then inflates the end of his specialized palps to pick it up. He then transfers his genetic material to the female's epigynum. (See this photo for more info.) I think my photo shows the actual moment of consummation. I've been finding quite a few of these spiders lately, both male and female, but these are the first ones I've seen actually "doing it". It's almost Wednesday, so Happy Hump Day! (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2021)

File.Name: l210a_l210-011.jpg
Notes:
50930623333 Here are two shots of a tiny fly (Diptera) ovipositing - laying its eggs - in a new leaf bud of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the woods today. You can see the fly's reduced wing venation in the shot on the left and its surprisingly long ovipositor on the right. I believe this is a Gall Midge or Wood Midge in the family Cecidomyiidae - with long legs, hairy wings with reduced venation, and long antennae with whorls of hairs on each segment that are barely visible here. I don't know of any galls on Wild Sweetpea, so maybe this is a some kind of Wood Midge? This is a tiny creature, only about 460 pixels long in these 1:1 macro shots, which comes to 1.9 mm - less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) These shots have a vertical aspect, which is how I found this fly. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2021)

It was foggy when I woke up this morning, but it soon cleared and warmed up to about 60∞. The wind held off all morning but picked up to 35 mph in the afternoon. We actually have a 40% chance of a small rain on Thursday night.
CommonNames: Wild Sweetpea; Gall Midge; Wood Midge

File.Name: l210b_l210-027.jpg
Notes: 50930577618 It's curious that both male and female trees of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) down by the creek picked today to open their first flowers. These trees are dioecious with male and female flowers on different plants, and the ladies look quite different from the gentlemen. That's the male pollen-bearing staminate catkin on the left and the female seed-bearing pistillate catkin on the right. These catkins look wind-pollinated, but I'm sure that both male and female catkins produce nectar and are host to many insects that will hopefully soon appear. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2021)

File.Name: l210c_l207-015.jpg
Notes: 50931250701 This is a Long-jawed Orb Weaver spider in the Tetragnathidae family rather than an Orb Web Weaver in the Araneidae family, though it spins an orb web as well. I believe it is species Metellina mimetoides from the angular shape of its abdomen. See this photo for another shot of the same spider at the same time in better focus, but this photo shows the inner circle of its web. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2021)

File.Name: l210d_l207-021.jpg
Notes: 50930538493 This is the webbing of a Sheetweb Spider (Linyphiidae) in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I can't see the spider, but I have no doubt it's another Hammock Spider in the genus Pityohyphantes. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2021)
CommonNames: Sheetweb Spider; Coast Live Oak; Hammock Spider

File.Name: l211a_l211-011.jpg
Notes:
50934512697 Here are two shots of a little Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider on its web this morning. The spider was looking sleepy like on the right, but it suddenly turned around to inspect its hoard of dead insect parts on its trash line - it was moving fast, but I got the photo. This spider has a handle on its butt. I already have my two photos for the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday group - but HBBBT anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2021)

Today started sunny, but it turned increasingly cold and cloudy during the afternoon, and we have a 40% chance of light rain later tonight. Wouldn't you know, we also have another of those @#&$ maintenance power outages scheduled for tomorrow from 8:00 am to 8:00 pm. I probably won't be able to post if they actually keep to that schedule.

File.Name: l211b_l211-004.jpg
Notes: 50933653668 Here are two shots of another Cortinarius mushroom pushing up through deep mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees in the woods today. This one has orange colors, different from the pallid one I showed in this photo last week. Like all Cortinarius mushrooms, this one has rusty-colored spores which stain everything - especially the shaggy hairs on its stem that originally form a veil to protect the gills. Maybe this one is Cortinarius xanthodryophilus - see the species page and photos at Mykoweb. I've called this one Cortinarius fulmineus in the past, but I reckon it's the same thing. The caps are viscid with wet weather, but this one was dry today. Edibility is unknown. The problem is that all Cortinarius mushrooms end up looking alike, so there's no good way to tell them apart - but they are handsome mushrooms when fresh. This is still only the second mushroom I've found this year, and it's interesting that it came up just about when they usually do, compare this photo from February 1 in another year with more rain. (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2021)

File.Name: l211c_l207-052.jpg
Notes: 50933631733 This is the rear end of the same handsome "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) that I showed in this photo a few days ago. It's crawling away from me on a twig of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I believe it's some kind of Stink Bug in the family Pentatomidae, but I don't think I've ever seen one like it before. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2021)

File.Name: l211d_l207-007.jpg
Notes: 50934420537 This is a fly (Diptera) with interesting colors and a bristly rear end, probably the same kind as I showed in this photo from last month. It's resting on a seed capsule of Bush Monkeyflower. Maybe it's a Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae, maybe not - but it certainly works for HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2021)

File.Name: l212a_l212-026.jpg
Notes:
50936896798 This is "just a fly" working a flower of non-native succulent Jade Plant (Crassula ovata, Crassulaceae) in the garden today. It's interesting that I've seen this fly (or one just like it) on the flowers every day this week. I reckon it's some sort of House Fly in the family Muscidae, and it's nice to see it taking an interest in our garden rather than our trash. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2021)

We had another of those @#&$ maintenance power outages today that was scheduled from 8:00 am to 8:00 pm - and this one was actually done by 5:00. Thanks. (Hah, the cynic in me says that they said 8:00 pm so people would feel better when they were done earlier - who knows??) It was a pretty crummy day for new photos anyway, cold and cloudy in the morning and then windy with 30-40 mpg gusts. The wind might continue for a few days.

File.Name: l212b_l212-010.jpg
Notes: 50937676407 Here is one more shot of a small Robber Fly (family Asilidae, Diptera) on old faded flower of native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) in the woods today. l've been finding these flies on the old flowers since early December, compare this photo taken on December 6 - two months ago! - and here it is again today. The flies are active, but it was chilly and windy with a few sprinkles today, and this fly was sitting it out. I already have my two photos for the Fly Day Friday group, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2021)

File.Name: l212c_l210-019.jpg
Notes: 50937541491 Here is a tiny fly (Diptera) ovipositing - laying its eggs - in a new leaf bud of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the woods a couple of days ago. I believe it is a Gall Midge or Wood Midge in the family Cecidomyiidae - with long legs, hairy wings with reduced venation, and long antennae with whorls of hairs on each segment that are barely visible here. This is a tiny creature, only about 460 pixels long in these 1:1 macro shots, which comes to 1.9 mm - less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I rotated this image 90∞ to try a horizontal aspect, but I have two more shots in a vertical aspect like I found it in this photo. This one is probably cropped too tight, but it shows the fly. I already have my two photos for the Fly Day Friday group, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2021)
CommonNames: Wild Sweetpea; Gall Midge; Wood Midge

File.Name: l212d_l207-022.jpg
Notes: 50937531676 This is another golden Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods a couple of days ago. I believe this fly is species Syrphus opinator which is said to be common in central California. It's a handsome fly for HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2021)

File.Name: l213a_l213-018.jpg
Notes:
50941069576 This is another small Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) on what's left of a flower bud in a shrub of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) that's been hit with a plant disease, see this photo for more info. The spider looks like one of the large bottlecap-size Halloween spiders like in this photo with those horns on its abdomen, though it's much smaller - just 650 pixels long in another 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.7 mm - about 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.). I'm thinking this one might be Araneus montereyensis with that noticeable dip between the horns, which would make it different from the similar Araneus bispinosus in this photo without much dip - see Alice Abelia's comment on this photo. It also appears to be male with enlarged palps. (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2021)

Today started cloudy and windy and finished sunny and windy - the wind was constant with 30-40 mph gusts all day. Our NWS Wind Advisory has become a more serious High Wind Warning. It sounds like the wind will stick around for a few days and get worse before it gets better. There's nothing to do but bump up the ISO and take lots of photos to get a few keepers.

File.Name: l213b_l213-004.jpg
Notes: 50940315833 Here is another shot of (I assume) the female Hammock Spider (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae) that I show in my [Previous] photo taken in the woods today. She's hanging from a web in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). What's curious is that between these two shots, she seemed to move in on a male spider that already had a web here. I caught them mating yesterday - I'll have a photo for Valentine's Day tomorrow - but the male is nowhere to be found today. I found something similar last month where a female spider of the same kind seemed to replace a male, see this photo. This time I know hanky panky has been going on, but I'm still curious what happened to the male. (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2021)

File.Name: l213c_l211-019.jpg
Notes: 50941074902 This is another Hammock Spider (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae) in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods a couple of days ago. It's a female with slender palps. What's interesting is that she seemed to be stalking the male spider I showed in this photo and this photo more than a week ago. The male came out on its web around the corner, and the lady became all excited and raised her legs up like this. I have another photo of this spider alone as my [Next] photo taken today. In between I caught them mating, but I'll save that photo for Valentine's Day tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2021)

File.Name: l213d_l210-003.jpg
Notes: 50941035697 Here are two shots of a tiny spider on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods a few days ago. The shot on the left works for scale if you know our oak trees, otherwise I can count pixels in the 1:1 macro shot on the right - about 460 pixels long, which comes to 2.3 mm - less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I believe this is a Mesh Web Weaver spider in the family Dictynidae. It has the leaves at the end of this branch wrapped with fine webbing while waiting for prey. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2021)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Mesh Web Weaver

File.Name: l214a_l214-001.jpg
Notes:
50944042023 I was staring right at this dead-looking Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) shrub, when I suddenly saw the dragonfly (Odonata) hanging from it like some strange fruit! It's our usual winter dragonfly, a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) in the family Libellulidae, like the one I last showed in this photo taken a month ago. I'm not sure if I keep finding the same one or whether they are all different. These dragonflies stay active in the canyon all winter long, though they sit out cold and windy days like today by finding an out-of-the-way perch like this. They keep moving around, so it's always a surprise when you suddenly notice one right in front of you. It's interesting how something so colorful can be so well camouflaged. (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2021)

Yesterday's wind turned into this morning's breeze, and then it turned back into wind again this afternoon with 20-30 mph gusts. *Sigh* It sounds like we're in for a few more windy days, and there's still no rain in sight.

File.Name: l214b_l213-012.jpg
Notes: 50944779077 Here is a flower of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum, Grossulariaceae) showing its heart-shaped anthers. As an ocasional botanist, I would actually call these anthers sagittate (arrowhead-like) rather than cordate (heart-like), but what the hay - it's Valentine's Day! (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2021)

File.Name: l214c_l213-018.jpg
