Notes: 50944643756 I never would have thought that ticks were affectionate creatures, but here are two examples involving Western Black-legged Ticks (Ixodes pacificus) on the same dead plant stem in the woods taken on two consecutive days. On the left is a female with a smaller male, and on the right are two females - though there was also a male about six inches down the stem, wink-wink. I've found ticks being affectionate before, see this photo. I guess showing affection is what St. Valentine's Day is all about! (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2021)

File.Name: l214d_l212-012.jpg
Notes: 50943909408 Here are mating or courting Hammock Spiders (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae) in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods a couple of days ago. That's the male on the right with large palps and the female on the left with slender palps. Focus is what it is - I had to scrunch back into the tree to get this shot under a leaf, and they deserve some privacy anyway. I got this photo on the day between the two photos I posted yesterday which show the female moving in on a male who was already on the web and then replacing it after this mating day, see this photo and this photo. It's interesting that I saw another of these female spiders stalking another male on its web today. I'm curious if they will be mating tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2021)

File.Name: l215a_l215-014.jpg
Notes:
50948455927 I found his well-camouflaged caterpillar on the dead end of a living stem of Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae) in the woods today. It's small, less than an inch long. It might well be the same caterpillar I showed in this photo a few weeks ago - it looks similar, and it is on a different stem of the same plant. I'm sure it's the larva of a Moth (Lepidoptera), but I have no idea what kind. I like its speckled face! (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2021)

It was windy all day with 25-35 mph gusts so far. Our NWS Wind Advisory has been extended until Wednesday, and it may end up being extended through the weekend. There's nothing to do but bump up the ISO and take lots of photos to get a few keepers.

File.Name: l215b_l215-020.jpg
Notes: 50948334971 This is another golden Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today, probably species Syrphus opinator which is said to be common in central California. It was windy today, and my photo is not quite sharp, and maybe it's vibrating its wings to get ready for take off? I can't resist trying to photograph every one of these gorgeous flies I see. (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2021)

File.Name: l215c_l215-035.jpg
Notes: 50947606748 This is another Hammock Spider (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae) on its sheet web in a flowering shrub of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum, Grossulariaceae). That's the yellow tip of a flower in the upper-right. This spider has the enlarged palps of a male, and it has a tiny fly (Diptera) for prey. I happened to see a female lurking near this male yesterday, and I was wondering if they'd be mating today. They weren't, but maybe I'll find them tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2021)

File.Name: l215d_l215-010.jpg
Notes:
50948367947 "I found a dimpled spider, fat and white . . ." Robert Frost

I found my dimpled spider on flowering shrub of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum, Grossulariaceae) in the woods today. It's a Crab Spider (Thomisidae), probably genus Mecaphesa with that hairy abdomen, and it really does have dimples on its rear. These are the spiders I usually find waiting for prey on summer flowers. They are just starting to appear for the new season, see this photo for another one from last week. (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2021)

File.Name: l216a_l216-019.jpg
Notes:
50951749176 I found this remarkable Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on what's left of a flower bud in a shrub of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) that's been hit with a plant disease, see this photo for more info. It's probably the same flower bud that I show in this photo with a different spider a few days ago, but the one in my photo today is even smaller. I believe it's a Ground Crab Spider in the genus Xysticus. The colors and rough texture of its abdomen texture are remarkable - and a perfect match for that diseased plant! I can't find this spider at BugGuide, and any help with ID will be appreciated. (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2021)

The chilly winds were blowing again today with 25-35 mph gusts all day. That makes it hard to get my kind of photos since my macro lens magnifies the motion as well as the tiny spiders. The NWS Forecast Discussion sums it up like this: "Fair skies and breezy conditions will persist for the week.

File.Name: l216b_l216-002.jpg
Notes: 50951697951 I moved a towel that was left out on a seat cushion last night, and this spider jumped out! I reckon we were both startled, and I managed to get the photo before it got away. I believe it's a Wolf Spider in the family Lycosidae, though I never did get a clear view of its face. It's not small. (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2021)

File.Name: l216c_l215-005.jpg
Notes: 50951669676 I found this female Hammock Spider (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae) sprawled across these oak leaves like the victim of a sacrifice. It's quite alive though, it did move when I poked it but not much. I don't know what the story is, but I believe there is one. (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2021)

File.Name: l216d_l216-010.jpg
Notes: 50950944698 Can you spot the caterpillar on this stem of Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae) that is leafing out in the woods today? It's the same caterpillar that I show in my [Previous] photo from yesterday (here). Hint, the caterpillar has turned around, well-camouflaged indeed! (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2021)

File.Name: l217a_l217-008.jpg
Notes:
50954852716 I found this interesting spider under a loose piece of oak bark in our woodpile this morning. It has striking colors, a combination of red and black, including its all-black abdomen which is not visible in this photo. I believe this spider is genus Kibramoa in the spider family Plectreuridae, possibly species Kibramoa madrona. It appears to be a male with remarkable palps that look very suggestive. Most of the spiders I find are entelegynes but this spider is a haplogyne - the difference has something to do with their sexual apparatus, the females lack an epigynum, but I figure that's more information than I need to know. This one played dead when I found it, it even let me arrange its legs with a stick to better show its face without complaining. I covered it up again with the bark after I got my photos. (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2021)

Beer and other necessities were getting low again, so it was time to make our usual shopping run to the Santa Ynez Valley that we do every two weeks - but I made time for a few photos before we left. It was too windy for photos anyway with gusts to 35 mph all day long. Tomorrow might be better, before the wind picks up again for the weekend.

File.Name: l217b_l216-005.jpg
Notes: 50954110373 Here is a boat-like seed capsule of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) with a deck of webbing across the top. This is the work of a caterpillar rather than spider webbing, see this photo for the caterpillar responsible. It's the caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly, and they are social web-spinners when they are young, see this photo from last July. They are less social as they mature, but they still like company, see this photo. I already have my two photos for the Web Wednesday group - but Happy Web Webnesday anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2021)

File.Name: l217c_l215-030.jpg
Notes: 50954894092 This is the sheet-like web of a Hammock Spider (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae) seen on edge with a flower of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum, Grossulariaceae) dipping perfectly into it. Hah, the tiny spider that made the web is hanging under it on the right. It's a male with enlarged palps, see this photo for a closer shot of the same spider. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2021)

File.Name: l217d_l211-030.jpg
Notes: 50954757606 This is the composite web of a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) in a shrub of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). The spider's orb web is on the left and its cobweb-like retreat on the right, and there is a "sensing line" which connects the two and allows the spider to sense the vibrations of its prey. What I find most interesting is that the sensing line is actually under tension, pulling the center of the web towards the spider. I guess it carries vibrations better that way, like an old-fashioned cup and string telephone which only works if the string is tight. You can actually see the spider in its retreat tending the sensing line - at least a couple of its legs. The structure of the web is similar to the different web in this photo, but this spider and web are larger. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2021)

File.Name: l218a_l218-023.jpg
Notes:
50958171837 I found this tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on the edge of the plexiglass windshield of the little electric cart I use to get around in our canyon today. It's a male with enlarged palps, and I suspect it's another genus Mecaphesa spider with that hairy body. It's tiny, about 590 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.5 mm - about 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) Of course, that's not including its very long front legs! I wonder what it was doing in my cart? (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2021)

After a couple of days of big north winds, today was very nice. The wind was just a breeze from the south, and it actually warmed up to 60∞. The wind might pick up again over the weekend.

File.Name: l218b_l218-008.jpg
Notes: 50958077097 I believe this is a tiny Psyllid (Psylloidea, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera ) insect on a glandular stem of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum, Grossulariaceae) in the woods today. At least I believe it's a psyllid, but barklice (Psocodea) can look similar since they both have tent-like wings at rest, compare this photo - even their names sound similar, psyllids and psocids! I'm pretty sure the one I found today is a proper psyllid, and it's interesting that I found it on Bitter Gooseberry since these insects are often host-specific. There is a photo at BugGuide of species Cacopsylla notapennis on the same plant, and maybe mine is the same kind? It's small, about 940 pixels long, which comes to 4.0 mm - a bit more than 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) It's an attractive insect, like a tiny cicada. (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2021)

File.Name: l218c_l215-044.jpg
Notes: 50957212403 This is a little Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae) hiding on an oak leaf - showing its rear end, not its face, though it looks like a face! I believe it's a lady cricket, and that's her ovipositor for laying eggs down below those two orange-colored appendages that look like fat antennae. I believe these crickets work the night shift and try to hide during the day, but they do it "Ostrich style" in the belief that if they can't see you then you can't see them - and of course that doesn't work with nosy photographers! This might be the first of these crickets I've seen this year, though I did find two other tiny ones last month that could be the same thing, see this photo and this photo. The way these crickets hide with their rear ends sticking out makes them perfect for . . . HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2021)

File.Name: l218d_l215-037.jpg
Notes: 50957992957 This is the rear end of an ant (Formicidae) which is digging into a pistillate catkin of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) down by the creek a couple of days ago. These trees are dioecious with male and female flowers on different plants, see this photo for a comparison shot of both. The catkins look wind-pollinated, but I'm sure that both male and female catkins produce nectar and are host to many insects - including these ants which are the first ones to find the open flowers this year. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2021)

File.Name: l219a_l219-019.jpg
Notes:
50960404258 Here is a small spider trying to balloon away from a leaf of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum, Grossulariaceae) in the woods today. That line of webbing coming from its elevated rear end is enough to carry it away - at least that's the idea, though it didn't seem to be quite windy enough for lift off this morning. This is a Crab Spider (Thomisidae), probably genus Mecaphesa with that hairy abdomen, and probably the same dimpled spider I showed in this photo a few days ago. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2021)

There was more wind than I was expecting today, though it was still not enough to get this spider off the ground this morning, and I did manage to get my photos for the day. I'm sure it was windy enough to give the spider a ride in the afternoon, with 25-35 mpg gusts. We are under a NWS Wind Advisory all day tomorrow. Wind - not rain - dominates the forecast for the rest of the month. February should be the wettest part of our rainy season in California, but it's a bust this year. There's info at Daniel Swain's California Weather Blog with the title "Unusually dry conditions to persist into March".

File.Name: l219b_l219-008.jpg
Notes: 50961173402 Here is one more photo of the tiny Psyllid (Psylloidea) insect that I showed yesterday in this photo - but I got this shot today, it doesn't appear than the insect has moved in 24 hours. It's resting on a glandular stem of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum, Grossulariaceae) - you can see little drops of glandular resin on the stem. See yesterday's photo for more info about this interesting insect. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2021)

File.Name: l219c_l219-009.jpg
Notes: 50961147232 This is the bristly behind of a fly (Diptera) on an oak leaf in the woods today. It looks bristly enough to be a Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae - but I know I say that too often. Yeah, this would be perfect for the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday group, but that was yesterday. Today it's time for . . . HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2021)

File.Name: l219d_l218-029.jpg
Notes:
50961014126 These are the light-trails of tiny Midge (Chironomidae) flies that dance in the sunbeams - taken with a slow exposure to show their movement. Sometimes you can see the steady beating of their wings as little blips in their trails that makes them look like tiny centipedes. And sometimes you can see two trails for each fly's two wings. My exposure time was 1/4 second, and I count about 42 beats in that time, so they are beating their tiny wings 168 times a second - really fast! I assume this is a mating thing. See this photo for what might be the same flies at rest.

These little flies are quite invisible unless you stand facing the sun just so - close, but not too close. A dark background is essential. Now the trick is to slow your camera down. I got this photo at 1/4 sec @ f/16 @ ISO 100, on a tripod of course. Some post-processing to bump up the contrast helps. In some years, we have large mating swarms of these flies raising like clouds above the oak trees - see this photo from two years ago. You'd think the flies would be an easy target for birds and other predators, but it's like swimming into a school of anchovies - they're all around but impossible to touch. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2021)

File.Name: l220a_l220-004.jpg
Notes:
50964550052 Me and my shadow. . . . This is another handsome California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly on a sandstone outcrop in the woods today. It has its proboscis out - no doubt trying to get water from the rock. Sandstone holds a lot of water after a rain, but there's probably not much left after our good rain last month - the moss and liverworts on the same outcrop have mostly dried out already to wait for the next rain. This butterfly is part of a group that overwinter as adults and come out on warm days like today. I rarely see them in summer. (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2021)

It was very windy all day with gusts to 50 mph according to a neighbor's weather station. It was really too windy for my kind of photos, but I got a few by virtue of bumping up the ISO and taking lots of photos to get a few keepers. Darn, I missed some decent photos too. Our NWS Wind Advisory expires later tonight, and I hope that's the end of it.

File.Name: l220b_l220-030.jpg
Notes: 50963688433 This is a goofy little Treehopper (family Membracidae) by a stipule at the base of a leaf of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the woods today. It's small, probably only not much more than t 1/8 inch long though I couldn't get a 1:1 photo to use to measure it by counting pixels. That odd bump on its head is really an extension of its thorax which also extends backwards over its abdomen. I don't know what kind of treehopper this is, and I believe it's a new one for me. See my [Previous] photo for closer shots, though I like being able to see the whole insect in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2021)

File.Name: l220c_l220-020.jpg
Notes: 50964463927 Here are two closer shots of the same little Treehopper (family Membracidae) that I show in my [Next] photo. It's on the leaf and stem of a vine of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the woods today. I'm don't know what kind it is, though I'm sure it's not the same as the Coast Live Oak Treehopper (Telamona vestita) that I show in this photo - it's much smaller, and the horn on its head has a more abrupt shape. You can find all my treehopper photos with a search. This appears to be a new one. (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2021)

File.Name: l220d_l219-015.jpg
Notes: 50964343016 Here is one more shot of the little Crab Spider (Mecaphesa, Thomisidae) that I show trying to balloon away in my [Previous] photo from yesterday. It's in a shrub of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum, Grossulariaceae). I got this photo up close with the sun behind. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2021)

File.Name: l221a_l221-026.jpg
Notes:
50967331173 This is another tough-looking Spiny Assassin Bug (genus Sinea, Reduviidae) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. You can see the prominent spines on its front legs that give it its name. They are gnarly-looking predators with that rough exterior and a spike-like proboscis, though they are actually shy and retiring in my experience. This isn't the best photo, but they are a favorite of mine - I want to show every individual I can find. (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2021)

The big north winds died down today, but there was still a breeze from the south to keep things interesting. It was warmer today too, in the mid-60s. Hah, it's funny how the breeze seems to pick up whenever I'm trying hard to get a photo like this one today!
CommonNames: Spiny Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: l221b_l221-027.jpg
Notes: 50967980681 Here are three shots of a little spider hanging on a thread in an oak tree today turning around and around in the breeze. At first I thought it was dead, and I didn't notice the exuviae or shed skin after molting that it's hanging from until I got the photos up on my computer screen. I reckon the spider is air-drying after its molt, and the breeze is probably helping it. The spider is another Hammock Spider (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae) - a lady with slender palps and a dingus-like epigynum visible on the left. (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2021)

File.Name: l221c_l221-005.jpg
Notes: 50967255388 This is a little "looper caterpillar" of a Geometrid Moth (Geometridae, Lepidoptera) pretending to be a stick on a leaflet of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the woods today. These caterpillars stand on just two pairs of hind prolegs with three pairs of true legs on the other end. The caterpillar is cantilevered out from the plant stem and held taut with a line of webbing that's barely visible here. Pretty good camouflage - at least it would be if it was green or the leaf was brown. As it is, I noticed it right away. (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2021)

File.Name: l221d_l221-020.jpg
Notes: 50967922376 Here is another caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on a new stem of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) that has been galled by the Monkey Flower Stem Gall Midge (Neolasioptera diplaci) in the family Cecidomyiidae, Diptera. It's interesting how the galled leaves look different from the ungalled leaves - a lighter color with more lobed margins, see this photo which shows them both. I can tell at a glance which stems are galled. (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2021)
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Southern Bush Monkeyflower; Monkey Flower Stem Gall Midge

File.Name: l222a_l222-006.jpg
Notes:
50971524126 Here is one more photo taken today of the tiny Psyllid (Psylloidea) insect that I've showed before in this photo and this photo. The tiny insect has moved to a glandular flower stalk on a different branch of its Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum, Grossulariaceae) shrub. This is still the only one I've found here. If you look into the clear part of its wing, you can see that it's making something white - eggs or a waxy exudate? Some of these psyllids produce great quantities of white waxy stuff, see this photo of a different species. (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2021)

Spring is still a month away by the calendar, but it felt like spring today with temps into the 70s. The morning breeze was from the south, not bad at all. A nice day - but where's the rain? February should be our rainiest month, but it's a bust this year.

File.Name: l222b_l222-016.jpg
Notes:
50971541157 Today I went back to where I found this spider hanging from a thread yesterday. It had just molted, and I wanted to see if I could find the exuviae - the shed skin. I lifted up the leaf and held it in place with a clothespin - a handy tool to carry. There's the exuviae on the right with a spider besides it - but it's a male spider, and the spider in my previous photo is female! If you look a little further left, you can barely make out the female waiting in the shadows. Yep, they were "doing it" - I disturbed them by lifting the leaf, but they got right back to what they were doing, and I'll have a photo tomorrow for hump day. (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2021)

A question - do spiders continue to molt as adults or are they like insects with one final molt to reach maturity? Maybe they do continue molting as adults, but the female in my photo seems to be rushing it by molting yesterday and mating today!

File.Name: l222c_l221-009.jpg
Notes: 50970700033 This is the whole life-history of a tiny leaf-miner larva that's completed its tunnel through a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). You can follow the path of its life from a small start to a big finish, I'll add notes. If you look close at the big end of the mine, you can see a little flap where the larvae got away. Hah, a couple of years ago I happened to be in just the right place at just the right time to catch another one that appears to be the same kind in the act of emerging, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2021)

File.Name: l222d_l222-025.jpg
Notes: 50970675913 Here is another shot taken today of the same tough-looking Spiny Assassin Bug (genus Sinea, Reduviidae) that I showed yesterday in my [Previous] photo (here). I'm pretty sure it's the same insect, this time walking on a stem of the same Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree. In this photo you can see its spike-like proboscis folded under its head. They are gnarly-looking predators, though they are actually shy and retiring in my experience. (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2021)
CommonNames: Spiny Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: l223a_l223-025.jpg
Notes:
50974925081 Here is a Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) climbing on the sandstone rock we keep in the water tub by our still-dry creek. They mostly stay at their nests in mid-winter, but it was warm enough today that they were out and about. These wasps look ferocious, but I've never been bothered by them - even when they nest under the eaves of our front porch. (San Marcos Pass, 23 February 2021)

It was another spring-like day with temps into the 70s. There was some breeze from the south but not too bad. It was warm enough to bring out the wasps and also some of the larger lizards which need longer to warm up than the youngsters. Tomorrow is expected to be a notch cooler but still pleasant, with the potential for real wind in the evening.

File.Name: l223b_l223-018.jpg
Notes: 50974888366 This is another tiny Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) in its nest in an old seed whorl of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. The spider is small but attractive, about 670 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.8 mm - less than 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) The webbing by its nest looks like a tangle, but it has a proper orb web off to the left which it "listens to" with one leg on a "sensing line" - see this photo. I'll try to get a better photo of this one's web tomorrow for the Web Wednesday group. (San Marcos Pass, 23 February 2021)

File.Name: l223c_l223-007.jpg
Notes: 50974869871 This is a female Hammock Spider (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae) hanging under her sheet web in the woods today. You can clearly see her darkened epigynum on the screen-right end of her abdomen, her organ for receiving the genetic material of a male - like in my [Previous] photo. It's curious that it looks like certain anatomical drawings! (San Marcos Pass, 23 February 2021)

File.Name: l223d_l222-018.jpg
Notes: 50974848546 Here are a mating pair of Hammock Spiders (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae) under a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods yesterday. These are the same spiders I showed yesterday in this photo after they got back to what they were doing. That's the darker male above with the obvious palps and the lighter-colored female below. This photo shows the male transferring his genetic material to the female's epigynum, see this photo for more info. It's almost Wednesday, so Happy Insect (and spider) Hump Day! (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2021)

File.Name: l224a_l224-006.jpg
Notes:
50977977796 Here is another shot taken today of the same little Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) that I've shown a few times already, see this photo and this photo. The spider is usually hunched up in a little bundle in this leaf cluster of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae), but today it was looking right at me - it's the first time I've seen its face. The spider is small, only about 1/8 inch long. It looks like one of the large bottlecap-size Halloween spiders with humps on its back like in this photo, though it's much smaller. I believe this one is either Araneus bispinosus or Araneus montereyensis, see Alice Abelia's comment on this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2021)

Maybe it was a couple of degrees cooler today than yesterday, but it was still pleasantly warm, up to 70∞. It was not particularly breezy today, but we are under a NWS Wind Advisory tonight.

File.Name: l224b_l224-022.jpg
Notes: 50977947136 This little Crab Spider (Mecaphesa, Thomisidae) is curled up in a bristly old stem of Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae). It's an annual plant that dies after one season, but we've had so little rain this winter that the old plant stems are still around. See this photo for what the plants look like when they still have flowers. They make a nice retreat for this spider. (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2021)

File.Name: l224c_l224-002.jpg
Notes: 50977214023 Spiders aren't the only ones! This is the nest of a web-spinning caterpillar binding together several leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I can't see the caterpillar, but I can see where it's been eating the leaves which are on their way to becoming skeletonized. I have photos of the caterpillar that's probably involved here, here, and here. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2021)

File.Name: l224d_l219-024.jpg
Notes: 50977189168 Spider webs are often nearly invisible, but sometimes when the light is just right they jump out at you like this one! It's the web of some kind of orb weaver in a small oak tree. I can't see the spider, but I can see that it's captured a number of tiny flies. I like all those out of focus highlights on the leaves in the background. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2021)

File.Name: l225a_l225-011.jpg
Notes:
50981249322 Here is a handsome California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I usually see these butterflies on the ground, so I was surprised to find this one up in a tree. It does have its proboscis out like it's puddling on the leaf - maybe harvesting the morning dew? This butterfly is part of a group that overwinter as adults and come out on warm days like today, though this one seems especially gorgeous. (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2021)

It was cooler today but still quite pleasant, in the mid-60s. There was a breeze, but I wouldn't call it windy despite our NWS Wind Advisory which has now been extended until noon tomorrow. The forecast is for wind all weekend.

File.Name: l225b_l225-036.jpg
Notes: 50981101066 This is an Umber Skipper (Poanes melane) butterfly perched on a water meter cover by our wellhead in the canyon. (All of the outside plumbing is painted blue.) It's the first skipper of any kind that I've seen this year. I didn't photograph my first skipper last year until May 11, and the year before was April 27, but maybe all that means is that I've been spending more time in the canyon this year and noticing more details. Hah, I haven't been anywhere else since the state stay-at-home order for the Covid-19 pandemic was announced last March 19 except for the occasional run for beer and other necessities. Now I'm in it for the record, and I plan to stay put until March 19 to complete my year in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2021)

File.Name: l225c_l224-032.jpg
Notes: 50981056331 This is another Paper Wasp that's been awakened by our recent warm weather visiting new flowers of non-native succulent Jade Plant (Crassula ovata, Crassulaceae) in the garden yesterday. But it's interesting that this is not our usual Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) that nests under the eaves of our front porch nearby because it lacks the petiolate (stalked) abdomen that is clearly visible in this photo. Maybe this one is the Golden Paper Wasp (Polistes aurifer) which I don't believe I seen here before. Whatever the wasp is, this photo shows its rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2021)

File.Name: l225d_l219-021.jpg
Notes: 50980329858 Here is another shot of a small Crab Spider (Thomisidae) trying to balloon away from a leaf of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum, Grossulariaceae) in the woods a few days ago, compare this photo of the same spider. That line of webbing coming from its elevated rear end should be enough to carry it away - at least that's the idea, though it wasn't quite windy enough for lift off on this particular day. This photo shows the spider's spinnerets, and it's interesting that it is only using the upper ones to make this special webbing. Is that like having different taps for different brews? HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2021)

File.Name: l226a_l225-008.jpg
Notes:
50983467243 Here is another handsome Cicada (Cicadidae) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods yesterday. This is the second one I've found this year, compare this photo from February 1. I believe these are both genus Platypedia. I don't think these early cicadas sing at all, they just make the clicking noise that I sometimes hear up in the oaks. I don't run into these handsome insects very often, but I do seem to find one or more in most years. (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2021)

It was breezy this morning becoming windy in the afternoon. It was also pleasantly warm, up to 70∞ again after being a bit cooler yesterday. Wind might be an issue tomorrow, and we're under a continuing NWS Wind Advisory until Sunday.

File.Name: l226b_l226-007.jpg
Notes: 50984240442 This is a long-legged fly (Diptera) that probably isn't a proper Long-legged Fly in the family Dolichopodidae, though it doesn't seem to be "just a fly" like so many of them are. I don't know what kind of fly it is, and any help with ID will be appreciated. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 26 February 2021)

File.Name: l226c_l225-004.jpg
Notes: 50984098586 This interesting fly (Diptera) landed right in front of me on an oak leaf in a small tree yesterday and began grooming. At first I thought it was a hairy bee like this one - but no, it's clearly a fly. It's got long white hairs like a March Fly (Bibionidae) and a scooped out space between its eyes on top of its head like a Robber Fly (Asilidae) and a proboscis like nothing else I know! I don't know what kind of fly it is, and any help with ID will be appreciated. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2021)

File.Name: l226d_l226-003.jpg
Notes: 50983357398 "Excuse me while I slip into something more comfortable." This is the exuviae of a caterpillar - the shed skin after molting into a bigger size. It's what's left of the caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly in one of those boat-like seed capsules of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) that they like to occupy - see this photo for a twosome. There's some webbing in this photo also, and I suspect the caterpillars like to strap themselves in as an aide to shedding their old skin. That word "exuviae" sounds plural, but it is the preferred word even in the singular. It occurs to me that the word "remains" as in "someone's remains" works the same way. (San Marcos Pass, 26 February 2021)

File.Name: l227a_l227-006.jpg
Notes:
50987663397 This is a ferocious little ant (Formicidae) on the back of an odd Scale Insect (Coccoidea) on a stem of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I know about ants and aphids, but I don't know if ants also get honeydew from these scale insects - but see this photo. If so, then I understand the ferocity of the ant - I know a dog that also gets that way if you go anywhere near his food bowl while he's eating! Is that an immature scale insect to the upper-right along the stem? There are a couple more on the other end of the stem that are out of focus. Maybe they are what the ants are protecting? (San Marcos Pass, 27 February 2021)

The NWS didn't lie about our wind advisory, we've had 25-40 mph gusts all day. That's really too windy to be out getting photos, but I got lucky by waiting for relatively calm moments and taking LOTS of photos and picking out the keepers. I hope the wind subsides tonight.
CommonNames: ant; Scale Insect; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: l227b_l227-017.jpg
Notes: 50987627507 Here are two shots of a little bee-like Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) working a new staminate catkin of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) down by the creek today. It's not as golden as the fly in this photo, but it does have a gold sheen about it that is attractive. The fly is dusted with pollen, but I'm sure it's really after nectar which is found under those little dark scales at the base of the individual stamens. (San Marcos Pass, 27 February 2021)

File.Name: l227c_l227-015.jpg
Notes: 50987571257 Here is a small invader exploring a caterpillar's nest on a dead leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) that's stuck up in the tree in the woods today. I believe the invader is the larva of a Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) - possibly the larva of the species Syrphus opinator which I've been showing lately, e.g. in this photo. The caterpillar makes a nest in the oak leaves with webbing and skeletonizes the leaves, look above the larva and to the right. But why would a fly larva be interested in a caterpillar, aphids are more to their liking. Maybe the caterpillar has already abandoned the nest and something else has moved in? I don't know the answer. (San Marcos Pass, 27 February 2021)

File.Name: l227d_l225-029.jpg
Notes: 50987542592 A couple of days ago, I posted a photo of a Paper Wasp that was not our usual Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) with a stalked abdomen, see this photo. Here's another wasp that is different on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I guess both wasps are the Golden Paper Wasp (Polistes aurifer), and it's interesting that I don't believe I seen them in the canyon before. Probably I just haven't noticed them because they are "just another wasp" - but those orange colors on its thorax and lack of a petiole seem pretty distinctive and hard to miss. (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2021)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Golden Paper Wasp

File.Name: l228a_l228-010.jpg
Notes:
50990593863 Here is another shot of a tough-looking Spiny Assassin Bug (genus Sinea, Reduviidae) on a stem of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. This might be the same predator I showed recently in this photo and this photo - at least it's on a different branch of the same tree. That also happens to be the same dead leaf that I showed yesterday in this photo of a Syrphid Fly larva. It's not a small oak tree, but it is a small world! (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2021)

The big winds yesterday finally died down, but there was still enough residual breeze from the south this morning to make getting new photos a challenge - like usual. It was cooler today, into the 60s. There was talk of a bit of rain on Wednesday, but that chance seems to have evaporated away as of tonight.
CommonNames: Spiny Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: l228b_l228-016.jpg
Notes: 50991223816 Look close! There's a well-camouflaged "looper caterpillar" of a Geometrid Moth (Geometridae, Lepidoptera) stretched right across the middle of this photo from left to right. It's in a leaf cluster of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) along with several of the boat-like seed capsules that I've been showing lately. One end of the caterpillar begins at the dried out remains of a flower attached to the seed capsule on top. That's actually a key character that distinguished the plant genus Diplacus from Mimulus, whether it has "corolla semi-persistent, shriveling on the developing capsule" or "corolla deciduous, falling away from the developing capsule". This shrub is clearly the former. I tried to move the shriveled flower to better expose the caterpillar, but its hind prolegs were firmly attached. (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2021)

File.Name: l228c_l228-003.jpg
Notes: 50990513638 I spotted this little Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) looking right at me from behind an oak leaf today. I got this quick photo, and the fly got away. (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2021)

File.Name: l228d_l227-012.jpg
Notes: 50990483353 Here is another shot of the same kind of wasp that I showed in this photo yesterday - the paper wasp without a stalked abdomen, probably the Golden Paper Wasp (Polistes aurifer). They mostly stay at their nests in mid-winter, but it's been warm enough that they are out and about. This one looks kind of *blah* - like it needs a cup of coffee to get moving. (San Marcos Pass, 27 February 2021)

File.Name: l301a_l301-006.jpg
Notes:
50995180981 Here is a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly on the ground today - the first one I've been able to photograph this year. I've been worried about these fine butterflies - they overwinter as caterpillars, and last year I had great luck finding them, but this year I haven't found a single one. *Sigh* It's been such an odd weather year, with almost no rain at all until the end of December, and I've been worried they might be impacted. The caterpillars are amazing, see this photo for an example from last summer. I'm glad that at least one has made it. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2021)

It was unexpectedly windy this morning with 15-20 mph gusts from the south - not a big wind but enough to keep everything moving. Despite the wind, I got some decent photos today. Our 20% chance of rain is back in the record for Wednesday. I'm not expecting anything, but it would be nice.

File.Name: l301b_l301-023.jpg
Notes: 50995114866 There are mosquito-like Dance Flies (Empididae, Diptera) on different trees of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) down by the creek today. The one on the left is on a neat pistillate catkin of a female tree, while the one on the right has been working the messy pollen-bearing staminate catkins of a male tree. Pollen-dusted or not, both flies are after nectar instead of pollen, and both kinds of willow catkins do provide. It's my understanding that these flies are primarily predators, but I find them every year nectaring on the catkins. These are the first of the flies that I've seen this year. I've been worried that they would miss the party because our first rains were so late, but at least a few have made it. I'll bet there will be more tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2021)

File.Name: l301c_l301-029.jpg
Notes: 50995065266 I believe this really is a Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae visiting new flowers of non-native succulent Jade Plant (Crassula ovata, Crassulaceae) in the garden today. Maybe it's genus Peleteria though there are other similar species that are hard to tell apart in a photo - compare the photos at BugGuide. I have more photos to post later this week that show that it really does have a subscutellum which BugGuide says is characteristic for the family. If I'm right, the larvae of these interesting flies parasitize other insect larvae. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2021)

File.Name: l301d_l301-025.jpg
Notes: 50995160512 Julie found this earthstar "mushroom" in the woods today and brought it to me to photograph on a bed of oak leaves in a place where I've found them before. I add scare quotes because these fungi are not at all fleshy like proper mushrooms are. I believe this is the Hygroscopic Earthstar (Astraeus hygrometricus). These fungal fruiting bodies are built with an inner puffball or spore sack surrounded by a thick outer rind. The rind splits to make the star, and the inner sack opens to release spores - first with a terminal pore like this one and later with an open slit. The rays of the outer rind open and close depending on the humidity, which effectively lifts the spore sack off the ground. The inner puffball is ephemeral, but the outer rinds are tough and can last for years. They look like something that crawled from the sea and stopped here! (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2021)

File.Name: l302a_l302-010.jpg
Notes:
50998566132 This fly (Diptera) is thoroughly dusted with pollen from a staminate catkin of of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) down by the creek today. It appears to be "just a fly" - but it's interesting to find it working these flowers some distance from any house or trash. If it visits a female pistillate tree later on, pollination might happen. Pollen-dusted as it is, the fly is after the tree's nectar which resides under the black scales that are visible at the base of the individual stamens. "It's a dirty job, but someone's gotta do it!" (San Marcos Pass, 2 March 2021)

It was windy again this morning with 10-20 mph gusts from the south - not a big wind but enough to keep everything moving. We still have a 20% chance of rain on Wednesday. I'm not expecting anything, but it would be nice.

File.Name: l302b_l302-021.jpg
Notes: 50998532942 Here is a Crab Spider (Thomisidae) with a wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) under a curled new leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I believe the spider is genus Mecaphesa, a female with slender palps. I would also guess that the bee is genus Andrena which are known to be active early in the season, compare this photo from last February. (San Marcos Pass, 2 March 2021)

File.Name: l302c_l302-005.jpg
Notes: 50998510942 Here is a male Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a leaf of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. I can tell it's a male because of its long legs and those enlarged palps which have a sexual function. I would guess that it's the hairy genus Mecaphesa, but then what is the spider in this photo? Maybe the one in my photo today is the same as the one in this photo and this photo with a very different mate that shows extreme sexual dimorphism? Yep, I'm confused - any help with ID will be appreciated. (San Marcos Pass, 2 March 2021)

File.Name: l302d_l301-011.jpg
Notes: 50997677708 This is a flower of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum, Grossulariaceae) with ants (Formicidae) investigating. I'm not sure what the ants are after, but I find them regularly on these flowers. This was a lucky shot with shade and contrasty light that came out just right. It's always worth trying for a photo. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2021)

File.Name: l303a_l303-005.jpg
Notes:
51001090223 Today was a shopping day, but I had time for a few photos in the morning before we left. I found this really tiny spider on a cactus spine in the garden. It's only about 370 pixels long in these 1:1 macro shots, which comes to 1.6 mm - about 1/16 inch. (See here for how I figure.) That's even smaller than the other tiny spider I posted today, compare this photo. The two spiders look similar, though that one is an orb weaver and this one has no sign of an orb web. It does have something even tinier for a prey. Tiny as it is, this is a handsome spider. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2021)

Beer and other necessities were getting low again, so it was time to make our usual shopping run to the Santa Ynez Valley that we do every two weeks. I got these photos before we left - in light rain this morning! *Sigh* We officially got 2/100 inch of rain - not much, but at least it was cloudy for the day. If we're going to get "March Miracle" rains this year, it will have to try harder than this.

File.Name: l303b_l302-001.jpg
Notes: 51000999968 Here is the web of a Bowl and Doily Spider (Frontinella communis aka Frontinella pyramitela, Linyphiidae) in a plant of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae). This web is not as well-defined as some, but I'm pretty sure of my ID - compare this photo. It consists of three main parts. The thickest part in my photo is "the bowl" of non-sticky webbing. The spider hangs below this and grabs its prey from underneath. Below this is "the doily" which seems to be like an oil pan to catch falling prey. Above the bowl is random webbing that supports the bowl and might knock down flying insects into it. I saw the spider when I got this shot, but I can't see it in the photo - it looks like this. The Deerweed is not doing well in this drought year (so far). This plant is half dead, and it's struggling with new growth - and that's typical this year. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 2 March 2021)

File.Name: l303c_l228-001.jpg
Notes: 51000963553 This is looking at the rear end of a little Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider on the other side of its web. This is what I call the "inner circle" of its web - see this photo to appreciate the difference. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2021)

File.Name: l303d_l224-013.jpg
Notes: 51000926113 This is a really tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) hanging in the center of it's web - though I'm showing it here off-center to show more of its web. The spider is about 430 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 1.8 mm - about 1/16 inch. (See here for how I figure.) It has a perfect orb web despite it's small size, though the whole web is only a few inches across. Maybe it's genus Hypsosinga for its size as much as anything else? Try comparing my first photo today - same or different? I already have my two photos for the Web Wednesday group - but Happy Web Webnesday anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2021)

File.Name: l304a_l304-008.jpg
Notes:
51004790316 Here is a caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly gazing at its shed skin (aka exuviae) after recently molting on a stem of Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae) that is leafing out in the woods today. The caterpillar seems to have a wistful gaze, like it has some emotional attachment to its old skin. I saw this same caterpillar the day before yesterday, and it had not yet molted, so it's just happened. Note that this web-spinning caterpillar has prepared a bed of webbing before molting upon it, I guess as something to pull against as it crawls out of its old skin. See this photo for a closer shot of another caterpillar's exuviae - also with webbing. (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2021)

Time was limited today because we went to town and finally got our first COVID vaccination shots! There was a large gathering in an outdoor parking lot with tents and lines of cars, well-organized, and really quite festive - lots of happy people and volunteers. I did have time for a few photos before our appointment, but I was fighting a chilly wind. I do like this photo of the caterpillar.
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Climbing Penstemon; Heart-leaved Keckiella

File.Name: l304b_l302-018.jpg
Notes: 51004875397 This is the rear end of a pollen-dusted fly (Diptera) working a staminate catkin of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) down by the creek. I have another shot of rhe rest of the fly in this photo. It appears to be "just a fly" - but it's interesting to find it working these native flowers some distance from any house or trash. Pollen-dusted as it is, the fly is after the tree's nectar which resides under dark scales at the base of the individual stamens. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 2 March 2021)

File.Name: l304c_l301-037.jpg
Notes: 51004738796 I believe this is a bristly Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae visiting new flowers of non-native succulent Jade Plant (Crassula ovata, Crassulaceae) in the garden a couple of days ago. It might be genus Peleteria, though there are other similar species that are hard to tell apart from a photo. If you look close, you can see that it really does have a subscutellum which BugGuide says is characteristic for the family - I'll add a note. The larvae of these interesting flies parasitize other insect larvae. This photo shows the fly's bristly rear end, see this photo for the rest of the fly. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2021)

File.Name: l304d_l226-014.jpg
Notes: 51004836112 This is the rear end of a Spiny Assassin Bug (genus Sinea, Reduviidae) disappearing over the edge of a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods a few days ago. This predator looks more impressive on top, compare this photo of what is probably the same critter. I already have my two photos for the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday group - but HBBBT anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 26 February 2021)
CommonNames: Spiny Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: l305a_l305-022.jpg
Notes:
51006954498 I found this handsome "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a stem of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the woods today. It's no doubt some kind of Stink Bug in the family Pentatomidae - the prefix "penta" means five, and you can see its five-segmented antennae in my photo. There are many different kinds, so 'nuff said. After I got this photo, the bug started to drill into the stem with its mouthparts, I'll probably have a photo of the action tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2021)

It was warmer today, almost up to 70∞. There was a pretty strong breeze all morning from the south which made getting even easy photos something to work at. We actually have "rain likely" next week - something to look forward to, though a lot can change between now and then.

File.Name: l305b_l305-015.jpg
Notes: 51007639096 Here is another shot of a tough-looking Spiny Assassin Bug (genus Sinea, Reduviidae) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. This might be the same one I showed recently in this photo and elsewhere - at least it's on a different stem of the same tree. They are gnarly-looking predators, though they are actually shy and retiring in my experience. I want to photograph every one I find - even if it's the same one each time! (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2021)
CommonNames: Spiny Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: l305c_l304-003.jpg
Notes: 51006897193 This is a mosquito-like Dance Fly (Empididae, Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods yesterday, probably genus Empis. They are small but not tiny - this one is about 1300 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 5.4 mm - not quite 1/4 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I think it was just having a time out on the oak leaf because it has a bit of yellow pollen from the nearby willow catkins on its thorax - compare this photo. It's my understanding that these flies are primarily predators, but I find them every year nectaring on the willow catkins. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2021)

File.Name: l305d_l301-031.jpg
Notes: 51006877363 I believe this really is a bristly Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae visiting new flowers of non-native succulent Jade Plant (Crassula ovata, Crassulaceae) in the garden a couple of days ago. I posted this photo of the same fly yesterday that shows that it does have a subscutellum which BugGuide says is characteristic for the family. It might be genus Peleteria, though there are other similar species that are hard to tell apart from a photo. The larvae of these flies parasitize other insect larvae, but the adults visit flowers. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2021)

File.Name: l306a_l306-023.jpg
Notes:
51010903746 Here are two more shots of a remarkable Crab Spider (Thomisidae) in the woods today on what's left of a flower bud in a shrub of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) that's been hit with a plant disease. The spider is small, just 690 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.9 mm - less than 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I showed probably the same spider on the same plant a couple of weeks ago, compare this photo. I believe it's a Ground Crab Spider in the genus Xysticus - a male with enlarged palps. The colors and rough texture of its abdomen are remarkable - and a perfect match for this diseased plant, see this photo for more info about the plant disease. I can't find this distinctive spider at BugGuide, and any help with ID will be appreciated. (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2021)

It's been windy all day, up to 40 mph last night and still around 20 mph this morning when I was out with my camera. Our NWS Wind Advisory expires tonight, and hopefully the wind will back off. It sounds like there really is rain coming next week! We stand at just of 35% of normal for the year, so any new rain will help.

File.Name: l306b_l305-008.jpg
Notes: 51010854616 I happened to spot this little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) about eight feet up in an oak tree in the woods yesterday, and I managed to get this photo standing on my tiptoes with tripod fully extended. I like the effect of the sunlight shining through the leaf from behind. I believe the spider is another in the genus Mecaphesa, a female with slender palps. It's the kind of spider I expect to find on flowers which are still scarce in our canyon, but there are other prey to catch - see this photo from a few days ago. (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2021)

File.Name: l306c_l305-014.jpg
Notes: 51010117873 Here are two shots of a tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) both coming and going on a leafy stipule of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the woods yesterday. Going by the pictures in my book, this might be a male of the species Pelegrina aeneola - but maybe not. It's an attractive spider with rich colors of gold, vanilla, and chocolate. (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2021)

File.Name: l306d_l305-031.jpg
Notes: 51010090868 This is another photo of the little Stink Bug (Pentatomidae) in my [Previous] photo from yesterday, see this photo. It's starting to drill into the stem of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae), and you can see its remarkable jointed mouthparts. Going from top to bottom, first there is the labrum, a piece of skin that covers the top of the rest, not visible here; then there is the dark bundle of thin stylets which contain food and salivary channels, but need support; finally there is the segmented labium which is jointed like an extra leg, and supports the stylets. Put them all together, and they become the beak or rostrum of the bug. I have a closer shot of the mouthparts of a different bug in this photo. I don't suppose the bugs have to think about how it all works anymore than we do! (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2021)

File.Name: l307a_l307-020.jpg
Notes:
51014553981 I found this small but handsome spider under a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. It's tiny, just 620 across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.6 mm, about 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figured.) The shape of its carapace and legs reminds me of a Sheet Web Weaver (Linyphiidae), but the bright colors and round shape of its abdomen seem wrong. I'm thinking it's a Cobweb Weaver aka Comb-Footed Spider in the family Theridiidae, possibly genus Theridion. It does have comb-like hairs on its legs, though they're hard to see in this photo. Whatever it is, it's an attractive spider. (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2021)

It was chilly (for March) and overcast all day. Cloudy and calm days might be my favorites for getting some light into the shadows, but today was cloudy and breezy which makes getting my kind of photos difficult. There's nothing to do but bump up the ISO and take lots of photos to get a few keepers! There's still talk of rain on Tuesday night and Wednesday, now up to an 80% chance - there's even a NWS Winter Storm Watch for possible snow on Wednesday night!
Locality: for March) and overcast all day.  Cloudy and calm days might be my favorites for getting some light into the shadows
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Cobweb Weaver; Comb-Footed Spider

File.Name: l307b_l307-042.jpg
Notes: 51013798488 I can't see under its wings, but I believe this is a Common Sawfly (family Tenthredinidae, Hymenoptera) that I found in the woods today on a leaflet of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae). If I could see under its wings, I should find that it has no sign of a "wasp-waist" unlike most of their cousins. This is the first one I've managed to photograph this year. It has attractive green eyes. Sometimes the larvae of these wasps become pests and eat the new leaves and catkins of oak trees, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2021)

File.Name: l307c_l307-037.jpg
Notes: 51013744043 I found this small creature on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today - but is it a maggot or a caterpillar? I tried turning it over to see if it has legs like a proper caterpillar, but it got away from me before I could tell. Even so, I believe it is the larva of a fly (Diptera), likely a Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae), compare the images at BugGuide. Its head is at the narrow end, and it's pretty active for a critter with no legs! (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2021)

File.Name: l307d_l307-010.jpg
Notes: 51013710668 I believe this is another Hammock Spider (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae) scrunched up under a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. The tuning fork-shaped mark on its carapace and serrated pattern on its back are distinctive. This is a small one, maybe 1/4 inch long or a bit more. This photo also nicely shows the stellate hairs on the oak leaf - little bundles of hairs coming from a common origin. (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2021)

File.Name: l308a_l308-014.jpg
Notes:
51018044932 Here are new leaves and catkins on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree in the woods today. For a few weeks, the new catkins will transflorm these trees into something strange and beautiful . . . at least that's the idea. I'm not expecting a great flowering this year because of our drought, but this particular tree shows that there is hope. (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2021)

The chilly winds were blowing today with 30-40 mph gusts all day. Just as well, we had our septic tank pumped out this morning, and the wind helped dissipate the rather pungent aroma. Our NWS Wind Advisory tonight will transform into a Winter Storm Warning for Tuesday night through Thursday night with the snow level maybe dropping to 2000 feet. A dusting of snow is possible here, and I hope it's only a dusting - snow is hard on the oak trees, especially when they are starting to flower.

File.Name: l308b_l308-008.jpg
Notes: 51017987372 This is a cute little moth (Lepidoptera) hiding on a twig of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I have no idea what kind of moth it is, and it probably looks quite different with its wings open rather than closed like this. I only realized later that I posted another photo of a similar moth a month ago, see this photo - but that one doesn't show that neat patch of blue scales behind its eyes. Any help with ID will be appreciated. Happy Moth Monday! (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2021)

File.Name: l308c_l308-011.jpg
Notes: 51017134203 Here is another little "looper caterpillar" of a Geometrid Moth (Geometridae, Lepidoptera) pretending to be a stick hanging from an old seed capsule of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). If you look close, you can see that it's holding on with two pairs of hind prolegs on top with three pairs of true legs on the other end. Pretty good camouflage. Happy Moth Monday! (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2021)

File.Name: l308d_l307-014.jpg
Notes: 51017943697 This is "just a moth" on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods yesterday. I don't know what kind of moth it is, but I like the camouflage colors with just a hint of green. Any help with ID will be appreciated. Happy Moth Monday! (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2021)

File.Name: l309a_l309-031.jpg
Notes:
51021297536 Here are two shots of what I believe is an actual Gall Wasp in the family Cynipidae today - however, it's not about to lay its eggs in an oak leaf bud, but it was crawling on the canopy of the little electric cart I use to get around our canyon! I have hundreds of photos of wasp galls but few of the wasps themselves, see this album and compare this photo of a similar wasp in its proper habitat. I got a few photos today, and the wasp disappeared back into the woods. Nice encounter! (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2021)

It was windy again today with 30-40 mph gusts - but from the south and west rather than the north like our winds yesterday. Hopefully this is our rain wind bringing our storm which is due to arrive later tonight. It's was chilly today, mostly in the 40s though it got into the 50s in the afternoon. It will drop into the 30s tonight if it hasn't already. We have a 100% chance of rain tonight, maybe an inch or a bit more, and maybe even a bit of snow sometime in the next couple of days. This is no "March miracle" storm that will cancel our drought, but any rain will be appreciated in this dry year.

File.Name: l309b_l309-009.jpg
Notes: 51020522783 It was windy again today, and I was looking for something to photograph that wasn't moving too much - like these oak leaves with tunnels. Each leaf shows the whole life-history (so far) of a tiny leaf-miner larva that has made a tunnel as it grows through its oak leaf. You can follow the path of each one's life from a small start to a big finish, growing as they move along. I don't know what kind of insect larva is responsible, but a couple of years ago I happened to be in just the right place at just the right time to catch one that appears to be the same kind emerging, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2021)

File.Name: l309c_l307-006.jpg
Notes: 51021187851 My first thought is that this is a fungus that is skeletonizing a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. I don't know what the fungus would be, but it starts as a blister and forms a dry cover that finally breaks up and falls away leaving a skeletonized leaf behind. We also have caterpillars that do something similar to the leaves, see this photo and this photo. So maybe the blister is the work of a leaf mining insect instead? This is something to investigate. (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2021)

File.Name: l309d_l306-014.jpg
Notes: 51021163031 Here is another mating pair of Hammock Spiders (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae) under a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods a few days ago. It was a difficult lighting situation with the spiders in the shade and bright sunlight in the same frame - I prefer not to use flash, and I did what I could. See this photo to understand what they're doing. I don't know why I find so many of this particular kind of spider doing what they are doing - but I do. It's almost Wednesday, so Happy Hump Day! (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2021)

File.Name: l310a_l309-015.jpg
Notes:
51024403286 Here is the web-spinning caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on a stem of Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae) with webbing in the woods yesterday. I believe these caterpillars make a little platform of webbing to help them molt, maybe it's something to pull against as it crawls out of its old skin. This one molted a week ago, but then it stayed around its old shed skin or "exuviae" for a few more days, see this photo of them both together. (The caterpillar looks different in that photo, I believe it's still teneral - just emerged from its molt.) When I got this photo yesterday, the shed skin has finally disappeared. If you compare the two photos, you can see that the new shoot of the plant on the right has also disappeared - the caterpillar's been hungry. Spiders aren't the only ones - Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2021)

No photos today, it was too cold and wet. We got 1-1/4 inches of cold rain this morning - along with a fair amount of hail that built up a layer of the upstairs porch. There was enough slippery hail on Cold Springs Bridge a mile down the highway to cause eight vehicles to crash on the bridge and close the highway for a few hours. If there was really "two inches of hail or snow" on the road as reports say, then that was more than we got!

Julie had to go to town while the highway was still closed, so I decided to go along for the ride. When we came home in the afternoon, the highway was open, the hail/snow was all melted, and the rain seemed to be over though clouds remain. We still have a 90% chance of more showers tonight, and we're still under a Winter Storm Warning until Thursday night - that means possible snow. It's cold enough, and it does indeed look like another band of precipitation is coming soon according to the radar map.
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Climbing Penstemon; Heart-leaved Keckiella

File.Name: l310b_l307-024.jpg
Notes: 51024358436 This is looking up into a new flower of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum, Grossulariaceae) in the woods a few days ago. I like the heart-shaped anthers, though I would actually call them sagittate (arrowhead-like) rather than cordate (heart-like). If you look close, you can see little pinhead glands on the "petals" (that are really sepals, the real petals are white). Leaves, flower stalks, and the flowers themselves are all glandular in this species. See this photo for a comparison of our different native gooseberries. (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2021)

File.Name: l310c_l307-001.jpg
Notes: 51024313261 I posted my first photo of new flowers of native California Bay (Umbellularia californica, Lauraceae) a month ago, but other trees in the canyon are just starting 7to open their flowers. The tree is related to sassafras and avocado, and they all have similar surprisingly intricate flowers - see this photo for a closer look at a single flower. Sure, you can use the leaves as "bay leaves" for cooking - but don't use too much, it's strong stuff! (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2021)

File.Name: l310d_l307-007.jpg
Notes: 51024393227 This is webbing associated with an untidy caterpillar rather than a spider - along with frass (caterpillar poop) and partially skeletonized leaves of the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree that it's on. I'm not sure what kind of caterpillar is responsible, but I have photos of them here and here among others. Spiders aren't the only ones - Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2021)

File.Name: l311a_l311-003.jpg
Notes:
51027584566 Here is a lupine leaf with raindrops from our stormy weather today. The leaf belongs to a seedling of Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae) that is coming up under winter-brown leaves of Western Bracken Fern which are visible in the large drop in the center. Nothing holds raindrops like lupine leaves! (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2021)

We had on and off showers for much of the day, mostly light rain but occasionally it picked up. It was a good day to do some computer backups, but I did get out for a short walk close to home. This brings our two-day total for the storm to 1.5 inches and our total for the season to 13.11 inches. That's still only about 40% of our normal rainfall for the year, so we have a long ways to go. We still have a chance for more showers tonight and tomorrow.

File.Name: l311b_l311-005.jpg
Notes: 51027539966 Here is a small Madrone (Arbutus menziesii, Ericaceae) tree being engulfed by a woody vine of native Chaparral Clematis (Clematis lasiantha, Ranunculaceae) that is just starting to leaf out, with a dark woodland of lichen-encrusted Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees behind. I got this photo from our covered porch in the rain today - maybe you can see some streaks of raindrops in the dark areas. I've shown this scene before when I needed a rain photo, try a search of my photos. The Madrone tree somehow puts up with being smothered. (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2021)
CommonNames: Madrone; Chaparral Clematis; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: l311c_l309-019.jpg
Notes: 51027616902 This is the bristly rear end of a small fly (Diptera) on an oak leaf in the woods a few days ago. I suspect it's a Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae just because of all those bristles, but it got away from me before I could get a good look at it. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2021)

File.Name: l311d_l307-027.jpg
Notes: 51027495676 This is another little Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae) hiding in a space between two oak leaves - showing its rear end, not its face. I believe it's a male cricket that lacks the ovipositor of the female in this photo though it does have similar orange-colored appendages. I believe these crickets work the night shift and try to hide during the day, but they do it "Ostrich style" in the belief that if they can't see you then you can't see them - and of course that doesn't work very well with nosy photographers! HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2021)

File.Name: l312a_l312-025.jpg
Notes:
51030144588 A few days ago, I posted this photo of what I guessed was a fungus skeletonizing a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) - but I wondered if it might be the work of a leaf-mining insect instead, and it is. I found another one of the blistered oak leaves today that was still intact. I opened it up and found the strange critter in my photo today that's been skeletonizing the leaf and leaving its dark frass (poop) - but what is it? I'm not even sure which end is which! My first thought was that it's a beetle larva, but a caterpillar seems more likely. It does look something like the leaf-miner caterpillar in this photo, at least it's got similar constrictions along its sides. That one mines leaves, but it makes tunnels rather than tents. One mystery solved but others remain! (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2021)

Today started sunny and calm, a chance to dry out after two days of good soaking rain. Then clouds started to move in, and we had a few small showers. Then around 4:00 in the afternoon, all hell broke out - thunder, lightning, and hail the size of pea-gravel! It didn't last long, but it was an exciting half-hour - and we picked up an additional 1/2 inch of rain!

File.Name: l312b_l312-007.jpg
Notes: 51030831976 Here is one of the caterpillars that spin webs and skeletonize leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in our woods this morning. It's in its nest with its horde of frass (poop), compare this photo from last week. I don't find these caterpillars out in the open very often, but I have found them before - try a search of my photos on the words "caterpillar web skeletonize". I don't know what kind of caterpillar this is, and I'm sure there is more than one kind that do this, see my [Next] photo for a different one. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2021)

File.Name: l312c_l312-040.jpg
Notes: 51030810496 This is another bristly fly (Diptera) on an oak leaf in the woods today. It's bristly enough to be another Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae), but I can't see any sign of a subscutellum which is said to be a sign of this family - see my [Previous] photo and this photo. Whatever it is, it's a handsome fly for . . . HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2021)

File.Name: l312d_l309-006.jpg
Notes: 51030793591 This is the bristly rear end of a fly (Diptera) on an oak leaf in the woods a few days ago. I believe it's a Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae because of all all the bristles, but it also has a subscutellum which BugGuide says is characteristic for the family. Sure, this photo would have been perfect for the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday group yesterday, but I figure it works just as well for Fly Day Friday - so HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2021)

File.Name: l313a_l313-006.jpg
Notes:
51034375212 This little spider looks like it's going for a ride in a boat-like seed capsule of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae)! It's a Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider that always has that odd handle on the back of its abdomen. I suspect most of our spiders in the woods lost their webs during our hail and rain yesterday, and I guess this one is in no hurry to rebuild. (San Marcos Pass, 13 March 2021)

I had an errand in town this morning, and by the time I got home after noon the wind was blowing with 25-35 mph gusts. That's not promising for new photos, but I went out anyway and got a few. The wind will hopefully calm down tomorrow, but then we have another 50% chance of a small rain on Sunday night and Monday. We ended up with 2.2 inches of rain from our recent storm, better than expected, but still only about 1/2 normal for the date.
CommonNames: Southern Bush Monkeyflower; Trashline Orb Weaver

File.Name: l313b_l313-011.jpg
Notes: 51034228091 Here is a small Planthopper (Fulgoroidea) on a dead leaf blade of Chaparral Yucca (Hesperoyucca whipplei, Agavaceae) in the woods today. (Hah - the leaf really is a blade!) I believe the insect is a Cixiid Planthopper in the family Cixiidae, possibly genus Cixius. Hah, that's about everything I know about this insect. Getting these photos every day is like casting a net into the sea and bringing in a fish. That tells you nothing about the creature except that it was here, today. The real challenge is to understand their lives! (San Marcos Pass, 13 March 2021)

File.Name: l313c_l312-036.jpg
Notes: 51034309052 This is a Common Sawfly (family Tenthredinidae, Hymenoptera) grooming its wings on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods yesterday. These wasps have no sign of a "wasp-waist" unlike most of their cousins. They are just starting to appear this year - at the same time that the oaks are forming catkins, see this photo. Sometimes the larvae of these wasps become pests and attack the new leaves and catkins of oak trees, see this photo. They don't seem to do this every year, so there's some mystery here. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2021)

File.Name: l313d_l312-017.jpg
Notes: 51033400108 Here are two more shots of the odd leaf-miner larva I show in my [Previous] photo (here). I believe now that it really is a caterpillar in the family of Leaf Blotch Miner Moths (Gracillariidae), see the comment by Tim Friedlander to my other photo. I believe that odd structure on the left of the caterpillar really is its head, like this illustration at Wikipedia. And I believe the black specs in its excavation are indeed frass (poop) and not fungus spores as I first thought in my comments to this photo last week. The photo on the left shows the critter's blister-like mine before I opened it up. The caterpillar itself is barely visible in the lower-left of its mine, I'll add a note. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2021)

File.Name: l314a_l314-024.jpg
Notes:
51037985166 Here is another golden Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) on a new leaflet of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the woods today. I believe this fly is species Syrphus opinator which is said to be common in central California. It was chilly and clouds were moving in, a good time to look for flies looking to sit out the weather. This one started grooming its face in preparation for taking off, and I think this photo shows it cleaning its proboscis. (San Marcos Pass, 14 March 2021)

Today was chilly and cloudy as another small storm moves in. We're under a NWS Winter Weather Advisory for possible snow tonight and a Wind Advisory for tomorrow. *Sigh* This storm will probably bring us a small rain followed by big winds.

File.Name: l314b_l314-006.jpg
Notes: 51038049947 This is a humpbacked Dance Fly (Empididae, Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today, probably genus Empis. They are small but not tiny, about 1/4 inch long. It's dusted with a trace of yellow pollen from the nearby willow catkins on its thorax - compare this photo to see what they do. It's my understanding that these flies are primarily predators, but I find them every year nectaring on the willow catkins. The willows are just about done now, so I guess they go back to being predators. (San Marcos Pass, 14 March 2021)

File.Name: l314c_l314-013.jpg
Notes: 51037175968 Here are two shots of a tiny wasp (Hymenoptera) that I believe is an actual Gall Wasp in the family Cynipidae - their shape is pretty distinctive. I found it on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today, and that leaf may become the galled host for its larvae. The wasp is tiny, just 410 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 1.7 mm - about 1/16 inch. (See here for how I figure.)
