I have hundreds of photos of different wasp galls but few of the wasps themselves, see this album and this photo of a different gall wasp that I got last week. (San Marcos Pass, 14 March 2021)

File.Name: l314d_l314-041.jpg
Notes: 51037870406 I believe this is a different leaf-miner caterpillar growing into its tunnel on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today - and that appears to be the actual caterpillar in the clear area at the end! It appears to be different from the caterpillars in this photo and this photo. The texture of the lower part of its tunnel is especially interesting, like lizard scales. I'm enjoying looking for different mines on the oak leaves - expect more photos as I find them. (San Marcos Pass, 14 March 2021)

File.Name: l315a_l315-004.jpg
Notes:
51041620711 This is another Dance Fly (Empididae, Diptera) on a staminate catkin of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) down by the creek today. Our willows are just about done flowering, though there are still a few fresh catkins like this one for the flies to find. It's my understanding that these flies are primarily predators, but I find them every year nectaring on the willow catkins. I guess they go back to being predators when the catkins are all done. (San Marcos Pass, 15 March 2021)

Small rain, big winds - that about wraps it up for today, though we also had moments of hail and even a snow flurry his afternoon. We had less than 1/4 inch of rain; the wind has been gusting to 40+ mph all day; and the temperature has been hovering above and below 40∞ - it feels cold with that wind chill. It was a pretty miserable day for new photos, but I bundled up and got a few. This shot of the Dance Fly was pure luck - but it's the kind of luck that comes when you take dozens of photos and find the one keeper.

File.Name: l315b_l315-007.jpg
Notes: 51041665657 Here are new galls on a leaf of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. I believe they are the galls of the Sage Leaf Gall Midge (Rhopalomyia audibertiae) fly in the family Cecidomyiidae, Diptera. This photo shows the bottom of a leaf, and the galls look different on the other side, see this photo. They are open on the end, with the opening initially filled with white hairs. I find similar galls on native White Sage (Salvia apiana), see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 15 March 2021)
CommonNames: Black Sage; Sage Leaf Gall Midge

File.Name: l315c_l315-005.jpg
Notes: 51041644822 Here is a succulent plant of native Southern California Dudleya (Dudleya lanceolata, Crassulaceae) growing out from under dead leaves at the base of a Chaparral Yucca (Hesperoyucca whipplei, Agavaceae) plant in the woods today. It's been like this for some time, see this photo from 2013. (San Marcos Pass, 15 March 2021)

File.Name: l315d_l315-001.jpg
Notes: 51041604712 This is the work of a leaf-mining caterpillar on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. Maybe it's the work of the odd caterpillar I showed in this photo a few days ago, but there are other caterpillars that do similar damage - see this photo. I think it looks like . . . an aerial view of an archeological ruin with walls and rooms?? The top layer of the leaf has been eaten, and in time it will be skeletonized all the way through. I thought at first that the little black specs were frass (poop), but on my computer screen they look more like places where the leaf is already worn through. In time, it will look something like this one. (San Marcos Pass, 15 March 2021)

File.Name: l316a_l316-039.jpg
Notes:
51044990787 Here is a Spiny Assassin Bug (genus Sinea, Reduviidae) looking down at me from a branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. This assassin was in the shade with bright light behind, but I like how it came out - see this photo for a more conventional view taken in a different tree. My photo today gives a good view of this predator's spike-like proboscis and the spines on its front legs that give it its name. They are gnarly-looking predators, though they are actually shy and retiring in my experience. (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2021)

Today was sunny but still chilly, in the low 50s. This wind was a moderate breeze from the south - a welcome change after our big winds from the north yesterday and last night. It was cold last night - especially for March - but I didn't see any ice this morning, it was probably mostly wind chill.
CommonNames: Spiny Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: l316b_l316-019.jpg
Notes: 51044950752 This is a Common Sawfly (family Tenthredinidae, Hymenoptera) perched on last year's seed husks of native Tall Brome (Bromus grandis) grass in the Poaceae plant family. These wasps have no sign of a "wasp-waist" unlike the much larger wasp in my [Previous] photo. Sometimes the larvae of these wasps become pests and attack the new leaves and catkins of oak trees, see this photo. They don't seem to do this every year, so there's some mystery here. (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2021)

File.Name: l316c_l316-031.jpg
Notes: 51044832131 This is a Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) laying flat on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I reckon this wasp was still feeling the chill from least night and really wanted to be left alone, but I bugged it enough getting a few photos that it finally flew away to a place a few feet away in the same small tree. This wasp has a petioled abdomen - like the stalk of a flower. This distinguishes it from the wasp in this photo which is probably a Golden Paper Wasp (Polistes aurifer) that lacks the petiole. They otherwise look quite similar. (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2021)
CommonNames: Western Paper Wasp; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: l316d_l316-012.jpg
Notes: 51044040848 Here are two shots of an odd little insect that I found walking along an oak leaf today - odd because it appears to be an aphid (Aphididae), but it's larger and gets around better than most aphids I encounter. It even seems to have cornicules aka siphunculi, those tube-like projections on its rear end, I'll make a note. It's tiny, about about 740 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.1 mm - about 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) Maybe one possibility is the Reddish Brown Willow Bark Aphid (Pterocomma bicolor). There's not much info about this species at BugGuide, but there is willow nearby and it has been collected in San Luis Obispo County just north of us. Any help with ID will be appreciated. (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2021)

File.Name: l317a_l317-001.jpg
Notes:
51047751821 This is an encounter between two different wasps on new leaves of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) on the wild edge of our garden this morning. There is a small Common Sawfly (family Tenthredinidae) on the right and a tiny wasp on the left that I can't identify. The wonder is that both are in reasonably good focus. Today was our shopping day, and I only had a short time to look for photos before777 leaving. I was looking for spider webs without luck, when I noticed a group of sawflies on the sunflower. The tiny wasp was just a blur when I got the photo, and it came out perfect! (San Marcos Pass, 17 March 2021)

Beer and other necessities were getting low again, so it was time to make our usual shopping run to the Santa Ynez Valley that we do every two weeks. I got this lucky shot before we left. Now we have another one of those @#&$ maintenance power outages tomorrow from 7:00 am to 3:00 pm. I should be able to post if they keep to that schedule. We've had way too many of these outages recently, the result of years of deferred maintenance coupled with a new sensitivity to liabilities.

File.Name: l317b_l316-017.jpg
Notes: 51047791197 Here is a little Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider that lost its web to the rain and hail earlier this week. In fact, it's the same little spider I showed looking like it's going for a boat ride in this photo a few days ago. It doesn't seem to be in any hurry to replace its web. There are basically no spider webs in he woods right now, and the spiders are scarce too - I hope they're all just moping like this one, and our hailstorm wasn't too hard on them. Darn, I haven't missed many Web Wednesdays since the group started. (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2021)

File.Name: l317c_l316-065.jpg
Notes: 51046951998 This is a different gall on a leaf bud of a tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It reminds me of the gall of a Live Oak Bud Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercusagrifoliae, Cynipidae), but there are differences - see this photo for a comparison of both kinds. The colors are different, it seems less hairy, and the way the bracts hold the gall like a claw and ball foot is also different. Both galls mature quickly and drop away. I'll try to keep an eye on this one as it matures, I'm sure it's something different. (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2021)

File.Name: l317d_l316-056.jpg
Notes: 51047652761 Here is another shot of the Spiny Assassin Bug (genus Sinea, Reduviidae) looking down at me from a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods yesterday - compare this photo of the same little predator. My photo today gives a good view of this predator's spike-like proboscis, and you can see the little recess on the front of its thorax that the tip of its proboscis fits into when traveling. I've read that they can produce a sound by rubbing their proboscis against ridges inside this groove, but I've never heard it. (Is this really true??) They are gnarly-looking predators, though they are actually shy and retiring in my experience. (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2021)
CommonNames: Spiny Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: l318a_l316-033.jpg
Notes:
51051072121 Here is a Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). This is the wasp that has its abdomen on a stalk or petiole, see this photo for another shot of the same wasp. And look how the wasp is cradling its abdomen with its wings! I've seen the wasps do this cradling before, see this photo and this photo and this photo. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2021)

Just two photos today, as we had another of those @#&$ maintenance power outages that was scheduled from 7:00 am to 3:00 pm - and ran until 8:00 instead. Bah, this is no way to run a business - or a public utility. Oh well, I had things to do in town anyway that took most of the day.
CommonNames: Western Paper Wasp; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: l318b_l312-044.jpg
Notes: 51050318718 This is the bristly rear end of the same fly (Diptera) on a oak leaf that I showed in this photo last Friday. It's bristly enough to be another Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae), but I can't see any sign of a subscutellum which is said to be a sign of this family - see this photo for an example. It's rear end looks like a polished airplane fuselage. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2021)

File.Name: l319a_l319-027.jpg
Notes:
51053774551 I found this handsome Cicada (Cicadidae) on leaves of Giant Rye Grass (Leymus condensatus, Poaceae) in a shady place today, and I moved it to a sunnier leaf of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) for a photo. It didn't seem to complain. It has the green colors I associate with being teneral - just emerged from a molt - though I couldn't find it's shed skin aka exuviae anywhere nearby. I believe this one is genus Platypedia. I don't think these early cicadas sing at all, they just make the clicking noise that I sometimes hear up in the oaks. This is the third of these handsome insects I've found this year, after this one and this one. That's more than I usually find. (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2021)

One year ago today on 19 March 2020, California Governor Gavin Newsom announced a statewide stay-at-home order to slow the spread of COVID-19. Now that's an order I can get behind, since we are fortunate to live in a fairly remote canyon with rich natural history and few neighbors, and I figure "stay at home" really means "stay in the home canyon" - and I have. I haven't been anywhere else since that original stay-at-home order except for the occasional run for beer and other necessities that we do every other week. I've been in it for the record - my year in the canyon, capturing new photos almost every day, and paying close attention to familiar plants and places which has helped me find things I wouldn't have noticed otherwise. I'll have more to say about this project at some point coming up, but I'll probably just go for another walk in the canyon tomorrow!
CommonNames: Cicada; Giant Rye Grass; Southern Bush Monkeyflower

File.Name: l319b_l316-050.jpg
Notes: 51053011823 Here are a group of Dance Flies (Empididae, Diptera) on a staminate catkin of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) down by the creek a few days ago. Our willows are just about done flowering, though there are still a few fresh catkins like this one for the flies to find. They were late appearing this year, and I guess they are trying to make up for it now. It's my understanding that these flies are primarily predators, but I find them every year nectaring on the willow catkins. I guess they go back to being predators when the catkins are all done. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2021)

File.Name: l319c_l316-024.jpg
Notes: 51053797772 Here are two shots of a tiny fly (Diptera) on a oak leaf a few days ago. This fly is about 720 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.0 mm - not quite 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) Tiny as it is, it looks just like a larger fly with all the same parts scaled down. I already have my two photos for the Fly Day Friday group, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2021)

File.Name: l319d_l314-025.jpg
Notes: 51052977218 This is a golden Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) on a new leaflet of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the woods. I believe this fly is species Syrphus opinator which is said to be common in central California. It was chilly and clouds were moving in, a good time to find flies looking to sit out the weather. This happens to be the same fly I showed in this photo on the day it was taken. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 14 March 2021)

File.Name: l320a_l320-011.jpg
Notes:
51057269841 Here is another tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) on a spine-tipped leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. The spider looks like one of the large bottlecap-sized Halloween spiders like in this photo with those horns on its abdomen, though it's much smaller - just 580 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.4 mm - about 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure. I usually figure the oak leaf spines are about 1 mm long as a handy ruler, and that proves to be about right.) I'm thinking this spider might be Araneus montereyensis with that noticeable dip between the horns, which would make it different from the very similar Araneus bispinosus in this photo without much dip - see Alice Abelia's comment on this photo. This is the first small spider I've found with an actual web since our hailstorm last week knocked them all down. (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2021)

Today is the vernal equinox and the first official day of spring. Happy equinox! The seasons change, life goes on. It's interesting that the last vernal equinox was on March 19, 2020 - the very day that California started a stay-at-home order to slow the spread of COVID-19, and I started to (mostly) stay at home in the canyon. What a long strange year its been!

File.Name: l320b_l320-017.jpg
Notes: 51057274447 This is looking into one of those aphid-induced "rolled leaf galls" on the edge of a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) that has fallen in the woods today, see this photo for a similar galled leaf that is still on the tree. The gall is caused by a colony of Woolly Oak Aphids in the genus Stegophylla of the Aphididae, and they stuff it full of their cottony wool. If you look close, you can see a few of the aphids moving along the top of the gall, I'll make a note. I've only come to appreciate these aphids during this last year of only getting photos in the canyon at home. (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2021)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Woolly Oak Aphids

File.Name: l320c_l319-011.jpg
Notes: 51057241917 This is a well-camouflaged caterpillar on the dead end of a stem of Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae) in the woods yesterday. It's small, maybe an inch long. It might well be the same caterpillar I showed in this photo and this photo last month - it looks similar, and it's on the same plant. I'm sure it's the larva of a Moth (Lepidoptera), but I don't know what kind - except that it has too many hind prolegs to be a "looper". (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2021)
CommonNames: caterpillar; Climbing Penstemon; Heart-leaved Keckiella; Moth

File.Name: l320d_l319-019.jpg
Notes: 51056415238 Here is one more shot of the handsome Cicada (Cicadidae) that I showed in my [Previous] photo (here). This time it's on my fingertip going for a ride as I moved it from a shady place into the sun for a photo. It has the green colors I associate with being teneral - just emerged from a molt - though I couldn't find it's shed skin aka exuviae anywhere nearby. I believe this one is genus Platypedia. (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2021)

File.Name: l321a_l321-004.jpg
Notes:
51060392498 This is looking close at a tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) in the woods today that has its web in a shrub of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae). It's probably the same spider I've shown before in this photo and this photo, though it's moved over a few feet if so. My photo today might be unnerving to some people, but the spider is really tiny, only about 1/8 inch long the last time I measured it. I believe this one is either Araneus bispinosus or Araneus montereyensis, probably the former, see Alice Abelia's comment on this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 21 March 2021)

It's been windy all day with gusts of 25-35 mph, and it sounds like we'll be in for a few more windy days this week coming up. There's nothing to do but grin and bear it . . . and bump up the ISO and take lots of photos to get a few keepers.

File.Name: l321b_l321-010.jpg
Notes: 51061014536 Here is one of the first flowers of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae) that I've seen in our home canyon - along with the first Crab Spider (Thomisidae) that has claimed it as its own. The little spiders like to sit by the center opening of the flowers to wait for predators, but this one sensed my presence and made a hasty exit. Try a search of my photos for "crab spider Prickly-Phlox" for lots more such photos. (San Marcos Pass, 21 March 2021)

File.Name: l321c_l321-014.jpg
Notes: 51060978476 Here are caterpillars of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on a stem of Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae) in the woods today. This is the same branch I showed a couple of weeks ago in this photo where a caterpillar layed down some webbing and shed its old skin. For some reason, it remains a popular spot for these social caterpillars. See this photo for a summer aggregation of the caterpillars. (San Marcos Pass, 21 March 2021)
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Climbing Penstemon; Heart-leaved Keckiella

File.Name: l321d_l320-020.jpg
Notes: 51061019827 This is one of those aphid-induced "rolled leaf galls" on the edge of a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) that has fallen in the woods, see this photo from yesterday for a closer shot. This one is crawling with the gray-colored Woolly Oak Aphids in the genus Stegophylla of the Aphididae that induce the gall and stuff it full of their cottony wool. It's interesting that the aphids come in two distinct sizes. Most of the ones visible here are the small sort, but there are a few larger ones visible too - I'll make a note and see this photo for a better shot. I've never seen ants tend these aphids, and I don't know what they do when their house falls down. (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2021)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Woolly Oak Aphids

File.Name: l322a_l322-006.jpg
Notes:
51064650571 Here is another one of the caterpillars that spin webs and skeletonize leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in our woods this morning. The caterpillar is in its nest with frass (poop) and a thin roof of webbing, and it looks like it's starting to dig into a new section of the leaf. I don't find these caterpillars out in the open very often, but I have found them before - try a search of my photos on the words "caterpillar web skeletonize". I don't know what kind of caterpillar this is, and I'm sure there is more than one kind that do this, see this photo for a different one. (San Marcos Pass, 22 March 2021)

It was pretty windy again today with 20-30 mph gusts for most of the day. Our NWS Wind Advisory will expire in the morning, but it sounds like the wind will continue. Like I've said before, there's nothing to do but grin and bear it . . . and bump up the ISO and take lots of photos to get a few keepers.

File.Name: l322b_l322-036.jpg
Notes: 51064707117 Here are two shots of a little Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider attacking its prey on a brand new web. It's probably the same spider I showed in this photo a few days ago after it lost its web in a hailstorm, but it has finally rebuilt - I'll have a photo for the Web Wednesdays group. It's interesting that in today's photo, the spider's prey looks like a segment of an old caterpillar's molt - not alive. The spider does look like it's having fun with it though! (San Marcos Pass, 22 March 2021)

File.Name: l322c_l322-015.jpg
Notes: 51064685672 I found this little Beetle (Coleoptera) on last year's woolly stem of of native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I'm pretty sure that it's a Click Beetle in the family Elateridae. These beetles have their thorax drawn out to points that can *click* against the wing covers - sometimes quite dramatically. This is the first one I've seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 22 March 2021)

File.Name: l322d_l322-017.jpg
Notes: 51064604047 This is the tunnel of a tiny leaf-miner caterpillar - like the one in this photo except that it's on a leaf of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) rather than an oak leaf. Like in that other photo, it shows the whole life-history of the caterpillar from a small start to a big finish. If you look close at the big end of the tunnel, you can see a little flap where the larva got away. Hah, a couple of years ago I happened to be in just the right place at just the right time to catch something similar emerging from an oak leaf, see this photo. There is a photo on plate 59.3 of my big book of moths that lead me to species Stigmella diffasciae in the family Nepticulidae which mines leaves on Coffeeberry. I can't vouch for the moth's identity, but I like these leaf mines - they are like leaf tattoos! (San Marcos Pass, 22 March 2021)

File.Name: l323a_l323-003.jpg
Notes:
51067592532 Here is another mating pair of Hammock Spiders (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae) under a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. That's the female on the right, and the male down under. It was a difficult photo situation with bright sunlight and shade and wind - I prefer not to use flash, and I did what I could. See this photo to understand what the spiders are doing with each other. I don't know why I find so many of this particular kind of spider doing what they are doing, but I do - try a search of all my photos on "mating hammock spider". It's almost Wednesday, so Happy Hump Day! (San Marcos Pass, 23 March 2021)

Wind, wind, wind . . . we've had 25-35 mph winds from the north all day long. Like I've said before, there's nothing to do but grin and bear it . . . and bump up the ISO and take lots of photos to get a few keepers.

File.Name: l323b_l323-012.jpg
Notes: 51067559722 This is looking up at a Green Stink Bug in the family Pentatomidae in an oak tree in the woods today. If you look close below its eye, you can see that the bug has its odd mouthparts engaged, apparently feeding on the tree twig. I find this a confusing group, but this one might be the Western Red-shouldered Stink Bug (Thyanta pallidovirens), compare this photo. Sometimes they look completely brown, and sometimes they actually change color like this one is doing. (San Marcos Pass, 23 March 2021)

File.Name: l323c_l323-009.jpg
Notes: 51067547757 Here is another little Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae) hiding in a cluster of new leaves of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). Those two little appendages ("cerci") on its rear end are distinctive. These small crickets have been rather scarce this year, though I've found a few. (San Marcos Pass, 23 March 2021)
CommonNames: Chaparral Camel Cricket; Southern Bush Monkeyflower

File.Name: l323d_l323-023.jpg
Notes: 51067439206 This is another shot of the different gall on a leaf bud of a tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) that I showed in this photo a week ago. It's been growing, and it looks like it's being squeezed out of the bud. It was actually a struggle to get this photo today because of the wind, but I felt like I better get it soon because it might not be here tomorrow. It reminds me of the gall of a Live Oak Bud Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercusagrifoliae, Cynipidae), but there are differences - see this photo for a comparison of both kinds. The color is different, it has longer hairs, and the way the bracts hold the gall like a claw and ball foot is different. Both galls mature quickly and drop away into the oak mulch below. (San Marcos Pass, 23 March 2021)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Live Oak Bud Gall Wasp

File.Name: l324a_l324-014.jpg
Notes:
51069807871 Here is a Hammock Spider (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae) hanging under its sheet web with a Common Sawfly (family Tenthredinidae) for prey. In fact, this is the same female spider that I showed mating in this photo yesterday, though the male was nowhere to be seen. Hah, this spider was bouncing up and down in the wind today, and the photo came out better than I expected. (San Marcos Pass, 24 March 2021)

I has a busy day with things to do in the valley in the morning, and in town in the afternoon, but in between I had a good hour to get a few photos at home. I was fighting a breeze again, but I got some decent photos. Breezy conditions are expected tomorrow as well. The good news is that we got our second shots of the COVID vaccine while we were in town this afternoon!

File.Name: l324b_l324-018.jpg
Notes: 51069786776 Here is a little ant (Formicidae) slowly picking its way across a spider web in the woods today. It's the web of another Hammock Spider (Pityohyphantes) in the Sheet Web Weaver (Linyphiidae) family. The ant almost bumped into the spider as it trudged along, and the spider moved away showing no interest in the ant. I guess the spider has no appetite for ants, and the web has no sticky stuff on it. I already have my two photos for the Web Wednesday group - but Happy Web Webnesday anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 24 March 2021)

File.Name: l324c_l324-003.jpg
Notes: 51000586850 This is another shot of the little Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider in my [Previous] photo taken today - on its web that is brand new again. First, the spider's web got knocked down by a hailstorm last week. The spider moped for a few day, then rebuilt, and I got my [Previous] photo two days ago. Then the spider's web got knocked down again by the wind that night, and the spider spent yesterday moping. I got this photo today of the spider on it's still-new web that's still devoid of the usual "trash" these spiders accumulate as the decorate their webs with the body parts of their prey, pirate fashion - like this. I hope this web lasts for a while. It's interesting that in my previous photo, taken from the same place, the spider is on the other side of the web. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 24 March 2021)

File.Name: l324d_l322-028.jpg
Notes: 51000571930 This is a little Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider on its brand new web a few days ago, still devoid of the usual "trash" these spiders accumulate as the decorate their webs with the body parts of their prey, pirate fashion - like this. It's the same spider I showed in this photo two days ago and probably the same spider in this photo after it lost its web in a hailstorm. The spider rebuilt, only to have its web knocked down by the wind yesterday. Then it rebuilt its web again, see my [Next] photo taken today. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 22 March 2021)

File.Name: l325a_l325-005.jpg
Notes:
51072308151 Here is a Green Stink Bug in the family Pentatomidae in a plant of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the woods today. The vine is growing in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae), and the bug is probably the same one I showed in this photo a couple of days ago in the same tree - the bug just moved over a couple of feet to the different plant. This bug might be the Western Red-shouldered Stink Bug (Thyanta pallidovirens), but it's a confusing group and there are other possibilities. (San Marcos Pass, 25 March 2021)

Wind, wind, wind . . . and more wind! We started today in a cloud with some drizzle, but then it cleared up and the north winds kicked up with 25-40 mph gusts so far. Our newest Wind Advisory expires in the middle of the night, so I hope tomorrow will be calm.
CommonNames: Wild Sweetpea; Coast Live Oak; Western Red-shouldered Stink Bug

File.Name: l325b_l325-007.jpg
Notes: 51003068965 This is another leaf-miner caterpillar at the growing end of its tunnel in a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. It appears to be the same kind of leaf miner that I showed in this photo last week, and it's close to that other one, just a few inches away on a different leaf of the same tree. The texture of the lower part of their tunnels is especially interesting, and I wonder if it's filled with dark frass (poop). That's the actual caterpillar in the clear area at the end of its mine. It appears to be different from the caterpillars in this photo and this photo, though maybe it has a similar head on the large end? One possibility is genus Stigmella in the moth family Nepticulidae - though there are other leaf miners. I'll keep looking for them. (San Marcos Pass, 25 March 2021)

File.Name: l325c_l325-012.jpg
Notes: 51003052375 Here is a little ant (Formicidae) looking for nectar under a swollen pistil of non-native succulent Jade Plant (Crassula ovata, Crassulaceae) in the garden today. One good thing I can say about this plant is that it doesn't move around much on a windy day like today. This photo shows the ant's rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 25 March 2021)

File.Name: l325d_l322-029.jpg
Notes: 51072226541 This is another shot of a little Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider hurrying along its web to catch its "prey" - see this photo of the same spider to see what it was! These spiders always have that odd handle on the back of its abdomen. This is a nice action shot of the spider's rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 22 March 2021)

File.Name: l326a_l326-020.jpg
Notes:
51074103513 This is another tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) tending its web in a shrub of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae) in the woods today. It's probably the same spider I've shown before here, here and here - and why not when it has such great poses for me! The spider really is tiny, only about 1/8 inch long the last time I measured it. I believe this one is either Araneus bispinosus or Araneus montereyensis, probably the former, see Alice Abelia's comment on this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2021)

The big winds did back off today, though there was the usual breeze from the south. We did get some rain at the start of the month, but then it turned dry - no March Miracle for us this time. We stand at just 42% of normal rainfall for the year. By my reckoning, this is the third driest year (to date) after 2014 and 2007. There's more info in the new post at Daniel Swain's California Weather Blog. It's going to be an interesting summer.

File.Name: l326b_l326-012.jpg
Notes: 51005584255 Here are two shots showing the work of a different leaf-miner caterpillar both above and below a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. The caterpillar is skeletonizing the leaf and turning the leaf material into frass. It's interesting that it has covered its work area on the bottom of the leaf with a clear material like plastic food wrap. But compare the caterpillar in this photo which covers its work with a translucent layer of material on the top of the leaf. I can't see the caterpillar in today's photos, but I'll keep an eye on the leaf. These leaf miners are more complicated than I thought at first, and I'll keep looking for different ones. (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2021)

File.Name: l326c_l325-017.jpg
Notes: 51074051928 Here is another golden Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) on a succulent leaf of non-native Jade Plant (Crassula ovata, Crassulaceae) in the garden yesterday. This fly might be species Syrphus opinator which is said to be common in central California, though it seems a little less golden than some others I've been showing lately like this one, so it might be something else like the genus Eupeodes. Whatever it is, it's a handsome fly. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 25 March 2021)

File.Name: l326d_l324-006.jpg
Notes: 51074833102 This is a tiny Non-biting Midge Fly (Chironomidae) tangled up in a spider's orb web. This fly is male with those feathery antennae and slender body. This might be one of the flies that dance in the sunbeams like in this photo, but I really haven't been seeing them lately. It's tiny, just 580 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.7 mm - about 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure. It was still struggling in its captive web, but I left it to its fate. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 24 March 2021)

File.Name: l327a_l327-019.jpg
Notes:
51077562032 This is a Crab Spider (Thomisidae) with a wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) for prey on a new flower of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae) in the woods today. I believe the spider is in the genus Mecaphesa with hairy legs and abdomen, a female with slender palps. It's the kind of spider I expect to find waiting on flowers, and maybe it's the same spider I showed on the same plant a week ago in this photo. I have a hard time getting my exposure right with these particular flowers. My photos always seem too dark so I increase the exposure - and overexpose every time. There's something odd about the light. (San Marcos Pass, 27 March 2021)

It was noticeably warmer today, into the low-70s. There was a mild breeze from the south all morning, and its picking up into a wind from the north this evening. It sounds like it will warm up considerably this week.

File.Name: l327b_l327-009.jpg
Notes: 51076734553 I was staring right at this dead leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) for some time before I realized what I was looking at today - it's the rear end of a Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae) sticking out of the leaf! The two little appendages ("cerci") on its rear end are distinctive, even when most of the cricket is hidden like this one. I believe these crickets work the night shift and try to hide during the day, but they do it "Ostrich style" in the belief that if they can't see you then you can't see them - and it almost worked this time! I've been thinking that these small crickets are rather scarce this year, but I wonder how many I've missed when they are hiding in plain sight right in front of me. (San Marcos Pass, 27 March 2021)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Chaparral Camel Cricket

File.Name: l327c_l327-015.jpg
Notes: 51008214620 Here are old galls of the Spiny Leaf Gall Wasp (Diplolepis polita) growing on what used to be foliage of native California Wild Rose (Rosa californica, Rosaceae) in the woods today - see this photo of the galls last spring when hey were fresh. I was about to delete this photo because I don't much like the focus, when I noticed something in the 5:00 position which is not a gall - it's the rear end of a Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae)! (I'll make a note.) The two little appendages ("cerci") on its rear end are distinctive, even in a distant shot like this one. I believe these crickets work the night shift and try to hide during the day, but they do it "Ostrich style" in the belief that if they can't see you then you can't see them - and it almost worked this time! (San Marcos Pass, 27 March 2021)
CommonNames: Spiny Leaf Gall Wasp; California Wild Rose; Chaparral Camel Cricket

File.Name: l327d_l326-003.jpg
Notes: 51076664133 Here are two shots of maybe the tiniest Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) I've ever tried to photograph. I got these photos on different days, both in the cactus patch of our garden, and I'm pretty sure they show the same spider. It's got a proper orb web, a few inches across. But the spider is tiny, only about just 400 across long in the 1:1 macro shot on the right, which comes to 1.7 mm - about 1/16 inch. (See here for how I figure.) Maybe it's genus Hypsosinga for its size as much as anything else? Or a juvenile of something else - I've been tricked before, see this photo, and read comments all the way to Ash's. Despite its tiny size, it's an attractive spider. (San Marcos Pass, 24 March 2021)

File.Name: l328a_l328-015.jpg
Notes:
51079594463 Here is something I haven't noticed before in the central opening of a new flower of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae) in the woods today - a small horde of tiny insects is spilling out! They appear to be tiny Thrips (Thysanoptera) - somewhat like the Predatory Thrips in the family Aeolothripidae that I sometimes find on manzanita flowers, see this photo for a whole lot of them a few years ago. They really are tiny, about 350 pixels across in another 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 1.5 mm, about 1/16 inch (See here for how I figured.) The thrips seemed to be rolling around in the pollen and sometimes coming out to breathe. I only saw them on this one flower, and they kept going back inside. See this photo for a look at what's inside these flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 28 March 2021)

Yipes, it was just shy of 80 degrees on our upstairs deck today - if it's this warm now, what will it be like in July?? I have to admit that the warmth was pleasant. There were no big winds today, but the breeze from the south was enough to be annoying.

File.Name: l328b_l328-014.jpg
Notes: 51080361332 This is another Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on a new flower of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae) in the woods today. I believe the spider is in the genus Mecaphesa with hairy legs and abdomen, a female with slender palps - probably the same spider I showed yesterday in this photo. I don't know why it seems to be grasping the flower petal between its chelicerae and palps. (San Marcos Pass, 28 March 2021)

File.Name: l328c_l328-011.jpg
Notes: 51011055080 Here is a Hammock Spider (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae) on the left hanging under its sheet web waiting for the moment to make its move on the Common Sawfly (family Tenthredinidae) on he right. The sawfly was still struggling, and I'm really not sure what the spider was waiting for. Behind the spider on the left, there are the little scale-like larvae or pupae of a Whitefly (Aleyrodidae, see this photo for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 28 March 2021)

File.Name: l328d_l327-029.jpg
Notes: 51080314872 This is another shot of my favorite little Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider on what's left of its web after the wind mostly tore it apart two nights ago. This is the same spider I've been showing lately e.g. in this photo and this photo as it rebuilds its web again and again. At least this time it managed to save a piece of the "trash line" that these spiders accumulate as the decorate their webs with the body parts of their prey, pirate fashion. (San Marcos Pass, 27 March 2021)

File.Name: l329a_l329-010.jpg
Notes:
51082826157 Here is a small Harvestman (Opiliones) "spider" on a dead leaf blade of Giant Rye Grass (Leymus condensatus, Poaceae). I suspect that the more you know about spiders, the stranger you will find these creatues! More properly, it's not a spider at all but an arachnid. It does have eight legs, but it has just one body part and two eyes. BugGuide says that they are more closely related to scorpions. I don't find then very often at home, but they seem more common in Oregon. (San Marcos Pass, 29 March 2021)

It was cooler today, but still in the mid-70s. I had something to do in the morning, and by the time I got out into the woods, the north winds were starting to pick up. Our wind advisory expires in the middle of the night, and tomorrow might be calmer.

File.Name: l329b_l329-019.jpg
Notes: 51013531245 A balcony scene for spiders? Here are a pair of Hammock Spiders (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae) in a small oak tree in the woods today - the female with slender palps on the left and the male with more dramatic palps on the right. They're not "doing it" at the moment, but I suspect they have been and will continue. I don't know why I find so many of this particular kind of spider doing what they are doing, but I do - try a search of all my photos on "mating hammock spider". (San Marcos Pass, 29 March 2021)

File.Name: l329c_l329-016.jpg
Notes: 51082789237 These aren't the best photos, but they show an interesting insect - a Lace Bug in the family Tingidae on a dead leaf blade of Giant Rye Grass (Leymus condensatus, Poaceae). I mostly find different species on different plants - this one on Wild Sweetpea, this one on Yerba Santa, this one on Caterpillar Phacelia, and this one on Greenbark Ceanothus. I've never noticed one before on a grass plant. They all have a remarkable net-like lattice-work on their body that reminds me of shogun-era samurai armor - but it also looks like bubble-wrap! (San Marcos Pass, 29 March 2021)

File.Name: l329d_l329-007.jpg
Notes: 50997749254 This is another tunnel of a leaf-miner caterpillar meandering through a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today - and I believe it shows the actual caterpillar responsible at the large end of its tunnel. You can follow the path of the caterpillar's life from a small start to a big finish, growing as it moves along. There is a photo on plate 59.3 of my Moths of Western North America book that leads me to the moth species Stigmella variella in the family Nepticulidae which mines leaves on Coast Live Oak. Alice Abelia has a photo of an adult that may be this species at BugGuide. I can't vouch for the moth's identity, but I like the patterns they make as they live their lives, see this photo for an assortment. (San Marcos Pass, 29 March 2021)

File.Name: l330a_l330-023.jpg
Notes:
51015823170 This is a tiny male Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. At least it's most of the spider. It's missing its two long front legs on the screen-left side and its two short rear legs on the screen-right side, but it managed to get around just fine and even ballooned away while I was watching. Life is resilient. (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2021)

Just two photos today, as I'm distracted by something I have to do tomorrow. It was a pleasant day, and I did get a few more decent photos for another day.

File.Name: l330b_l330-017.jpg
Notes: 51084986356 I found this nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae) on a leaflet of native Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae) in the woods today. It can't be much more than 1/8 inch long - without those long antennae of course. This is the first one I've noticed this year. I guess this is the nymph of a Angle-winged Katydid (Microcentrum californicum). We also have Bush Katydids in the genus Scudderia, but those nymphs appear later in the summer and are much more ornate, compare this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2021)
CommonNames: Canyon Lupine; Broadleaf Lupine; Angle-winged Katydid

File.Name: l501a_l501-001.jpg
Notes:
51152774390 This is a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly on leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2021)

I had a little stroke. I kind of forgot how to talk, though I expect it to come back with some help by the speech therapists. Fortunately I didn't forget how to take photos - though it taking longer than usual to write comments. And fortunately I didn't forget my sense of humor!

File.Name: l501b_l430-004.jpg
Notes:
51152638140 Here is a spider with its prey, a Six-spotted Orb Weaver (Araniella displicata) spider. It might be a Gnaphosidae. (San Marcos Pass, 30 April 2021)

I had a little stroke. I kind of forgot how to talk, though I expect it to come back with some help by the speech therapists. Fortunately I didn't forget how to take photos - though it taking longer than usual to write comments. And fortunately I didn't forget my sense of humor!

File.Name: l502a_l417-026.jpg
Notes:
51153398667 Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly ovipositing on unopened native Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae) in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2021)

These were the first photos I took after getting out of the hospital. Right now it is a major task for me to write.
CommonNames: Silvery Blue; Canyon Lupine; Broadleaf Lupine

File.Name: l502b_l417-045.jpg
Notes: 51154799774 Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly on native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2021)

File.Name: l503a_l424-013.jpg
Notes: 51155723252 This is another little moth (Lepidoptera) on a leaflet of Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae). It's small, about 3/16 inch long, with striking metallic copper and silver colors. I believe it is a Tortricid Moth in the family Tortricidae, maybe genus Grapholita and species Grapholita lunatana. (San Marcos Pass, 24 April 2021)
CommonNames: Canyon Lupine; Broadleaf Lupine; Tortricid Moth

File.Name: l503b_l422-038.jpg
Notes: 51157150484 This is a Western Tussock Moth (Orgyia vetusta, Lymantriinae, Lepidoptera) on a catkin of Coast Live Oak in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 22 April 2021)

File.Name: l504a_l504-005.jpg
Notes: 51158540911 This a emerging two-winged Gall Midge (Cecidomyiidae, Diptera) coming out of its gall on native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae). There's is a pupal husk, and it may well be that the fly just emerged from the gall leaving its old pupal skin behind and is now hardening up. I suspect this is something rarely seen. (San Marcos Pass, 4 May 2021)

File.Name: l504b_l504-010.jpg
Notes: 51157824102 Here are two shots of a spring gall of the Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis, Cynipidae) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). They are about 1/4 inch high and broad, shaped like little mushrooms or golf tees. These are bisexual galls because they will give rise to both male and female wasps that will mate and produce the summer unisexual oak apple galls in this photo which contain only female wasps. Those ladies will emerge next spring and produce another crop of bisexual galls like the one in my photo, and the cycle of alternating generations repeats. (San Marcos Pass, 4 May 2021)
CommonNames: Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: l505a_l505-011.jpg
Notes:
51159793517 Here a Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family on a leaf of Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I believe this is the species Pselliopus spinicollis in the subfamily Harpactorinae. The spines and striped legs are remarkable. This little predator is quite small, less than 1/2 inch long. I don't find them very often. (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2021)

One of those @#&$ maintenance power outages from 8:00 am to 8:00 pm.

File.Name: l505b_l505-019.jpg
Notes: 51160415526 Tiny tangled threads under a leaf of Bush Monkeyflower in the canyon. It appears to be an egg deposit, I guess it is a spider? (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2021)

File.Name: l507a_l506-012.jpg
Notes: 51164342861 This remarkable fly on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. Those oversize antennae are remarkable. The marking on thorax reminded of a Stiletto Fly in the family Therevidae, and I found it - genus Nebritus and maybe species Nebritus pellucidus. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2021)

File.Name: l507b_l425-011.jpg
Notes: 51165429820 This shot of a big-eyed male March Fly (family Bibionidae, order Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. I believe this is the genus Bibio, probably Bibio albipennis with those long white hairs. ("Albipennis" is Latin for "white feathers", and those hairs are kind of feather-like.) It's eyes are remarkable - and hairy. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2021)

File.Name: l508a_l508-012.jpg
Notes: 51166673048 This is a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly on leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 8 May 2021)

File.Name: l508b_l508-023.jpg
Notes: 51166656753 It's the egg of a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly. I was put on the right track by recent work by Dom Greves showing White Admiral butterfly eggs in the UK, see this photo - Admirals and Sisters are both in the same Limenitidinae subfamily, and both have eggs like tiny geodesic domes. Remarkable! (San Marcos Pass, 8 May 2021)

File.Name: l509a_l506-024.jpg
Notes: 51168173772 This is a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). These butterflies are starting to appear, but I'm not expecting them to have a great year. (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2021)

File.Name: l509b_l506-022.jpg
Notes: 51169918720 This is a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). These butterflies are starting to appear, but I'm not expecting them to have a great year. (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2021)

File.Name: l510a_l510-006.jpg
Notes: 51171174311 This is a little Tephritid Fruit Fly (Tephritidae, Diptera) on a cabbage-like flower bud of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae). The fly is female, and I can clearly see her ovipositor in other photos. One possibility for ID is the Sunflower Seed Maggot (Neotephritis finalis), see BugGuide. (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2021)
CommonNames: Tephritid Fruit Fly; Canyon Sunflower; Sunflower Seed Maggot

File.Name: l510b_l510-017.jpg
Notes: 51171153746 Here is the shed skin aka exuviae on a Cicada (Cicadidae). I believe this one is genus Platypedia. I don't think these early cicadas sing at all, they just make the clicking noise that I sometimes hear up in the oaks. (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2021)

File.Name: l511a_l511-023.jpg
Notes: 51172599107 I found this small Western Lynx Spider (Oxyopes scalaris, Oxyopidae) on a flowering plant of native Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the woods today. Its heavily spined legs give it away as a Lynx Spider, related to the more familiar Green Lynx Spider in this photo, but this one is much smaller. (San Marcos Pass, 11 May 2021)
CommonNames: Western Lynx Spider; Southern Bush Monkeyflower

File.Name: l511b_l511-003.jpg
Notes: 51173238391 These little Tephritid Fruit Flies (Tephritidae, Diptera) are mating on a cabbage-like flower bud of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae). The ovipositor of the female fly is clearly visible. One possibility for ID is the Sunflower Seed Maggot (Neotephritis finalis), see BugGuide. It's almost Wednesday, so Happy Hump Day! (San Marcos Pass, 11 May 2021)

File.Name: l512a_l512-023.jpg
Notes: 51175445613 I found this little "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a leaf of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) on the wild edge of our garden this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2021)

File.Name: l512b_l512-001.jpg
Notes: 51176303800 This is a looking up at a fabulous glandular flower head of native Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the Lamiaceae plant family. The flower heads seem unusually fat and full of flowers this year. (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2021)

File.Name: l513a_l513-018.jpg
Notes: 51178172954 This is a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today with a "caterpillar" inside. It is actually a Common Sawfly (family Tenthredinidae, Hymenoptera) larva, and it is taking a dump! This is looking into one of those aphid-induced "rolled leaf galls". HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 13 May 2021)

File.Name: l513b_l429-01.jpg
Notes: 51177584238 I found this nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae) on a leaflet of native Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the woods. I guess this is the nymph of a Angle-winged Katydid (Microcentrum californicum). HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 29 April 2021)

File.Name: l514a_l511-007.jpg
Notes: 51178668267 This is a tiny black fly on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Maybe it is a Leaf Miner Fly in the family Agromyzidae - thanks to Roger Thomasom. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 11 May 2021)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Leaf Miner Fly

File.Name: l514b_l502-021.jpg
Notes: 51180117574 This is a big-headed fly Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the woods. I believe this is a Big-headed Fly in the Pipunculidae family. What a strange little fly! HFDF. (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2021)

File.Name: l515a_l515-010.jpg
Notes: 51182556735 This is a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today with a "caterpillar" inside. It is actually a Common Sawfly (family Tenthredinidae, Hymenoptera) larva. One difference is the single eye. (San Marcos Pass, 15 May 2021)

File.Name: l515b_l515-048.jpg
Notes: 51180768772 This is a wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) in the throat of a new Pitcher Sage (Lepechinia calycina, Lamiaceae). (San Marcos Pass, 15 May 2021)

File.Name: l516a_l516-022.jpg
Notes: 51184671409 This caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) is on a branch of Nuttall's Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis, Caprifoliaceae) covered with water droplets from fog. It's peculiar because this caterpillar is only these caterpillars on "scrophs" - plants that were once placed in the Scrophulariaceae plant family like Climbing Penstemon and Bush Monkey Flower - but here they are on a plant in the mint family. (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2021)

File.Name: l516b_l516-015.jpg
Notes: 51184654844 Here are beautiful new flowers of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family in the woods today. The flowers were dancing around in the wind today, and I got lucky with this shot - nice lighting, if not quite sharp. This deciduous shrub has everything - gorgeous white flowers like these, edible nuts (here), and pretty autumn leaves (here). The flowers have a strong, sweet fragrance like perfume. For the few weeks that they flower, there is no finer flower in our mountains. They are pretty common here on San Marcos Pass - both in woodland (like these) and chaparral. They are stunning flowers, worth hunting for. (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2021)

File.Name: l517a_l517-013.jpg
Notes: 51186253376 I'm pretty sure this caterpillar-like creature is the larva of a Cimbicid Sawfly (Cimbicidae) in the order Hymenoptera, maybe the species Abia americana. There a vine of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae) and I've found the larvae before on this plant - see this photo. There sawfly larvae always have more prolegs - six or more pairs - while caterpillars have five pairs or fewer. (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2021)

File.Name: l517b_l515-021.jpg
Notes: 51185516397 This tiny Western Tussock Moth (Orgyia vetusta, Lymantriinae, Lepidoptera) on a Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae). Happy Moth Monday! (San Marcos Pass, 15 May 2021)
CommonNames: Western Tussock Moth; Canyon Lupine; Broadleaf Lupine

File.Name: l518a_l518-013.jpg
Notes: 51187628002 These little Tephritid Fruit Flies (Tephritidae, Diptera) are mating on native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae). One possibility for ID is the Sunflower Seed Maggot (Neotephritis finalis), see BugGuide. It's almost Wednesday, so Happy Hump Day! (San Marcos Pass, 18 May 2021)

File.Name: l518b_l514-006.jpg
Notes: 51189084884 Here are mating Weevils (Curculionidae, Coleoptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). There are many different kinds of these weevils, but maybe this one is species Thricolepis inornata. It's cute - the scales on its body almost look like feathers up close. It's almost Wednesday, so Happy Hump Day! (San Marcos Pass, 14 May 2021)

File.Name: l519a_l519-014.jpg
Notes: 51189728902 This is a little "looper caterpillar" of a Geometrid Moth (Geometridae, Lepidoptera) on a leaf of Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) Like all of these caterpillars, it stands on a platform of two pairs of hind prolegs with three pairs of true legs in front, and walks by looping along. (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2021)

File.Name: l519b_l514-026.jpg
Notes: 51191478570 This is a small spider web on a Coast Live Oak, but I'm not sure of the spider's identification. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 14 May 2021)

File.Name: l520a_l520-019.jpg
Notes: 51192398876 Here are an Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly working flowers of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) in the woods today. The first of these butterflies that of this the I've seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 20 May 2021)

File.Name: l520b_l519-023.jpg
Notes: 51192597993 This another Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly ovipositing on unopened of Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae). HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2021)

File.Name: l521a_l521-019.jpg
Notes: 51194316146 This is a little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) getting a drink from the sandstone rock in the water tub we keep by the summer-dry creek. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 21 May 2021)

File.Name: l521b_l519-006.jpg
Notes: 51195355700 This bristly fly (Diptera) on a oak leaf. It's bristly enough to be another Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae). HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2021)

File.Name: l522a_l522-001.jpg
Notes: 51196586018 This is a tiny male Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae). It's missing two of its legs, but it managed to get around just fine. Life is resilient. (San Marcos Pass, 22 May 2021)

File.Name: l522b_l520-027.jpg
Notes: 51196564783 Here is a Pacific Azure aka Echo Azure (Celastrina echo) butterfly for of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae). These butterflies are distinctive in having no distinctive markings below even when fresh. I believe these butterflies have spring and summer broods, and I'm not sure if this is a late early one or an early late one. (San Marcos Pass, 20 May 2021)
CommonNames: Pacific Azure; Echo Azure; Canyon Sunflower

File.Name: l523a_l523-012.jpg
Notes: 51199516634 I found this nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae) on a leaflet of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I believe it is the nymph of a Bush Katydid in the genus Scudderia, likely Scudderia mexicana. (San Marcos Pass, 23 May 2021)

File.Name: l523b_l523-009.jpg
Notes: 51198933033 This is another a little "looper caterpillar" of a Geometrid Moth (Geometridae, Lepidoptera) on a leaf of Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) in the woods today. Like all of these caterpillars, it stands on a platform of two pairs of hind prolegs with three pairs of true legs in front, and walks by looping along. (San Marcos Pass, 23 May 2021)

File.Name: l524a_l524-028.jpg
Notes: 51200550307 I'm pretty sure this is a small Cuckoo Wasp (Family Chrysididae) in the order Hymenoptera, maybe in the genus Chrysis. I believe their larvae are raised in the nests of other wasps and bees, hence the common name. (San Marcos Pass, 24 May 2021)

File.Name: l524b_l524-002.jpg
Notes: 51201238161 The beetles are Buprestid Beetles aka Metallic Wood-boring Beetles (Buprestidae, Coleoptera), probably Spotted Flower Buprestids in the genus Acmaeodera. They are visiting Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae). (San Marcos Pass, 24 May 2021)

File.Name: l525a_l525-031.jpg
Notes: 51202688682 This is a goofy little Chaparral Monkey Grasshopper (Morsea californica) in the Eumastacidae family of the Orthoptera in a plant of native Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae) in the woods today. They is really tiny. (San Marcos Pass, 25 May 2021)
CommonNames: Chaparral Monkey Grasshopper; Canyon Lupine; Broadleaf Lupine

File.Name: l525b_l524-004.jpg
Notes: 51204443900 The are mating beetles are Buprestid Beetles aka Metallic Wood-boring Beetles (Buprestidae, Coleoptera), probably Spotted Flower Buprestids in the genus Acmaeodera. They are visiting Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae. It's almost Wednesday, so Happy Hump Day! (San Marcos Pass, 24 May 2021)

File.Name: l526a_l526-025.jpg
Notes: 51204837777 This cute little spider is a female of the species Thiodina hespera aka Colonus hesperus on an oak leaf in the woods. The males have considerably more black on the head, see this photo. This is one Jumping Spider (Salticidae) that you can recognize even without seeing its face with those conspicuous dark areas around its eyes. Many jumping spiders look alike, but this one is distinctive. (San Marcos Pass, 26 May 2021)

File.Name: l526b_l526-003.jpg
Notes: 51204821747 I found this small male Western Lynx Spider (Oxyopes scalaris, Oxyopidae) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Look at those swollen pedipalps! (San Marcos Pass, 26 May 2021)
CommonNames: Western Lynx Spider; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: l527a_l527-012.jpg
Notes: 51208611210 This tiny caterpiller hatched from an egg of a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly. It is sitting next to what remains of its egg that originally looked like a tiny geodesic dome, see this photo from two weeks ago. (San Marcos Pass, 27 May 2021)

File.Name: l527b_l525-046.jpg
Notes: 51208582055 This bristly fly (Diptera) is no doubt another Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae). HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 25 May 2021)

File.Name: l528a_l528-024.jpg
Notes: 51210339729 The marking on the thorax reminds me of a Stiletto Fly in the family Therevidae, and I found it is genus Nebritus and maybe species Nebritus pellucidus. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 28 May 2021)

File.Name: l528b_l527-020.jpg
Notes: 51209759053 I believe it is a Stiletto Fly in the family Therevidae by the creek, maybe the species Thereva fucata, see this BugGuide info page. I've seen these handsome flies before, but not often. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 27 May 2021)

File.Name: l528c_l523-006.jpg
Notes: 51208791887 This is either a Syrphid Fly in the species Ceriana tridens in the family Syrphidae or a Thick-headed Fly (Conopidae). A moment after I got this photo, it flew with the wind and I lost it. I already have my two photos for the Fly Day Friday group, but HFDF anyway - if it's the right species. (San Marcos Pass, 23 May 2021)

File.Name: l528d_l522-024.jpg
Notes: 51208775117 I found this Snipe Fly (Rhagio, Rhagionidae, Diptera) on a leaf of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) in the woods today. These handsome flies are not really common here, but I find them now and then in the woods. I already have my two photos for the Fly Day Friday group, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 22 May 2021)

File.Name: l529a_l529-025.jpg
Notes: 51211583036 Here is a brand-new caterpillar of a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly on a partially eaten leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. These tiny caterpillars build what UK photographer Dom Greves describes as a "defensive pier constructed with silk and frass along the leaf mid-rib" - see his photo of a different caterpillar in the same Limenitidinae group that builds a similar structure. I have a photo from two days ago that shows the caterpillar emerging from its egg, this photo. Looking at the amount it's eaten, it has quite an appetite. I look forward to watching this one grow for a while. (San Marcos Pass, 29 May 2021)

File.Name: l529b_l529-020.jpg
Notes: 51210851422 This is a goofy little Chaparral Monkey Grasshopper (Morsea californica) in the Eumastacidae family of the Orthoptera in a plant of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) in the woods today. They are remarkably variable as you can see if you search all my photos for "Morsea californica". They is really tiny. (San Marcos Pass, 29 May 2021)

File.Name: l530a_l530-013.jpg
Notes: 51214767854 Here is Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on new composite flowers of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) in the woods today. They are handsome butterflies, a little gaudy perhaps, and easier than most to photograph. (San Marcos Pass, 30 May 2021)

File.Name: l530b_l529-028.jpg
Notes: 51213267822 Here is a stunning little spider on a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) in the woods. Its legs look like they're made of tinted glass! This one might be Nigma linsdalei in the family Dictynidae of Mesh Web Weavers. It's really tiny but really beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 29 May 2021)

File.Name: l531a_l531-020.jpg
Notes: 51216810798 I found this nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae) on a throat of a flower of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). I believe it is the nymph of a Bush Katydid in the genus Scudderia, likely Scudderia mexicana. (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2021)

File.Name: l531b_l530-007.jpg
Notes: 51217645645 Here is a small Planthopper (Fulgoroidea) on Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the woods in the woods. I believe the insect is a Cixiid Planthopper in the family Cixiidae, possibly genus Cixius. (San Marcos Pass, 30 May 2021)

File.Name: l601a_l601-017.jpg
Notes: 51219046816 This is a California Hairstreak (Satyrium californica) butterfly working new flowers of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae). This is the first one I've seen this year. It is somewhat similar to the Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly in this photo, though that one appears later in the summer when these are all gone. (San Marcos Pass, 1 June 2021)

File.Name: l601b_l601-008.jpg
Notes: 51219242278 Here is a Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) walking across a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 1 June 2021)
CommonNames: Leafhopper Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: l602a_l602-006.jpg
Notes: 51221572558 I believe this is a Green Lacewing (Chrysopidae, Neuroptera) larva on a leaf of Coast Live Oak. (San Marcos Pass, 2 June 2021)

File.Name: l602b_l602-002.jpg
Notes: 51221320441 This is Green Lacewing (Chrysopidae, Neuroptera) egg on a tip of a hairlike stalk that is about 1/2 inch long. This helps to reduce cannibalism of the eggs by sibling larvae. This is on a leaf of White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae). Notice the aphids on the right. The lacewings will eventually eat the aphids. (San Marcos Pass, 2 June 2021)

File.Name: l607a_l607-003.jpg
Notes: 51232828863 Here is a small Harvestman (Opiliones) arachnid on flowers of White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae) growing on the wild edge of our garden today. These harvestmen are built differently from true spiders by having just one body part and only one pair of eyes - BugGuide says they are more closely related to scorpions. They are quite surprising when you aren't expecting them. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2021)

File.Name: l607b_l607-026.jpg
Notes: 51233663380 Here is a tiny Tick (Ixodidae, Arachnida) waiting on the end of a new grass stem. Hah - it's waiting for you or me to walk by so it can fall away and attach itself! Our most common tick is the Western Black-legged Tick (Ixodes pacificus), see this photo, but this one looks slightly different - for one thing, it doesn't have black legs. That's it's the Pacific Coast Tick (Dermacentor occidentalis). It's tiny, about 1/8 inch long. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2021)

File.Name: l608a_l608-001.jpg
Notes: 51235948900 Look close . . . this is not an ant but a remarkable ant mimic! It's a "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) that looks and moves like a real ant, but no ant has a proboscis like that. The bug is Closterocoris amoenus (Miridae), and it's the adult that comes from the ant-mimic nymphs that I showed in this photo last year. It's interesting that the adults don't look anything like ants, just the nymphs. I usually find them on White Sage (Salvia apiana), but I sometimes them find inside the flowers of Southern Bush Monkey Flower. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2021)

File.Name: l608b_l608-006.jpg
Notes: 51235634979 This is "true bug" lounging inside a flower of native Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). The bug is Closterocoris amoenus (Miridae), and it's the adult that comes from the ant-mimic nymphs that I showed in this photo last year. It's interesting that the adults don't look anything like ants, just the nymphs. I usually find them on White Sage (Salvia apiana), but I guess these roomy flowers are another host. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2021)

File.Name: l609a_l609-007.jpg
Notes: 51236312022 I found this sleek little Snakefly (family Raphidiidae, order Raphidioptera) insect. I believe it is genus Agulla with that "kite-shaped" head and female with a long ovipositor on the other end. They have subtle but beautiful colors. (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2021)

File.Name: l609b_l607-005.jpg
Notes: 51237210953 This is White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae) on a drippy morning in our garden. Notice the tiny spider web in between the flowers. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2021)

File.Name: l610a_l603-036.jpg
Notes: 51239854579 I believe this is a Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) getting a drink on the sandstone rock that we keep in a water tub by dry creek. This photo shows the wasp's impressive "petiolate abdomen" for HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 3 June 2021)

File.Name: l610b_l603-020.jpg
Notes: 51239263853 This is a fly (Diptera) it's perch in the water tub by our dry creek. I don't know what kind of fly it is, but it looks bristly enough to be another Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 3 June 2021)

File.Name: l611a_l608-044.jpg
Notes: 51242059340 I believe this is a Thick-headed Fly (family Conopidae) in the order Diptera on California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). This is one of my favorite groups of flies - see this photo. The larger ones like this are remarkable wasp mimics. The small ones don't seem to be mimics, but I can recognize them right off by the combination of long antennae, stout proboscis which is bent, and the curled abdomen. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2021)

File.Name: l611b_l603-005.jpg
Notes: 51242059340 It's is a unusual Small-headed Fly (Acroceridae) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. I believe is genus Turbopsebius. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 3 June 2021)

File.Name: l612a_l612-012.jpg
Notes: 51244049400 I'm pretty sure this is a Rural Skipper (Ochlodes agricola) butterfly new flowers of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. These skippers have little translucent spots near the tips of their forewings which are visible with the light behind wings in my photo. (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2021)

File.Name: l612b_l610-030.jpg
Notes:
51243743539 *Sigh* The things we do for a photo...

This is another biting fly in our mountains drawing blood from my arm. I believe it is a Rocky Mountain Bite Fly in the genus Symphoromyia of the Snipe Fly (Rhagionidae) family, but it's different from the one in this photo taken which is distinctly orange in color. These flies will be annoying for a week or two, then they'll disappear - and the Deer Flies will start to appear. (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2021)

File.Name: l613a_l613-040-.jpg
Notes: 51245555988 Here is a California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly working flowers in the this garden this morning. It's a female, more plain than the males with no "dogface" pattern, and a paler yellow color that has a hint of green. Those pointy forewings are distinctive for both male and female, and distinguish them from other Sulphur butterflies. It's an attractive butterfly, but you have to see a male with its wings open to appreciate why it is the official California State Butterfly - like this one. (San Marcos Pass, 13 June 2021)

File.Name: l613b_l613-007.jpg
Notes: 51246406345 Here is gorgeous Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working new flowers of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae). This is another one that's not so pale - they get a cream color as we move into summer. (San Marcos Pass, 13 June 2021)

File.Name: l614a_l614-010-.jpg
Notes: 51248754850 Here is a caterpillar of a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly on a partially eaten leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. These tiny caterpillars build what UK photographer Dom Greves describes as a "defensive pier constructed with silk and frass along the leaf mid-rib" - see his photo of a different caterpillar in the same Limenitidinae group that builds a similar structure. (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2021)

File.Name: l614b_l612-018-.jpg
Notes: 51246969272 I found this nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae) on a California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). I believe it is the nymph of a Bush Katydid in the genus Scudderia, likely Scudderia mexicana. (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2021)

File.Name: l615a_l615-008.jpg
Notes: 51250669339 Here is a tiny Six-spotted Orb Weaver (Araniella displicata, Araneidae) on its web stretched across a couple of leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. This one seems unusually pale. (San Marcos Pass, 15 June 2021)
CommonNames: Six-spotted Orb Weaver; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: l615b_l611-006.jpg
Notes: 51250924920 Here is a stunning little spider on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. This one might be Nigma linsdalei in the family Dictynidae of Mesh Web Weavers - a male, note the palps. Here is the female for comparison this photo. Are they similar? (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2021)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Mesh Web Weavers

File.Name: l616a_l616-043.jpg
Notes: 51251981076 This is a wild bee on a flower of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae). I believe it is a Resin Bee in the family Megachilidae, probably genus Dianthidium. (San Marcos Pass, 16 June 2021)

File.Name: l616b_l615-006.jpg
Notes: 51252989910 Here is a tiny Six-spotted Orb Weaver (Araniella displicata, Araneidae). Its web is stretched across a couple of leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. I turned the camera to the left so the web would be visible. The spider is actually hanging down from the web. (San Marcos Pass, 15 June 2021)
CommonNames: Six-spotted Orb Weaver; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: l617a_l617-009.jpg
Notes: 51254075161 This is the beautiful golden behind of an interesting fly that's hiding in a flower of native Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). It's a remarkable Metallic Small-Headed Fly in the genus Eulonchus of the uncommon family Acroceridae of the order Diptera - probably Eulonchus smaragdinus. These flies seem made for the flowers - just the right diameter to fit inside and a long proboscis to reach the nectary at the flower's base. These flies move so fast that they are very hard to capture - even just coming out of the flower. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 17 June 2021)
CommonNames: Bush Monkey Flower; Metallic Small-Headed Fly

File.Name: l617b_l611-017.jpg
Notes: 51254057706 This is an odd little creature. I believe it's a nymph of a Tortoise Beetle (Cassidinae on a leaf of non-native American Black Nightshade (Solanum americum aka Solanum nodiflorum, Solanaceae) in the woods. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2021)

File.Name: l618a_l618-025.jpg
Notes: 51255316247 This is a big-headed fly getting a drink on the sandstone rock that we keep in a water tub by the dry creek. I believe this is a Big-headed Fly in the Pipunculidae family. What a strange little fly. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 18 June 2021)

File.Name: l618b_l617-007.jpg
Notes: 51256236738 It's shot of a remarkable Metallic Small-Headed Fly in the genus Eulonchus of the uncommon family Acroceridae of the order Diptera - probably Eulonchus smaragdinus. These flies seem made for Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) - just the right diameter to fit inside and a long proboscis to reach the nectary at the flower's base. These flies move so fast that they are very hard to capture - even just coming out of the flower. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 17 June 2021)

File.Name: l619a_l619-060.jpg
Notes: 51258861679 This is a mighty California Bee Assassin (Apiomerus californicus) - a large Assassin Bug in the family Reduviidae . It's nicely posed on a native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) where it is waiting by the flowers for prey. (San Marcos Pass, 19 June 2021)

File.Name: l619b_l619-024.jpg
Notes: 51258833524 This is a wild bee on a flower of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae). I suspect that the bee is family Megachilidae, maybe genus Megachile. I'm thinking it is a Horn-faced Leafcutter (Megachile fidelis)? These bees collect pollen under their abdomens rather than on their hind legs like most other bees. (San Marcos Pass, 18 June 2021)

File.Name: l620a_l616-032.jpg
Notes:
51261247479 This is another a little "looper caterpillar" of a Geometrid Moth (Geometridae, Lepidoptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. Like all of these caterpillars, it stands on a platform of two pairs of hind prolegs with three pairs of true legs in front, and walks by looping along. Happy Moth Monday! (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2021)

The summer solstice officially happened today on June 20 at 8:31 pm PDT. It's the first day of summer when the sun is highest above the ecliptic and the path of the sun is highest in the sky. Happy solstice!

File.Name: l620b_l616-028.jpg
Notes: 51259746222 This is a remarkable moth on a native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae). I believe it is Rectiostoma fernaldella in the family Twirler Moths (Gelechioidea). Happy Moth Monday! (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2021)

File.Name: l620c_l616-025.jpg
