Notes: 51259736837 This is a remarkable wasp-mimic Clearwing Moth (family Sesiidae, Lepidoptera) on a native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae). Maybe the moth is a Sycamore Borer (Synanthedon resplendens)? There were Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa, Platanaceae) trees nearby. Happy Moth Monday! (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2021)

File.Name: l621a_l621-008.jpg
Notes: 51263881065 This is a wild bee on a flower of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae). I suspect that the bee is family Megachilidae, maybe genus Osmia. (San Marcos Pass, 21 June 2021)

File.Name: l621b_l621-017.jpg
Notes: 51262810996 This is a remarkable Metallic Small-Headed Fly in the genus Eulonchus of the uncommon family Acroceridae of the order Diptera - probably Eulonchus smaragdinus. These flies seem made for Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus longiflorus, Phrymaceae), but this is Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. These flies move so fast that they are very hard to capture - even just coming out of the flower. (San Marcos Pass, 21 June 2021)
CommonNames: Metallic Small-Headed Fly; Woolly Blue Curls

File.Name: l621c_l621-015.jpg
Notes: 51262069812 Here is a Pacific Azure aka Echo Azure (Celastrina echo) butterfly of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae). These butterflies are distinctive in having no distinctive markings below even when fresh. (San Marcos Pass, 21 June 2021)
CommonNames: Pacific Azure; Echo Azure; California Coffeeberry

File.Name: l622a_l622-004.jpg
Notes: 51264965046 Here is "bird turd" caterpillar of a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) on a leaf of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods. These photos show the growth of one caterpiller over 12 days. It's probably the same caterpillar, but I can't be sure. I found the newly molted green caterpillar today. (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2021)

File.Name: l622b_l619-045.jpg
Notes: 51264959446 I'm pretty sure this is a Rural Skipper (Ochlodes agricola) butterfly new flowers of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 19 June 2021)

File.Name: l622c_l620-021a.jpg
Notes: 51265985545 This is another Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on a flower of native Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). (San Marcos Pass, 20 June 2021)

File.Name: l623a_l623-012.jpg
Notes: 51266198977 This is a wild bee on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) hiding under the leaf. Note that it has no pollen sacs on its legs. I thought this is a Cuckoo-leaf-cutter Bee (Coelioxys, Megachilidae), but this it a male Halictus. (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2021)

File.Name: l623b_l623-025.jpg
Notes: 51266198977 Tiny tangled threads under a leaf of native Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the canyon today. It appears to be an egg deposit. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2021)

File.Name: l623c_l621-032.jpg
Notes: 51267666439 Tiny tangled threads under a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the canyon. It appears to be an egg deposit, I guess it is a spider? Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 21 June 2021)

File.Name: l624a_l624-029.jpg
Notes: 51269156523 Here is a fantastic diurnal (daytime) California Clearwing Moth (Hemaris thetis) flying into a flower of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. This is the first one of these moths I've managed to photograph this year, and it came out surprising well. It's always a challenge to capture these moths that fly so fast among the summer flowers and almost never stop. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2021)
CommonNames: California Clearwing Moth; Woolly Blue Curls

File.Name: l624b_l623-022.jpg
Notes: 51269142088 I found this nymph of a "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a stem of Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). I believe it's some kind of Stink Bug in the family Pentatomidae. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2021)

File.Name: l624c_l619-052.jpg
Notes: 51268952191 This is a mighty California Bee Assassin (Apiomerus californicus) - a large Assassin Bug in the family Reduviidae . It's on a native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) where it is waiting by the flowers for prey. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 19 June 2021)

File.Name: l625a_l625-025.jpg
Notes: 51271093928 Here is a small Planthopper (Fulgoroidea) on the the water tub we keep by the summer-dry creek. It looks like a fishing fly! I assume that it's a nymph. It's traveling on the water surface, so I wonder if it fell in the water or is there on purpose. (San Marcos Pass, 25 June 2021)

File.Name: l625b_l622-027.jpg
Notes: 51271929510 This is a little Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) that I always seem to find at this time of year perched on the steel guard rail of the little bridge across our creek. I don't know the proper name of this fly, but I've always called it the "Steel Bridge Fly", see this photo of the same kind of fly on the same bridge rail taken in July 2013. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2021)

File.Name: l625c_l616-008.jpg
Notes: 51271915485 This is a fly (Diptera) in a native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae). I don't know what kind of fly it is, but it looks bristly enough to be another Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 16 June 2021)

File.Name: l626a_l626-026.jpg
Notes: 51273185603 I found this uncommon Fatal Metalmark (Calephelis nemesis) butterfly working California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in our canyon today. These butterflies mind their own business and quietly work different flowers in the woods. They may be more common than they seem to be, but I don't find them very often. (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2021)

File.Name: l626b_l626-037.jpg
Notes: 51272222127 I believe this is an atypical Bee Fly in the family Bombyliidae, probably in the genus Geron. The name sounds like "gerontology" - imagine a humpbacked old man with a cane instead of a proboscis. These are small flowers of native Leafy Fleabane (Erigeron foliosus, Asteraceae). Julie calls it the "Kokopelli Fly" - which is perfect. (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2021)

File.Name: l626c_l626-017.jpg
Notes: 51273989200 I'm pretty sure this is a male scale insect or mealybug (Coccoidea, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera) in the woods today. The long white tails are really just long hairs covered by a waxy exudate. I made a grab for one a few years ago as it flew by, and the white stuff came off in my fingers leaving just the filament behind. I guess the whole insect is covered with the same waxy exudate. If this is a male, I have no idea what the females look like - but they must be out there. (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2021)

File.Name: l627a_l627-005.jpg
Notes: 51275269526 This is a remarkable wasp (Hymenoptera) visiting new flowers of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in our canyon today in the woods today. I believe it's a Carrot Wasp in the family Gasteruptiidae, almost certainly genus Gasteruption. (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2021)

File.Name: l627b_l627-015.jpg
Notes: 51274506327 This is another little Bee Fly (Bombyliidae) on new flowers of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in our canyon today in the woods today that seems to be dusted with gold flecks - a golden bee on golden flowers. I believe this fly is genus Thevenetimyia, maybe the species Thevenetimyia luctifera. (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2021)

File.Name: l627c_l627-008.jpg
Notes: 51274495052 Here a tiny beetle in a native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) plant family. I believe the beetle are tiny Carpet Beetle (Dermestidae, Coleoptera), probably genus Anthrenus. The larvae of these or similar beetles are notorious for cleaning up dead animal parts or that old butterfly collection you forgot about. The adults are rather pretty and visit flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2021)

File.Name: l628a_l628-026.jpg
Notes: 51277760113 These flies are frequently mistaken for wasps . . . for which they are remarkably effective mimics! I believe this is a Syrphid Fly in the species Ceriana tridens in the family Syrphidae. The antennae are remarkable, bound together at the base to form a "Y" shape by a structure called a "frontal prominence" or "antennifer" - see this page at BugGuide. This wasp is visiting native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in our canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2021)

File.Name: l628b_l628-009.jpg
Notes: 51278292149 Here is a another caterpillar of a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly on leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2021)

File.Name: l628c_l628-040.jpg
Notes: 51276808067 This is a remarkable wasp (Hymenoptera) visiting new flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) in the woods today. I believe it's a Carrot Wasp in the family Gasteruptiidae, almost certainly genus Gasteruption. (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2021)

File.Name: l629a_l629-019.jpg
Notes: 51279859966 I'm pretty sure this is a Rural Skipper (Ochlodes agricola) butterfly on new flowers of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2021)

File.Name: l629b_l628-042.jpg
Notes: 51279846601 This is a Flower Longhorn Beetle (Lepturinae, Cerambycidae, Coleoptera) on flower of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) - maybe the species Judolia sexspilota or something similar. These beetles are pretty common right now on many different kinds of flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2021)

File.Name: l629c_l626-030.jpg
Notes: 51280842875 Here are tiny mating beetles in a flower of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). It's almost Wednesday, so Happy Hump Day! (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2021)

File.Name: l630a_l630-011.jpg
Notes: 51281234642 Here are raindrops on a spider web covering a stem of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae) in the woods today. It didn't rain today, but we had a nice wet fog for most of the morning. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2021)

File.Name: l630b_l628-001.jpg
Notes: 51281193172 Here is a spider web is on the window sill in our bathroom, right next to the throne. It looks larger than it is. It's a little Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider, but the spider is only visible when it is in sunlight. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2021)

File.Name: l630c_l628-002.jpg
Notes: 51282947730 This is the "cushion of webbing" where the caterpillar of a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly has been resting on a leaf of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae), see this photo for similar caterpillars on their webbing. It's my impression that the caterpillars make this silk cushion to have something to hang onto when they are resting. I already have my two photos for the Web Wednesday group - but Happy Web Webnesday anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2021)

File.Name: l701a_l701-022.jpg
Notes: 51285086470 This is a new flower of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. I was not expecting this Hummingbird, and count this as a lucky surprise! (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2021)

File.Name: l701b_l628-037.jpg
Notes: 51284770534 Here is cute little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) flying into native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). I believe it is a genus Bombylius because it has the same shape as the Greater Bee Fly (Bombylius major) in this photo, but it's smaller with different colors. I suspect it is Bombylius lancifer or B. facialis - even BugGuide can't decide. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2021)
CommonNames: Bee Fly; California Buckwheat; Greater Bee Fly

File.Name: l701c_l627-036.jpg
Notes: 51284034666 This is a fly (Diptera) getting a drink on the sandstone rock that we keep in a water tub by the dry creek. I don't know what kind of fly it is, but it looks bristly enough to be another Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2021)

File.Name: l702a_l702-029.jpg
Notes: 51286153968 Here is a Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly perched for moment in a shrub of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). They can sit quietly for a long time with their wings closed, looking quite plain, and then open their wings in a quick flash! (San Marcos Pass, 2 July 2021)

File.Name: l702b_l702-023.jpg
Notes: 51286994015 I believe this is a Syrphid Fly in the species Ceriana tridens in the family Syrphidae. The antennae are remarkable, bound together at the base to form a "Y" shape by a structure called a "frontal prominence" or "antennifer" - see this page at BugGuide. This wasp is visiting native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in our canyon. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 2 July 2021)

File.Name: l702c_l627-038.jpg
Notes: 51286682579 This is a large Black Soldier Fly (Hermetia illucens, Stratiomyidae) in a water tub by the dry creek. It's maybe 3/4 inch long. These flies have antennae like an old-fashioned TV set and wonderful patterned eyes. I think of them as "compost flies" - see this photo of a maggot in our compost bin. They might actually do some good in the compost bin, see the article at Wikipedia, though it's interesting that I actually found this one some distance away from anyone's compost. I've seen them near here before, see this photo. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2021)

File.Name: l713a_l713-018.jpg
Notes: 51311050315 This is a remarkable Bembecine Sand Wasp (genus Steniolia, Bembicini, Hymenoptera) working a new flower of Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum, Lamiaceae). These wasps seem to show up wherever there are summer flowers. They are not uncommon, but they are so fast and alert that it's hard to get decent photos of them. (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2021)
CommonNames: Bembecine Sand Wasp; Woolly Blue Curls

File.Name: l713b_l713-013.jpg
Notes: 51310731759 Here is a mating beetles (Coleoptera) with with red epaulettes on a leaf of native Chaparral Pea (Pickeringia montana, Fabaceae). I'm pretty sure sure it's I guess it is a male Long-horned Bee in the genus Melissodes or Eucera with those long antennae. The males do have hairy legs, but the females have even hairier legs with proper pollen baskets (scopae) designed to carry pollen. (San Marcos Pass, 2 July 2021)

File.Name: l714a_l714-026.jpg
Notes: 51312850074 Here are caterpillars of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly in well-named but non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii, Scrophulariaceae or Buddlejaceae) in the garden today. These caterpillars are web-spinners, and they eat the leaves as they cover them with their shroud-like webbing. They will feed into the summer, and then they form tough skins and drop to the forest floor to over-winter. Finally they emerge, eat and get fat, and pupate into adults next year. Tents now mean butterflies next year. The butterflies have been pretty scarce so far this year, and I reckon the caterpillars are precious to keep the population going next year. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2021)

File.Name: l714b_l714-017.jpg
Notes: 51312308543 I found this interesting spider webbing on a stem of Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) in the woods today. I believe the frizzy webbing is the work of a cribellate species of Mesh Web Weaver in the family Dictynidae. The webbing is comprised of very fine threads that are spun into strands like hand-spun wool or chenille yarn, different from ordinary spider webbing. I have photos of similar webbing here, here, and here. There's more info at Wikipedia and a nice article in The Atlantic. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2021)

File.Name: l714c_l714-024.jpg
Notes: 51312078486 Here is a tiny spider on a leaf of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) in the woods today. I believe this is a Mesh Web Weaver spider in the family Dictynidae, compare this photo from two days ago. (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2021)

File.Name: l715a_l715-003.jpg
Notes: 51314355158 Here is small Robber Fly (family Asilidae, Diptera) on a leaflet of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae). I already have my two photos for the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday group - but HBBBT anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 15 July 2021)

File.Name: l715b_l710-045.jpg
Notes: 51314861984 I believe this is a Potter Wasp aka Mason Wasp (Eumeninae, Vespidea) - compare this photo of a different wasp with its neat mud nest. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2021)

Notes: 51314327038 This is a pretty little beetle (Coleoptera) sticking its butt up in the air while it feeds from a flower of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae). I'm pretty sure this critter is a Soft-winged Flower Beetle (Melyridae), maybe genus Collops and species Collops quadriguttatus. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2021)

I already have my two photos for the Fly Day Friday group, but HFDF anyway. (San Marcos Pass, 15 July 2021)

Notes: 51316101646 Here is a small Planthopper (Fulgoroidea) on the the water tub we keep by the summer-dry creek. I believe this is just another Flesh Fly (Sarcophagidae) in the woods. Is it capturing prey? HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2021).

Notes: 51322057027 These is a small white berry of native Climbing Bedstraw (Galium porrigens) in the Rubiaceae plant family. Galium is an interesting genus partly because some native species have berries, while others have dry nutlets. I have no idea if the berries are edible. They're too small to matter much, and there's something unappetizing about white berries. They disappear from the plants pretty quickly, so something out there likes them! (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2021)

Notes: 51326259082 Here is a web made by caterpillars of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly in the well-named but non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii, Scrophulariaceae or Buddlejaceae) in the garden today. These caterpillars are web-spinners, and they eat the leaves as they cover them with their shroud-like webbing. The caterpillars that created this web have moved on. Happy Web Webnesday! File.Name: l721c_l720-024.jpg
Notes: 51327970000 This is the bowl-shaped web of a Sheet Web Weaver spider in the family Linyphiidae, a Bowl and Doily Spider (Frontinella communis aka Frontinella pyramitela) in this case. It consists of three main parts. The main part is "the bowl" of non-sticky webbing. The spider hangs below this and grabs its prey from underneath. Below this is "the doily" which seems to be like an oil pan to catch falling prey. Above the bowl is random webbing that supports the bowl and might knock down flying insects into it. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2021)
CommonNames: Sheet Web Weaver; Bowl and Doily Spider

It's a grand spider, larger than most jumpers. (San Marcos Pass, 22 July 2021)

I believe the bees are subgenus Pygoperdita of the genus Perdita of the Andrenidae - maybe Perdita californica or P. calochorti. (San Marcos Pass, 23 July 2021).

These bees collect pollen under their abdomens rather than on their hind legs like most other bees. (San Marcos Pass, 23 July 2021)

My first thought was California Angle-winged Katydid (Microcentrum californicum) or the Greater Angle-wing Katydid (Microcentrum rhombifolium) - this photo. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 28 July 2021)

Notes: 51348902543 Yipes! This is a real Western Bloodsucking Conenose Bug (Triatoma protracta) in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera. It's a kind of kissing bug - so called because they feed on blood during the night and prefer to bite humans around the mouth or eyes. I moved it to a white plate outside. This is one insect I don't want to find in our house! (San Marcos Pass, 31 July 2021)
CommonNames: Western Bloodsucking Conenose Bug; kissing bug

Notes: 51353687301 Here is a handsome Lorquin's Admiral (Basilarchia lorquini aka Limenitis lorquini) butterfly perched on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) by the creek. Usually they are in the willows nearby, but this one was chasing a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly, and it settled here for a moment. Hah, the Sisters seem to taunt the Admirals! These butterflies don't seem to visit flowers. They just sit and wait - to chase away any other butterfly that comes close to their willow tree! (San Marcos Pass, 2 August 2021)

They look much alike, but the orange wing-tips of the Admiral goes right to the edge of its forewings without a dark border like the Sister has. (San Marcos Pass, 2 August 2021)

File.Name: l809b_l806-017.jpg
Notes: 51368684753 I found this impressive wasp (Hymenoptera) on in the water tub we keep by the summer-dry creek. I believe it's an Ichneumon Wasp in the family Ichneumonidae - superfamily Ichneumonoidea for sure. These are parasitic wasps that lay their eggs on the larvae of other insects, see this photo for an example. That's pretty gruesome, but it's essential to preserving any kind of balance in nature. (San Marcos Pass, 6 August 2021)

File.Name: l809c_l730-031.jpg

File.Name: l810a_l810-008.jpg
Notes: 51370532011 On the left is a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) and on the right is a Lorquin's Admiral (Limenitis lorquini). They look much alike, but the orange wing-tips of the Admiral goes right to the edge of its forewings without a dark border like the Sister has. They are similar but different, and I got both photos today. (San Marcos Pass, 10 August 2021)

File.Name: l810b_l810-012.jpg
Notes: 51370522616 I'm pretty sure this it's the egg of a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly. I was put on the right track by recent work by Dom Greves showing White Admiral butterfly eggs in the UK, see this photo - Admirals and Sisters are both in the same Limenitidinae subfamily, and both have eggs like tiny geodesic domes. (San Marcos Pass, 10 August 2021)

Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 7 August 2021)

Notes: 51375576990 I believe this photo shows a Potter Wasp (Eumeninae, Vespidea) on the mint flowers in the garden. The "double abdomen" of these wasps is distinctive. Maybe it's genus Eumenes? They make neat little mud nests mostly on plant stems. A couple of years ago, one of them made a nest on the antennae of my truck - see this photo. I haven't found any nests yet this year, but the wasps are out there. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 8 August 2021)

File.Name: l813c_l808-013jpg.jpg
Notes: 51375813092 This is an interesting little Syrphid Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) on new flowers in the mint patch of the garden. I believe the fly is genus Syritta in the subfamily Eristalinae, probably species Syritta pipiens. (The genus Tropidia looks somewhat similar.) I seem to see these little flies every year. HFDF! File.Name: l817a_l817-026.jpg
Notes: 51385545168 Here is a California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly working flowers in the this garden this morning. It's a female, more plain than the males with no "dogface" pattern, and a paler yellow color that has a hint of green. Those pointy forewings are distinctive for both male and female, and distinguish them from other Sulphur butterflies. It's an attractive butterfly, but you have to see a male with its wings open to appreciate why it is the official California State Butterfly - like this one. (San Marcos Pass, 17 August 2021)

Notes: 51394533685 Here are raindrops on the tiny "glochid" spines of a Prickly Pear (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus growing in our garden. These cacti have regular large spines that are easy to avoid, but these tiny ones are the ones that devil me. They get in and you need tweezers to get them out. It was cool and drizzly. (San Marcos Pass, 21 August 2021)

File.Name: l822a_l822-049.jpg
Notes: 51397155615 Here are two shots of a nymph of a different Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family on a leaf of native Branching Phacelia (Phacelia ramosissima var. latifolia, Boraginaceae) in the woods today. I believe this is the species Pselliopus spinicollis in the subfamily Harpactorinae. The spines and striped legs are remarkable. This little predator is quite small, less than 1/2 inch long. I don't find them very often, and I've never found a nymph before. (San Marcos Pass, 22 August 2021)

File.Name: l822b_l822-005.jpg
Notes: 51396132621 This is another furry Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) on non-native cactus in the garden. I believe it is genus Villa in the tribe Villini. (San Marcos Pass, 22 August 2021)

File.Name: l822c_l822-039.jpg
Notes: 51395357672 Here is a West Coast Lady (Vanessa annabella) butterfly working new flowers of non-native Lantana (Verbenaceae) in the garden in the garden today. It's the smallest of our Lady butterflies and the least common - at least up here in the mountains. The distinguishing characteristics are the squared-off forewing tips and the first orange bar in the middle of the front edge of the upper wing which is white in other Ladies. I think size and the shape of the wings are usually enough to tell that it's different. (San Marcos Pass, 2 September 2021) Yep, they have remarkable goggle-like eyes - but what gives with that amazing "third eye" between its two real eyes - the mark of the Illuminati?? Yep, it's real, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 22 August 2021)

File.Name: l823a_l823-004.jpg
Notes: 51399517050 Look close! This a Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) on a ripening fruit of a prickly pear (Opuntia) cactus in the garden this morning. Pretty good camouflage! This is a mature male cricket. The females have narrow wings, while the males have broad wings that they rub together to make their song - see this photo where I caught one in the act. Only the males sing, and they're just starting their evening chorus this year. (San Marcos Pass, 23 August 2021)

File.Name: l823b_l823-005.jpg
Notes: 51398716458 I found this nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae) on a non-native plant in the garden today. Did you notice that this nymph is missing a hind leg? That must be pretty important for these little hoppers. I wonder if they can regenerate that missing leg on a future molt?? I believe it is the nymph of a Bush Katydid in the genus Scudderia, likely Scudderia mexicana - see this photo, but they change as they mature. (San Marcos Pass, 23 August 2021)

File.Name: l823c_l823-001.jpg
Notes: 51399182009 Here is another little nymph of a Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) of a prickly pear (Opuntia) cactus in the garden this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 23 August 2021)

Notes: 51403459169 I found this a Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) walking across our outdoor table. (San Marcos Pass, 25 August 2021)

File.Name: l825b_l823-010.jpg
Notes: 51403429864 I found this interesting spider under a loose piece of oak bark in our woodpile this morning. It was tending it's nest, but then it I disturbed it. It might be a Stealthy Ground Spiders (Gnaphosidae) with elongate lateral spinnerets, maybe the Western Parson Spider (Herpyllus propinquus? Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 23 August 2021)

File.Name: l825c_l820-009.jpg
Notes: 51403412704 I found this interesting spider webbing on a stem of a prickly pear (Opuntia) cactus in the garden. I believe the frizzy webbing is the work of a cribellate species of Mesh Web Weaver in the family Dictynidae, maybe genus Dictyna itself. The webbing is comprised of very fine threads that are spun into strands like hand-spun wool or chenille yarn, different from ordinary spider webbing. I have photos of similar webbing here, here, and here. There's more info at Wikipedia and a nice article in The Atlantic. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 20 August 2021)

File.Name: l902a_l831-015.jpg
Notes: 51419615902 This is a Stiletto Fly in the family Therevidae on a prickly pear (Opuntia) cactus, see this photo. Maybe it's the species Thereva fucata, see this BugGuide info page. I've seen these handsome flies before, but not often. (San Marcos Pass, 31 August 2021) I will be away for a few days, so I'm posting my Fly Day Friday and Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday photos today. HFDF and HBBBT!

File.Name: l902b_l902-005.jpg
Notes: 51421338535 This a another male Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) on a ripening fruit of a prickly pear (Opuntia) cactus in the garden this morning. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 2 September 2021)

File.Name: l902c_l831-029.jpg
Notes: 51421106929 This is tiny wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) on a non-native Mint plant in the garden. Maybe it's the genus Perdita of the Andrenidae - it's small enough. (San Marcos Pass, 31 August 2021)

File.Name: l905a_l905-009.jpg
Notes: 51428708074 Here is a California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly working flowers in the this garden this morning. It's a female, more plain than the males with no "dogface" pattern, and a paler yellow color that has a hint of green. Those pointy forewings are distinctive for both male and female, and distinguish them from other Sulphur butterflies. It's an attractive butterfly, but you have to see a male with its wings open to appreciate why it is the official California State Butterfly - like this one. (San Marcos Pass, 5 September 2021)

File.Name: l905b_l905-016.jpg
Notes: 51428694679 This is a another Bembecine Sand Wasp (genus Steniolia, Bembicini, Hymenoptera) visiting a of non-native Lantana (Verbenaceae) plant in the garden this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 5 September 2021)

File.Name: l905c_l902-006.jpg
Notes: 51428672559 Here is a West Coast Lady (Vanessa annabella) butterfly working new flowers of non-native Lantana (Verbenaceae) in the garden in the garden today. It's the smallest of our Lady butterflies and the least common - at least up here in the mountains. The distinguishing characteristics are the squared-off forewing tips and the first orange bar in the middle of the front edge of the upper wing which is white in other Ladies. I think size and the shape of the wings are usually enough to tell that it's different. (San Marcos Pass, 2 September 2021)

File.Name: l906a_l906-009.jpg
Notes: 51432143339 This is another tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on a non-native Basil (Ocimum basilicum, Lamiaceae) plant in the garden today. It's a male, as evidenced by its enlarged palps. (San Marcos Pass, 6 September 2021)

File.Name: l906b_l906-010.jpg
Notes: 51432357615 This is the nymph of a Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) on a non-native Basil (Ocimum basilicum, Lamiaceae) plant in the garden today. (San Marcos Pass, 6 September 2021)

File.Name: l906c_l901-019.jpg
Notes: 51430618007 This is the nymph of a Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) on a non-native Mint plant in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 2 September 2021)

File.Name: l907a_l907-053.jpg
Notes: 51433973172 I found another nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae) on a non-native Mint plant in the garden this morning. I believe it is the nymph of a Bush Katydid in the genus Scudderia, likely Scudderia mexicana. (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2021)

File.Name: l907b_l907-040.jpg
Notes: 51434708446 Here is a Woodland Skipper (Ochlodes sylvanoides) butterfly on a flower of a non-native Aster (genus Aster, Asteraceae) flower growing in the garden today. (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2021)

File.Name: l907c_l907-016.jpg
Notes: 51435433649 This is an Umber Skipper (Poanes melane) butterfly working non-native Lantana (Verbenaceae) flowers in the garden this morning. I believe these butterflies have two broods - a spring brood early in the year and a summer brood just starting now. (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2021)

File.Name: l908a_l908-009.jpg
Notes: 51438589894 This a female Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) on a prickly pear (Opuntia) cactus in the garden this morning. The females have narrow wings, compare this this photo of a male. This is the first female that I've seen the seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 8 September 2021)

File.Name: l908b_l906-026.jpg
Notes: 51438555329 Here is a Yellowjacket wasp (genus Vespula) caught in a spider's web. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 6 September 2021)

File.Name: l908c_l905-003.jpg
Notes: 51438035743 This is another shot of odd Feather-legged Spider (Uloborus diversus) in the family Uloboridae of Cribellate Orb Weavers aka Hackled Band Orb Weavers. These spiders make a proper orb web that is interesting because it is usually horizontal. The spider is only a little more than 1/8 inch across - easy not to notice. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 5 September 2021)

File.Name: l909a_l909-009.jpg
Notes: 51441763544 Here is a fairly large Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on spent flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) in the woods today. The spider is mostly hairless, which separates it from the more common Mecaphesa crab spiders in our woods. This one might a White-banded Crab Spider (Misumenoides formosipes) with a ridge between its chelicerae and anterior eyes, but the Goldenrod Crab Spider (Misumena vatia) is similar, and I may have them mixed up. (San Marcos Pass, 9 September 2021)
CommonNames: California Buckwheat; White-banded Crab Spider; Goldenrod Crab Spider

File.Name: l909b_l906-022.jpg
Notes: 51441746974 This is the nymph of a Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) on a non-native Mint plant in the garden. It has its front legs up in the air in a defensive pose - like in this photo and this photo. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 6 September 2021)

File.Name: l909c_l902-015.jpg
Notes: 51441239448 Here is a Green Bottle Fly on a non-native Mint plant in the garden - or is it a Blue Bottle Fly? It's hard to pin down green or blue colors with these iridescent flies. Maybe it's genus Lucilia, and it's definitely a Blow Fly in the family Calliphoridae. The colors are attractive, but this is one those flies you don't want to see walking across your potato salad. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 2 September 2021)
CommonNames: Green Bottle Fly; Mint; Blue Bottle Fly; Blow Fly

File.Name: l911c_l911-024.jpg

File.Name: l912a_l912-005.jpg
Notes: 51459630696 I believe this is a caterpillar of a Fatal Metalmark (Calephelis nemesis) butterfly on a leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae). I've never seen one of these before. (San Marcos Pass, 12 September 2021)

File.Name: l912b_l912-023.jpg
Notes: 51460270719 I found this tiny Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) on an open flower of native Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 12 September 2021)

File.Name: l912c_l911-022.jpg
Notes: 51459719808 This is a remarkable male Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) that's starting for flower. The spider is only about 1/10 inch long, but those front legs are much larger - 1/2 inch or more! Compare this photo of a female - there is no comparison. This is real sexual dimorphism - meaning that males and females look totally different. See this photo for another example with crab spiders. (San Marcos Pass, 11 September 2021)

File.Name: l913a_l913-020.jpg
Notes: 51468514297 Here is a Woodland Skipper (Ochlodes sylvanoides) butterfly on new autumn flower of Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae). (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2021)

File.Name: l913b_l913-015.jpg
Notes: 51468432442 Here is a tiny wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) visiting a new autumn flower of Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae) in the woods this morning. I'm pretty sure this is a Masked Bee aka Yellow-faced Bee in the genus Hylaeus. This tiny bees lack pollen-collecting hairs but instead carry pollen and nectar in their gut and regurgitate it when they get back to their nests. These tiny bees are starting to become common in the woods - at least, I'm noticing them more lately. (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2021)
CommonNames: Sawtooth Goldenbush; Masked Bee; Yellow-faced Bee

File.Name: l913c_l913-011.jpg
Notes: 51469386478 I found this "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a branch of native Chaparral Pea (Pickeringia montana, Fabaceae) in the woods today. I believe it's some kind of Stink Bug in the family Pentatomidae - the prefix "penta" means five, and you can see its five-segmented antennae in my photo. There are many different kinds, so 'nuff said. (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2021)

File.Name: l914a_l914-004.jpg
Notes: 51476930368 This is a cute little net-winged Planthopper (Fulgoroidea) on a branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I believe it is genus Neaethus in the family Tropiduchidae. (San Marcos Pass, 14 September 2021)

File.Name: l914b_l913-031.jpg
Notes: 51477583460 Here is a tiny wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) visiting a new autumn flower on a native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae). I believe these are Small Carpenter Bees in the genus Ceratina - not very hairy but its does have pollen-collecting hairs on it's hind legs. (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2021)

File.Name: l914c_l913-028.jpg
Notes: 51476838758 This is the tunnel of a tiny leaf-miner on a leaf of native Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae). I suppose it's a leaf-miner caterpillar, but compare this one and this one - it appears to have many branches. (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2021)

Notes: 51491599370 Here is a Grass-Skipper (Hesperiinae, Hesperiidae) butterfly hiding under a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. Perhaps it is a an Umber Skipper (Poanes melane) butterfly? (San Marcos Pass, 18 September 2021)

File.Name: l918c_l918-007.jpg
Notes: 51490846298 Here is an interesting fly (Diptera) on a flower of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) that's starting for flower. I believe the fly in an atypical Bee Fly (Bombyliidae) in the genus Neacreotrichus, compare the images at BugGuide. It's an attractive fly with a remarkably long curved proboscis. There's also an "looper caterpillar" of a Geometrid Moth (Geometridae, Lepidoptera) in the background. (San Marcos Pass, 18 September 2021)
CommonNames: Coyote Bush; Bee Fly; Geometrid Moth

Notes: 51494058106 Here is an interesting fly (Diptera) on a flower of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) that is taken today. I believe the fly in an atypical Bee Fly (Bombyliidae) in the genus Neacreotrichus, compare the images at BugGuide. It's an attractive fly with a remarkably long curved proboscis. File.Name: l919b_l919-022.jpg
Notes: 51494252858 This is an another female Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) on native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) this morning. The females have narrow wings, compare my previous photo. (San Marcos Pass, 19 September 2021)

File.Name: l919c_l919-017.jpg
Notes: 51494924645 This a another male Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) hiding on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. The females have narrow wings, while the males have broad wings that they rub together to make their song - see this photo where I caught one in the act. Only the males sing. (San Marcos Pass, 19 September 2021)

File.Name: l920a_l920-022.jpg
Notes: 51498519299 I found this tiny Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) among the flowers of native Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2021)

File.Name: l920b_l920-014.jpg
Notes: 51496987332 This is another Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on a new almost-autumn flower on a native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I believe the spider is in the genus Mecaphesa with hairy legs and abdomen, a female with slender palps. (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2021)

File.Name: l920c_l920-007.jpg
Notes: 51497759136 Here is tiny gall of a Pumpkin Gall Wasp (Dryocosmus minusculus) on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. It does look a bit like a tiny pumpkin or gourd. Small as it is, this wasp-induced gall is home to the tiny wasp larva that lives inside. Look at that neat perfect exit hole where the tiny wasp chewed its way out! Note how this gall causes a color change in the leaf surface.
CommonNames: Pumpkin Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: l921a_l921-021.jpg
Notes: 51501526778 This is a Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) with prey on a flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) shrub in the woods today. It's prey is a tiny wasp (Hymenoptera), just captured. They are named for the tiny size of their prey, not for any personal resemblance. (San Marcos Pass, 21 September 2021)
CommonNames: Leafhopper Assassin Bug; Coyote Bush; wasp

File.Name: l922a_l922-003.jpg
Notes:
51504841673 This is another Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on a new autumn flowers on a native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I believe the spider is in the genus Mecaphesa with hairy legs and abdomen, a female with slender palps. (San Marcos Pass, 22 September 2021)

Today is the Autumnal Equinox - happy equinox! We're starting to lose morning sunlight in the garden at home on the north-facing side of the canyon, but it's interesting that we're actually getting more sunlight under the trees on the other side of the canyon - the sun is lower, so it lets sunlight in under the leaves. Window eaves work the same way.

File.Name: l922b_l921-031.jpg
Notes: 51504517436 This is a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider sitting in the tunnel of the funnel-opening of her web in the canyon. This is the same spider with a fly, see this this photo. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 21 September 2021)

File.Name: l922c_l916-020.jpg
Notes: 51503658567 This is spider webbing strung between between leaves of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae) in the woods. I believe this is the work of a Mesh Web Weaver spider in the family Dictynidae. The webbing joins the leaves like rigging on a dusty ship model. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2021)
